	{font-family:Chicago;
	panose-1:2 11 6 4 2 2 2 2 2 4;}
@font-face
	{font-family:"\@PMingLiU";
	panose-1:2 1 6 1 0 1 1 1 1 1;}
@font-face
	{font-family:"AR CENA";
	panose-1:2 11 6 4 2 2 2 2 2 4;}
@font-face
	{font-family:"Proxima Nova";
	panose-1:2 11 6 4 2 2 2 2 2 4;}
@font-face
	{font-family:SuperGreek;
	panose-1:2 11 6 4 2 2 2 2 2 4;}
 /* Style Definitions */
 .word-content p.MsoNormal, .word-content li.MsoNormal, .word-content div.MsoNormal
	{margin-top:0in;
	margin-right:0in;
	margin-bottom:10.0pt;
	margin-left:0in;
	line-height:115%;
	font-size:11.0pt;
	font-family:"Calibri",sans-serif;}
p.MsoCommentText, li.MsoCommentText, div.MsoCommentText
	{mso-style-link:"Comment Text Char";
	margin-top:0in;
	margin-right:0in;
	margin-bottom:10.0pt;
	margin-left:0in;
	font-size:10.0pt;
	font-family:"Calibri",sans-serif;}
p.MsoHeader, li.MsoHeader, div.MsoHeader
	{mso-style-link:"Header Char";
	margin:0in;
	font-size:11.0pt;
	font-family:"Calibri",sans-serif;}
p.MsoFooter, li.MsoFooter, div.MsoFooter
	{mso-style-link:"Footer Char";
	margin:0in;
	font-size:11.0pt;
	font-family:"Calibri",sans-serif;}
p.MsoListBullet, li.MsoListBullet, div.MsoListBullet
	{margin-top:0in;

	font-size:11.0pt;
	font-family:"Calibri",sans-serif;}
p.MsoListBulletCxSpFirst, li.MsoListBulletCxSpFirst, div.MsoListBulletCxSpFirst
	{margin-top:0in;
	margin-right:0in;
	margin-bottom:0in;
	margin-left:.25in;
	text-indent:-.25in;
	line-height:115%;
	font-size:11.0pt;
	font-family:"Calibri",sans-serif;}
p.MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle, li.MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle, div.MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle
	{margin-top:0in;
	margin-right:0in;
	margin-bottom:0in;
	margin-left:.25in;
	text-indent:-.25in;
	line-height:115%;
	font-size:11.0pt;
	font-family:"Calibri",sans-serif;}
p.MsoListBulletCxSpLast, li.MsoListBulletCxSpLast, div.MsoListBulletCxSpLast
	{margin-top:0in;
	margin-right:0in;
	margin-bottom:10.0pt;
	margin-left:.25in;
	text-indent:-.25in;
	line-height:115%;
	font-size:11.0pt;
	font-family:"Calibri",sans-serif;}
p.MsoCommentSubject, li.MsoCommentSubject, div.MsoCommentSubject
	{mso-style-link:"Comment Subject Char";
	margin-top:0in;
	margin-right:0in;
	margin-bottom:10.0pt;
	margin-left:0in;
	font-size:10.0pt;
	font-family:"Calibri",sans-serif;
	font-weight:bold;}
p.MsoAcetate, li.MsoAcetate, div.MsoAcetate
	{mso-style-link:"Balloon Text Char";
	margin:0in;
	font-size:8.0pt;
	font-family:"Tahoma",sans-serif;}
p.MsoListParagraph, li.MsoListParagraph, div.MsoListParagraph
	{margin-top:0in;
	margin-right:0in;
	margin-bottom:10.0pt;
	margin-left:.5in;
	line-height:115%;
	font-size:11.0pt;
	font-family:"Calibri",sans-serif;}
p.MsoListParagraphCxSpFirst, li.MsoListParagraphCxSpFirst, div.MsoListParagraphCxSpFirst
	{margin-top:0in;
	margin-right:0in;
	margin-bottom:0in;
	margin-left:.5in;
	line-height:115%;
	font-size:11.0pt;
	font-family:"Calibri",sans-serif;}
p.MsoListParagraphCxSpMiddle, li.MsoListParagraphCxSpMiddle, div.MsoListParagraphCxSpMiddle
	{margin-top:0in;
	margin-right:0in;
	margin-bottom:0in;
	margin-left:.5in;
	line-height:115%;

	font-family:"Calibri",sans-serif;}
p.MsoListParagraphCxSpLast, li.MsoListParagraphCxSpLast, div.MsoListParagraphCxSpLast
	{margin-top:0in;
	margin-right:0in;
	margin-bottom:10.0pt;

p.MsoQuote, li.MsoQuote, div.MsoQuote
	{mso-style-link:"Quote Char";
	margin-top:0in;
	margin-right:0in;

	line-height:115%;

	color:black;
	font-style:italic;}
span.HeaderChar
	{mso-style-name:"Header Char";
	mso-style-link:Header;}
span.FooterChar
	{mso-style-name:"Footer Char";
	mso-style-link:Footer;}
span.BalloonTextChar
	{mso-style-name:"Balloon Text Char";
	mso-style-link:"Balloon Text";
	font-family:"Tahoma",sans-serif;}
span.QuoteChar
	{mso-style-name:"Quote Char";
	mso-style-link:Quote;
	color:black;
	font-style:italic;}
span.CommentTextChar
	{mso-style-name:"Comment Text Char";
	mso-style-link:"Comment Text";}
span.CommentSubjectChar
	{mso-style-name:"Comment Subject Char";
	mso-style-link:"Comment Subject";
	font-weight:bold;}
span.msoIns
	{mso-style-name:"";
	text-decoration:underline;
	color:teal;}
span.msoDel
	{mso-style-name:"";
	text-decoration:line-through;
	color:red;}
.MsoChpDefault
	{font-size:11.0pt;
	font-family:"Calibri",sans-serif;}
.MsoPapDefault
	{margin-bottom:10.0pt;
	line-height:115%;}
 /* Page Definitions */
 
div.WordSection1
	{page:WordSection1;}
 /* List Definitions */
 ol
	{margin-bottom:0in;}
ul
	{margin-bottom:0in;}
</style>

<div class="word-content">
<p class=MsoNormal align=center style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:center'><b><span style='font-size:
14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>OLD TESTAMENT TRANSLATION
NOTES</span></b></p>

<p class=MsoNormal align=center style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:center'><b><i><span
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>The
Conversational Version</span></i></b></p>

<p class=MsoNormal align=center style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:center'><i><span style='font-size:
14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></i></p>

<p class=MsoNormal align=center style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:center'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Virgil Warren, PhD</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>     </span></b></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:13.5pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>The
Conversational Version</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> of the Old Testament combines two goals:
(1) to express biblical material the way people speak conversationally in twenty-first-century
American English; (2) to apply to the text the principles a professor of
composition would use to evaluate expository writing in that style. The goals supplement
and limit each other. The alterations from the wording in the original text
intend not to add to, subtract from, or change the semantic content—the
referents themselves. Word pictures for the same referent can differ across
languages without interfering with semantic equivalency; hence, translation
endeavors lose “accuracy” in smaller points like imagery/word picture,
emphasis, and attitude to make it sound natural to today’s reader.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:13.5pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>The</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> <i>Conversational
Version</i> endeavors to make its contribution at the second level, the
custom-of-usage level. The first level reproduces the source language’s terms
and syntax as closely as reasonable—“literal” translation. But languages differ
considerably in ways noted below, and each language changes in its own usage customs
over time. <i>CV </i>aims particularly to update English custom of usage while
being faithful to authorial intent without going so far as to “tell it in our
own words,” as in loose paraphrase. This “dynamic equivalence” differs from paraphrase
by trying to work item-for-item from literal wording to resulting expression. It
avoids recasting the text whole cloth. So translation styles range from exact
word-for-word on a continuum to general retelling. These “Translator’s Notes” help
specify the “mechanics” <i>CV</i> adopts.</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBullet style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:13.5pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Besides
the literal-periphrastic continuum, there is the translation-interpretation
variable. The ideal calls for rendering the text in a way that leaves
interpretation up to the reader, at least in “important” cases. Should
clarifications appear in the text or in marginal comments? One alternative puts
the literal in the text and footnotes the translator’s take on its meaning. The
other alternative puts the clarified wording in the text with marginal notation
that gives the “literal” wording. An example, Deuteronomy </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>23</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:18
in CV, </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>“Don’t take the hire of a female or male prostitute*
to the L</span></i><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>’s house for an offering
to fulfill a vow.” </span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>For “male prostitute,”<i> </i>the original
reads <i>“dog.”</i> The adopted wording understands <i>“dog”</i> as a derogatory
term for a male prostitute, based perhaps on the image of a “horny” old dog. A
typical reader might not make that connection; so <i>CV</i> “clarifies” in the
translation itself to avoid distraction over uncertainty. Covering the mouth was
a gesture with various nuances that a modern reader would have to surmise; so
we have sometimes substituted other wording or expanded the wording with an
explanatory phrase: Job <b>21</b>:5; Proverbs <b>30</b>:22; Ezekiel <b>24</b>:17;
Micah <b>3</b>:7. See also Ezekiel <b>16</b>:63). Legal proceedings took place
in the city gate, but modern readers would not necessarily suppose that. So, CV
has substituted the expression “in court”: Deuteronomy <b>21</b>:19; Ruth <b>4</b>:11;
Amos <b>5</b>:10, 12. The quandary shows that translation involves interpretation.
Consistency between the two alternatives is difficult to achieve because individual
cases are not equally unclear or important.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Summary
of Symbols in <i>CV</i>/<i>OT</i></span></b></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></i></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      words     original
authors’ words translated; or </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                           translator’s
added words in italicized, quoted material</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            word<sup>m
</sup>    replaces the word <i>man</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>word<sup>b
</sup>     replaces the word <i>brother</i> as an affectionate term</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <i>words</i>     dialog
and quotations by the original authors; or </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                           translator’s
added words for clarification in non-dialog material; or</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                           the
alternate name or spelling of it that is used elsewhere for the unitalicized
name</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <u>words</u>     translator’s
addition for highlighting lists and for proper stress in reading</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:81.0pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:-81.0pt'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            [words]   translator’s
occasional explanatory notation within the text</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:81.0pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:-45.0pt'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>[<sup>x</sup>]<sup>repeat
</sup>or [ ]<sup>    </sup><i>CV</i> omits a few standard English verses as
repetitive or not necessary:  </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:81.0pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:-81.0pt'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            (words)   original
author’s comment parenthesized in this version</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <i>[words]</i>  original
authors’ comments inside quoted material </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:81.0pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:-81.0pt'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <sup>+                    </sup>complete
list </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>*             major
point, note explanatory notation</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><sup><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>fem</span></sup><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>           feminine
grammatical gender</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><sup><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>mas</span></sup><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>           masculine
grammatical gender</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><sup><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>sg</span></sup><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>             singular
in the original</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><sup><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>pl</span></sup><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>             plural
in the original</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>LXX       Septuagint
reading (ancient Greek translation of the OT)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>MT         the
standardized Hebrew text</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:81.0pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:-45.0pt'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>/              used
between variants of original names, standard type being the form of the name in
this instance and italics for the form of the name found elsewhere;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:81.0pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:-45.0pt'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>               used
also for words added for clarification</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><sup><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>f 
</span></sup><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>             imperfect
(= future) tense; used where the imperfect tense has an imperatival force</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.5pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-4.5pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Misc.
abbreviations: <i>BDB</i> (<i>A Hebrew and English Lexicon of the Old Testament
</i>by Brown, Driver, and Briggs), <i>CV</i> (<i>The Conversational Version</i>),
MT (Masoretic text), LXX (pre-Christian Greek translation of the Old
Testament), ptc (participle), <i>TEV</i> (<i>Today’s English Version</i>, 1976)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.5pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><sup><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></sup></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><sup><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></sup></p>

<p class=MsoNormal align=center style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:center'><b><span
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Notable</span></b><span
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> <b>Features
of Contemporary English</b></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Active-Voice
Verbs </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Active
voice means the grammatical subject is acting in the verb: <i>“God created the
earth and sky.”</i> <u>Passive voice</u> means the subject is acted on by the
verb: <i>“the earth and sky were created by God.” Languages</i> develop passive
voice for cases (a) where the agent of action is not known, (b) where the
writer does not want to identify the agent, (c) where the agent’s identity does
not matter, (d) where putting the object of action in the subject slot connects
more easily with the preceding context. Contemporary English prefers active
voice as more straightforward, so <i>CV </i>transforms<i> </i>passive-voice
constructions into active ones wherever possible: <i>“Half of the kingdom will
be given to you”</i> can become <i>“I’ll give you half the kingdom”</i> (Esther
<b>5</b>:3, 6; see also Isaiah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>2</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:11,
<i>etc</i>.). Transformation can happen only if the context specifies the agent
(or means) elsewhere—or is at least part of general knowledge (Isaiah </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>33</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:24).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.5pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Action Verbs</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpFirst style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>Action verbs carry more punch than linking verbs or
nouns equivalents. That calls for rewording some <u>linking-verb sentences</u> with
active verbs, as in 2 Samuel <b>22</b>:19 (<i>“was my support” </i>becomes <i>“supported
me”</i>); 1 Kings <b>4</b>:5 (<i>“Nathan was overseer” </i>becomes <i>“Nathan
oversaw”</i>); Proverbs </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>15</span></b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:8 (<i>“A wicked
man’s sacrifice is detestable to the L</i></span><i><span style='font-size:
9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>”</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
becomes <i>“The L</i></span><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> detests a wicked
man’s sacrifice”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>); Jeremiah<b> 6</b>:10 (<i>“The word of
the L</i></span><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> has become a reproach to
them” </span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>becomes <i>“</i></span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Any message from the L</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
offends them”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>). Action verbs can replace <u>nouns</u>,
as in the opening statement in Joshua, <i>“After the death of Moses”</i>
becomes <i>“after Moses died.”</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>The recurring expression at the end of accounts of
judges is <i>“he died and was buried in . . .”</i> <i>CV </i>has recast the
formula as, <i>“When he died, they buried him . . . .”</i> The change gets rid
of a passive voice, replaces paratactic with hypotactic construction, and uses <i>they</i>
advisedly to increase readability (Judges </span><b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>10</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:2,
5; </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>12</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:7,
10, 11, 15). See also Deuteronomy </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>18</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:12;
Judges <b>11</b>:6, 9; Ruth <b>4</b>:14, <i>etc. “After the death of Moses”</i>
becomes <i>“After Moses died”</i> (Joshua </span><b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>1</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:1).</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpLast style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>The
principle applies to <u>expletive constructions</u> with <i>it</i> and <i>there.</i>
An expletive is a meaningless word that indicates the subject comes later in
the sentence. <i>“There was a mistake<sup>subject</sup> in the sentence”</i>
can become <i>“The sentence had a mistake in it.” “It is necessary for you to
leave<sup>subject</sup>” </i>can become <i>“You need to leave” </i>(In the
original form of the sentence, the infinitive is the conceptual subject.)
Transforming expletive sentences into action-verb formats does not always
improve them: <i>“There was a severe famine in the land”</i> (Genesis <b>12</b>:10);
<i>“It’s so good and pleasant for brothers to live together in peace”</i>
(Psalm<b> 133</b>:1). Expletive constructions are common in everyday talk, but <i>CV</i>
minimizes their use because this is, after all, a written presentation; so written
rhetorical preference overrides conversational tendencies. </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Linking
verbs sometimes get “covered up” inside of contractions with the subject: <i>“John’s
my brother.”</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.5pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.5pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Advised Usages</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>They</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
and <i>you</i> can have the value of indefinite pronouns (used “advisedly”):</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <i>they</i> in Joshua</span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> <b>24</b>:33:
<i>“</i><i>Eleazar . . . died, and they buried him at Gibeah.”</i> cp. 1 Samuel
<b>16</b>:2; Jeremiah <b>36</b>:30.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            you</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
in Genesis <b>41</b>:21: “</span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Yet when they’d eaten them, you
couldn’t tell it. They were as scrawny as before.”</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
cp. 1 Kings </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>8</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:6; Job <b>28</b>:12;
Proverbs<b> 1</b>:15; <b>20</b>:17; Jeremiah <b>24</b>:2, 3, 8; <b>19</b>:11;<b>
46</b>:23; Ezekiel <b>40</b>:40; Hosea <b>1</b>:10, <i>etc</i>.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Avoiding
Cognate Accusatives</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>C</span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ognate
accusatives can sound trite to the English ear. The construction combines a
verb with a cognate direct object. <i>CV </i>sometimes rewords them. In Hebrew <i>“die
the death”</i> adds emphasis, something like <i>“surely die”</i> (Numbers <b>23</b>:10);
<i>“pray this prayer”</i> changes to <i>“offer this prayer”</i> (2 Samuel<b> 7</b>:27);
<i>“the commandments that he commanded”</i> becomes <i>“commandments to Jacob’s
descendants”</i>). </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“If a prophet or dreamer <s>of dreams</s>
arises and gives you a sign or wonder . . . </span></i><b><sup><span
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>5</span></sup></b><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Execute
that prophet or dreamer <s>of dreams</s>” (</span></i><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Deuteronomy<b> 13</b>:1,
5). </span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>See
also 2 Kings <b>17</b>:34; <b>18</b>:6; Nehemiah<b> 1</b>:6; Psalm </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>65</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:18,
<i>etc.</i> </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:13.5pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:13.5pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Contractions<b>    </b> </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <i>The</i><i>
Conversational Version </i>uses contractions whenever natural, one of its most
noticeable features. Contractions happen with pronouns plus auxiliary verbs, auxiliary
verbs plus negatives, <i>let us</i> (<i>let’s</i>), and some noun subjects plus
third-person auxiliaries (<i>is</i>, <i>has</i>). The first group includes <i>I’d</i>,
<i>I’m</i>, <i>I’ll</i>, <i>etc.</i> The second group has <i>won’t</i>, <i>can’t</i>,
and the like. Finally, there are some examples like <i>“The L</i></span><i><span
style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>’s
my Shepherd”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (cp. Isaiah <b>25</b>:9; Joel <b>2</b>:2).
We have dispensed with contractions in the Decalogue (Exodus <b>20</b>;
Deuteronomy <b>5</b>) to create a more deliberate feel. The core of Israel’s
constitution calls for more formal expression: <i>“Do not commit murder”</i> <i>vs.</i>
<i>“Don’t murder.”</i> </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:49.5pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>NOTE: In the Ten
Commandments, we have replaced the Hebrew imperfect (uncompleted action) used as
a command form (<i>“You will not . . .”</i>) with the imperative mood in
English (<i>“Do not . . .”</i>). Secondly, <i>CV</i> has dispensed with <i>“thou
shalt”</i> as a second-person archaic pronoun-plus-imperfect in these
commandments. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Using
contractions combines with several other features of <i>CV </i>to make create
the “feel” of the text when reading it aloud. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Inclusive
Language</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Translators
render what the text says rather than what they or their audience would like it
to say. We have tried to be faithful to the sense of scripture while agreeing
that we gain nothing from holding onto older customs of English usage that
distract and distance the content from its modern audience. In the other
direction, we have avoided some forms of inclusive language that seem
cumbersome and confusing: the “<i>he or she</i>” format or alternating <i>he</i>
and <i>she</i> within a segment. Unspecified reference comes across with
something straightforward: <i>man/men </i>shifts to <i>a person/people</i>, a
plural form, changing the reference to first or second person, or an indefinite
pronoun. Other possibilities are advised <i>you</i> (Job </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>8</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:18;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>9</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3;
Psalm </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>49</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:8; Ecclesiastes <b>1</b>:10),
advised <i>they </i>(Genesis <b>17</b>:1), an abstraction expressed by a gerund
(Leviticus </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>11</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:24, 26, 27, 39; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>22</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:4,
5), by switching the construction to passive voice, which eliminates necessary
reference to agent of action (Isaiah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>28</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:4),
or by using <i>mortals</i> in place of <i>men</i> when the contrast is with the
divine: Genesis</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 6</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3; Judges </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>9</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:9;
Job </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>4</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:17;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>5</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:7;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>14</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:1;
2 Chronicles </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>14</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:11; Job </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>2</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:4.
Another practice that is becoming increasing useful is using a plural pronoun to
reference a singular antecedent. Trying to accommodate current proclivities in
this matter poses one of the more challenging aspects of biblical translation
into contemporary English usage. That is because of the all-pervasive practice
historically of using the same term for the human species and the male of the human
species in Hebrew, Greek, Latin, standard English, <i>etc.</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            More
importantly, we have followed scripture’s precedent in its uniform reference to
Judaeo-Christian deity and angelic beings in masculine terms, despite sex as something
irrelevant to the spiritual realm (John </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>4</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:24
[<i>“God is spirit”</i>] plus Luke </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>24</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:39
[<i>“A spirit doesn’t have flesh and bones as you can see I have.”</i>]).<i> </i>God
does not have sex and is not a combination of sexes; God is transcendent to
sexuality, being transcendent to physicality. The surmise is that, given the
confines of human understanding and human language based on it, masculine
terminology maintains, not only the personhood (<i>vs.</i> <i>it</i>), but the
primacy and power of God and other spiritual and physical beings. </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>The
God of the Bible has ultimate responsibility for the rest of reality, hence
authority over it and ultimate power to direct it. Working backward through
that series, plays on his personal omnipotence that issues in stability and
security, and guarantees hope for his people. The human parallel <i>in his
image</i>—the interpersonal capacity, we take it—likewise issues in an
observation that greater physical strength conveys greater psychological
security in those affected by another person’s actions. It is more difficult
for women to project strength, hence, power, and security (cp. Genesis <b>17</b>:1;
2 Peter<b> 2</b>:11). </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            </span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Traditionally,
Hebrew, Greek, Latin, English, and many other languages have used <i>man</i> in
contrast to woman (hence, male), God (hence, humankind, mankind), animal
(hence, humankind; Numbers </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>3</span></b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:13), child
(hence, adult as later in 1 Corinthians </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>13</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:11),
thing, or idea. As said above, it has been used both for the “species” and the
male of the species. This version has avoided <i>man/men</i> for mixed or
unspecified reference when able to do so without creating awkward expression. In
Proverbs, however, <i>CV</i> has retained <i>son</i> in the vocative because
the book pictures a father teaching his son. Similarly in Ecclesiastes, <i>man</i>
is retained in situations where the king is speaking about his own experience
since presumably King Solomon is speaking.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            The
word <i>man</i> appears where it contrasts with woman. In the other
circumstances, we have usually rendered <i>man</i> (</span><span lang=HE
dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>אדם</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span>) as <i>a person</i>, <i>people</i>, an indefinite pronoun, a
plural, <i>a mortal</i>, or some other inclusive term; deleted it altogether
(Micah <b>6</b>:8) or appealed to passive voice. In such cases, <i>man</i> in
the original text itself did not mean “man in contrast to woman”; it meant <i>humankind
vs.</i> other orders of being or kinds. So, the adjustments do not involve
altering intended content.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                                                                                                                    </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Interjections      
</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:-4.5pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:40.5pt'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Written
English minimizes the use of interjections. The Old Testament has a number of them,
and <i>CV</i> keeps a few: <i>ha</i> (Ezekiel</span><b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 25</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:2;
<b>26:</b>2; </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>36</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:2); <i>ah</i> (Jeremiah <b>34</b>:5;
Ezekiel <b>4</b>:14). The original Hebrew and Aramaic terms are as follows:</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            </span><span
lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>הוֹי</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span lang=HE style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span dir=LTR></span> </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>is
the most common interjection, appearing especially in Isaiah. It comes across in
English translations as <i><u>a</u>las, ah</i>, <i>ha</i>, <i><u>w</u>oe</i>: 1
Kings </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>13</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:30<sup>ah</sup>; Isaiah <b>1</b>:24<sup>x</sup>;
<b>5</b>:8<sup>woe</sup>, 11<sup>w</sup>, 18<sup>w</sup>, 20<sup>w</sup>, 21<sup>w</sup>;
<b>5</b>:22<sup>w</sup>; <b>10</b>:1<sup>w</sup>, 5<sup>w</sup>; <b>17</b>:12<sup>ah</sup>;
<b>18</b>:1<sup>ah</sup>; <b>28</b>:1<sup>w</sup>; <b>29</b>:1<sup>w</sup>, 15<sup>w</sup>;
<b>30</b>:1<sup>w</sup>; <b>31</b>:1<sup>w</sup>; <b>33</b>:1<sup>w</sup>; <b>45</b>:9<sup>w</sup>,
10<sup>w</sup>; Jeremiah <b>22</b>:13<sup>w</sup>, 18<sup>2x</sup>; <b>23</b>:1<sup>w</sup>;
<b>30</b>:7<sup>x</sup>; <b>34</b>:5<sup>ha</sup>; <b>47</b>:6<sup>x</sup>; <b>48</b>:1<sup>w</sup>,
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>50</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:27<sup>w</sup>;
Amos <b>6</b>:1<sup>w</sup>; Micah<b> 2</b>:1<sup>w</sup>; Nehemiah <b>3</b>:1<sup>w</sup>;
Habakkuk <b>2</b>:6<sup>w</sup>, 9<sup>w</sup>, 12<sup>w</sup>, 15<sup>w</sup>,
19<sup>w</sup>; Zephaniah <b>2</b>:5<sup>w</sup>; <b>3</b>:1<sup>w</sup>;
Zechariah <b>2</b>:6<sup>x</sup>, 7<sup>x</sup>; <b>11</b>:17<sup>w *</sup>. <i>CV</i>
avoids the expression as archaic, substituting some other wording to catch the
sentiment (Amos</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 5</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:16, 18).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>אההּ</span><span dir=LTR></span><span
lang=HE style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span> </span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>is not translated (Joshua <b>7</b>:7;
Judges <b>6</b>:22; <b>11</b>:35; 2 Kings <b>3</b>:10; <b>6</b>:5, 15; Jeremiah
<b>1</b>:6; <b>4</b>:10; <b>14</b>:13; <b>32</b>:17; Ezekiel <b>4</b>:14; <b>9</b>:8;
<b>11</b>:13; <b>20</b>:49*).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            </span><span
lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>הֶאָח</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span> is in Job <b>39</b>:25<sup>aha</sup>; Psalm <b>35</b>:21<sup>2
aha</sup>; <b>40</b>:15<sup>2 aha</sup>; <b>70</b>:3<sup>2 ah</sup> Isaiah <b>44</b>:16<sup>ah</sup>;
Ezekiel <b>25</b>:3<sup>ha</sup>; <b>26</b>:2<sup>ha</sup>;<b> 36</b>:2*.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      </span><span lang=HE dir=RTL
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>אָח</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span> is in Ezekiel <b>6</b>:11<sup>x</sup>;<b> 21</b>:15*.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      </span><span
lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>הֵא</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span> is in Genesis <b>47</b>:23<sup>x</sup>; Ezekiel <b>16</b>:43<sup>w</sup>;
Daniel <b>2</b>:43<sup>x</sup>*.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      </span><span
lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>הָא</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span> is in Daniel <b>3</b>:25<sup>x</sup>*.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>CV</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
has not taken the liberty of using cursing. Exodus </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>5</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:21
is kept at <i>“Yahveh condemn you.”</i> At this time, so far removed from the
original, what constituted cursing may be uncertain. Cursing usually involves
distinct pitch patterns, which are not available in a written text that does
not have punctuation to indicate features of the oral presentation. It would
not be surprising if the import of Pharaoh’s word carried the force of <i>“God
damn you.”</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Litotes
Avoided</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            </span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Litotes
is a figure of speech that makes an affirmation by denying its opposite. It has
been replaced, for example, in Deuteronomy <b>5</b>:11: <i>“He will not leave
him unpunished who takes his name in vain”</i> becomes “</span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>He
will punish whoever does that</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>.” Similarly, </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Joshua
<b>3</b>:10; 1 Samuel <b>20</b>:5; Proverbs <b>6</b>:29;<b> 17</b>:5; <i>etc.</i>;
but kept in Deuteronomy</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> <b>33</b>:6 and Proverbs <b>16</b>:5; <b>19</b>:4.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Negating
the Verb</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Current
English idiom prefers negating the verb rather than the direct object: <i>“you
give him nothing”</i> becomes <i>“you don’t help him”</i> (Deuteronomy<b> 15</b>:9;
cp. 1 Samuel </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>12</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:15; <b>18</b>:24; 2
Chronicles <b>18</b>:15; Ezra <b>4</b>:3; Daniel <b>6</b>:5, <i>etc.</i>).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Nouns
Typical of Their Class</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            In
place of a definite article for nouns typical of their class, contemporary English
prefers an <u>indefinite article</u>: <i>“Miriam . . . took a/<s>the</s>
tambourine”</i> (Exodus <b>15</b>:20); </span><b><sup><span style='font-size:
14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“</span></sup></b><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>But
a/<s>the</s> nation that puts its neck under his yoke, I’ll let remain on its
land”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> (Jeremiah<b> 11</b>:8).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            In
some cases, <u>plural nouns</u> can stand for the class: <i>“[The gleanings]
will be for foreigners, orphans, or widows”</i> (Deuteronomy <b>24</b>:19); “</span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Worms
will eat them” </span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(Deuteronomy <b>28</b>:39);<i> “Go look at
ants”</i></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> (Proverbs <b>6</b>:6).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Other
cases of translating nouns typical of their class include </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Exodus
<b>13</b>:49; Numbers <b>35</b>:6, 12-13; Deuteronomy<b> 19</b>:4; Ruth <b>3</b>:14
(a woman); 1 Samue<b>l 16</b>:16; <b>17</b>:36; 2 Kings <b>8</b>:19; 2
Chronicles <b>7</b>:13, 15: <b>25</b>:18; <b>26</b>:13; Nehemiah 6:13; <b>11</b>:13;
Esther <b>6</b>:6;<b> 7</b>:17; Psalm <b>10</b>:18; <b>34</b>:19; <b>49</b>:4;<b>
92</b>:10; Proverbs <b>14</b>:1; <b>23</b>:24; Ecclesiastes<b> 1</b>:14. 16; Isaiah
<b>11</b>:6-8; <b>24</b>:2; Isaiah<b> 28</b>:24; <b>32</b>:5; <b>40</b>:10; <b>41</b>:19;
<b>44</b>:7; <b>51</b>:8; Jeremiah <b>27</b>:11, 28; Ezekiel <b>18</b>:13, 18,
19;<b> 22</b>:20, 22; Hosea <b>9</b>:10; Hosea <b>13</b>:3; <b>14</b>:5-6; Zechariah
<b>14</b>:14, 18; Malachi <b>3</b>:5.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:45.0pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>NOTE:<i> CV</i> has
translated Malachi <b>2</b>:17 as <i>“Where’s a just God?”</i> as if to say
there is no such thing. The Hebrew article does appear on <i>“justice”</i> (<i>“x
God of [the] justice”</i>), which would normally make the construct noun
definite as well: “the just God” (a noun in construct cannot carry the
article). The rendering supposes this statement involves a case of a noun
typical of its class. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <u>No
article</u> can cover the <i>“kind of thing,”</i> similar to a class: <i>“Give
me liberty or give me death.” </i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Numbers</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><b><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            </span></i></b><u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>A</span></u><u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>rabic
cardinal</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> numbers appear more often than style manuals advise; they
are easier to spot in a text. They occur to indicate reigns, distances, dimensions,
especially in contexts where groups of numbers come up: Genesis <b>46</b>:26,
27; <b>47</b>:9; Exodus </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>25</span></b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:53; Judges<b> 7</b>:5;
<b>8</b>:10; </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>11</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:26; <b>20</b>:44; 1
Kings <b>9</b>:23; <b>10</b>:12, 20; Daniel <b>13</b>:11-12. In several places,
sentences even start with Arabic numbers: Judges </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>7</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:6;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>20</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:34,
44; Numbers </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>25</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:9; 2 Kings <b>2</b>:7; and
so on.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Only
twice do <u>ordinal numbers</u> come in Arabic form: the Genesis flood began in
the </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>600<sup>th</sup></span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
year of Noah’s life (Genesis <b>7</b>:11); Solomon began to build the Temple in
the </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>480<sup>th</sup></span></b><sup><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> </span></sup><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>year
after the Israelites came out of Egypt (1 Kings <b>6</b>:1). </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Positive
Statement</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Written
expression prefers positive over negative statements. Some passages <i>CV</i>
transforms from passive to active statements: 1 Kings<b> 2</b>:4 (<i>“Will not
fail to have a man on Israel’s throne” </i>becomes <i>“will always have a man
on Israel’s throne”</i>); Isaiah <b>48</b>:16 (<i>“not secretly” </i>becomes <i>“openly”</i>);
cp. Genesis<b> 8</b>:22; Ruth <b>4</b>:15; Ezra </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>4</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:22;
Job <b>27</b>:5; Psalm <b>44</b>:18; Proverbs <b>2</b>:19; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>22</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:6;
Jeremiah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>32</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:30; <b>35</b>:19; Ezekiel <b>16</b>:22,
43.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Post-Positive
Intensive Pronouns   </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Modern
English puts intensive pronouns at the end of clauses rather than immediately
after their antecedents. 1 Samuel <b>19</b>:22 says, </span><b><i><sup><span
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“</span></sup></i></b><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Then
Saul went to Ramah himself”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>; similarly, Genesis <b>31</b>:39; 2
Samuel <b>12</b>:28; 2 Chronicles <b>26</b>:20; Nehemiah<b> 4</b>:10; Job <b>19</b>:27;
<b>21</b>:20; Psalm <b>57</b>:6; Proverbs <b>22</b>:16; Isaiah <b>41</b>:17;
Jeremiah 21:5; <b>46</b>:2; Lamentations <b>1</b>:4; Ezekiel <b>14</b>:4, 7; <b>18</b>:11;
<b>34</b>:11, 25; Daniel<b> 6</b>:16.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></i></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Post-Positive
Prepositions</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Ending
sentences with prepositions has become standard in conversation and informal
writing. The usage connects the object of the preposition more closely to its
antecedent: <i>“the man [that] I gave the book to.”</i> The format also avoids the
dreaded <i>whom</i>, as when it combines with a syntax that drops the objective
pronoun in personal relative clauses: <i>“the man <s>whom</s> I gave the book
to.”</i><i> </i>See Joshua <b>22</b>:17; Ecclesiastes <b>3</b>:10;</span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
4</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:8;
Isaiah <b>19</b>:16; <b>40</b>:25; <b>46</b>:14, <i>etc.</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Pro-Verbs
</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>English,
oral English especially, (a) replaces a repeated verb with a stand-alone <i>do/did/done
</i>(<i>“Don’t make the same mistake I <u>did</u>”</i>); (b) uses the verb’s
auxiliary (<i>“She was taking the same course I <u>was</u> <s>taking</s>”</i>),
or (c) uses only the sign of the infinitive (<i>“unless the doctor tells you to
<s>quit</s>”</i>). See Genesis </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>19</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:22;
<b>20</b>:7; <b>24</b>:6; </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>43</span></b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:5; Deuteronomy <b>19</b>:20;
Joshua <b>5</b>:5; <b>6</b>:17??; Judges </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>6</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:4;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>8</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:23;
Ruth <b>3</b>:13; 1 Samuel </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>1</span></b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:1; <b>13</b>:22; <b>18</b>:30;
2 Samuel </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>12</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:28; Job <b>42</b>:7; Psalm </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>106</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:34;
Proverbs <b>15</b>:7; Ecclesiastes <b>9</b>:2; Jeremiah <b>37</b>:9 (<i>“they
won’t”</i>); Ezekiel <b>18</b>:24, 29; Hosea <b>8</b>:7; <i>etc.</i> Hebrew
does not have auxiliary verbs; so it lacks pro-verb constructions.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Restrained
Expression</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>English
writers tend away from superlatives, especially elative superlatives like <i>very</i>,<i>
really</i>, <i>completely</i>. They do it to avoid the suspicion that they are artificially
heightening the importance of what they are saying. <i>CV</i> has dispensed
with several of them (Zechariah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>8</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:2,
<i>etc</i>.). </span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>Included in this matter is a Hebrew idiom for
intensified action: infinitive absolute plus cognate finite verb: Genesis <b>22</b>:14
(ptc).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Split
Infinitives </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Split
infinitives are losing their place among proscribed English constructions: <i>“to
boldly go where no one has gone . . .”</i> In classical languages and their
derivatives, infinities are one word created by a suffix. In English, <i>to</i>
stands separate from the root (if <i>to</i> is expressed) and has no more
reason to function as a bound construction than a progressive verb does: <i>to
go/to quickly go</i>; <i>they were going</i>/<i>they were not going</i>. See
Deuteronomy <b>17</b>:18 (<i>“to greatly increase silver and gold”</i>); <b>7</b>:24;
<b>6</b>:24; Ruth<b> 1</b>:16; 2 Samuel <b>14</b>:32; Nehemiah </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>11</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:13;
Proverbs <b>19</b>:2; Ecclesiastes </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>5</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:5;
Isaiah <b>29</b>:15; <b>51</b>:13; <i>etc.</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Miscellaneous
Grammatical Points</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <i>CV</i>
has one case of double subject (<i>“My brother, he . . . .”</i>) in Psalm </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>24</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:10:
<i>“The L</i></span><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> of Hosts, <u>he</u>
is the glorious King”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (for emphasis), otherwise called a
pendens construction.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <i>Who
</i>regularly stands in slots where traditional grammar calls for <i>whom</i>: <i>“Who
will I be afraid of? Who will I dread?”</i> (Psalm </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>27</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:1).
</span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“Who
would he want to honor more than me?”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (Esther <b>6</b>:6); Genesis
<b>19</b>:12; <b>32</b>:17; Joshua <b>24</b>:15; 1 Samuel <b>10</b>:24;<b> 28</b>:11;<b>
30</b>:13; 2 Samuel <b>3</b>:12; 2 Kings <b>2</b>:22; <b>18</b>:20; Ecclesiastes
<b>4</b>:8<sup>2</sup>; Lamentations <b>2</b>:20; Isaiah <b>6</b>:8; <b>10</b>:3;
<b>53</b>:1; <b>57</b>:4<sup>3</sup>, 11. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Idiom
can override grammar: <i>“If I go into the city, there<u>’s</u> famine and
disease”</i> (Jeremiah <b>14</b>:18).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Anachronisms</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <i>CV</i>
has resisted the temptation to express information in ways that are
anachronistic; that is, the wording has not carried dynamic equivalency to the
point of using expressions that do not fit with Old Testament times—even though
the modern equivalents would identify closer with today’s readership. “Casting
lots” is retained in place of <i>“flipping a coin”</i> (Proverbs <b>18</b>:18)
or <i>“throwing dice”</i> (Psalm <b>22</b>:18); <i>“a nagging wife is like
constant dripping”</i> instead of a <i>“dripping faucet”</i> (Proverbs <b>19</b>:13);
speaking of employer-employee in places that talk about master-slave. In cases
where the form of the document seems involved, <i>CV</i> calls it a <i>“scroll”</i>
rather than a <i>“book,”</i> which in the modern usage conjures up a set of
bound leaves, a codex.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></b></p>

<p class=MsoNormal align=center style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:center'><b><span
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Conversational
Characteristics</span></b></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></b></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>All
languages experience “linguistic drift.” In addition, at any one time more than
one “level” of usage also exists from formal and technical to everyday and slang.
We have endeavored to strike a middle level that avoids formal, academic
expression while staying away from regionalisms, slang; flippant, shocking, catchy,
cutesy verbiage; unrequired pejorative words and terms and expressions with
negative connotations: Exodus </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>5</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:21
could sound like swearing, <i>“The L</i></span><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
condemn you!”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> Because of its source and subject matter,
scripture calls for respectful expression that everybody can understand. Besides,
non-native speakers might not recognize English slang, regionalisms, jive, and
the like. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>We
have followed American custom in spelling, vocabulary, syntax, and punctuation where
they differ from British conventions (-ize/-ise, -er/-re when not comparative
degree or agency, or/-our, and so on). Collective nouns have accompanying
singular verbs: <i>“The committee holds session daily”</i> rather than <i>“hold”</i>).
The practices highlighted below add to observations presented earlier in
“Current English Features.” </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></b></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Contrary-to-Fact
Sentences</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            English
has virtually lost the subjunctive mood. It occurs almost exclusively in the
protasis of a contrary-to-fact sentence: <i>“If I <u>were</u> John, . . .”</i>
People usually do not employ the subjunctive even there: <i>“If I was John . .
.”</i> A hearer knows from experience that the statement is contrary-to-fact
without hearing a special construction for it. What is not necessary tends to
fall out of usage, so we have broken the formal rule in some places:
Deuteronomy <b>28</b>:27<sup>2</sup>; Joshua <b>22</b>:32; 1 Samuel <b>20</b>:20;
2 Samuel<b> 19</b>:6; 2 Kings<b> 5</b>:3; Job </span><b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>16</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:4;
Psalm </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>35</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:14</span><sup><span
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>2</span></sup><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>;
Isaiah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>49</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:26; Jeremiah <b>9</b>:1; <b>22</b>:24;
Job<b> 6</b>:2; <b>50</b>:12; </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“Dative
of Possession” Avoided</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“<u>For</u>
the Jews there was light and gladness and joy and honor”</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
becomes </span><b><i><sup><span style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“</span></sup></i></b><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>The
Jews had light, happiness, joy, and honor”</span></i><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (Esther <b>8</b>:16;
cp. Daniel<b> 9</b>:7, 9).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Distribution
Replacing Case </span></p>
