<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Since
English has lost case-form everywhere except in pronouns, speakers have become
unskilled in “proper” case usage with pronouns personal, relative, intensive, and
reflexive. In place of case-form, English uses distribution in a sentence; it determines
a word’s role by its location (where it appears in a sentence) rather than by its
form. When it comes to pronouns, the growing practice is to put the subjective
form ahead of the verb and the objective form after it even if it is a linking
verb: <i>“It’s not me. God will give you a sensible answer.”</i> (Genesis <b>41</b>:16;
cp. Genesis <b>39</b>:9; Exodus <b>4</b>:11; 1 Kings </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>22</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:33;
2 Kings </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>9</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:37; Job <b>6</b>:2; <b>16</b>:4; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>34</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:33;
Isaiah <b>45</b>:3, 21; <b>49</b>:26).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            See
“Individual Words” in the final section of these Notes for current usage
changes with individual words like <i>a</i>, <i>as</i>, <i>can</i>,<i> even, for,
if, just, lest, like</i>, <i>man</i>, <i>of</i>, <i>one</i>, <i>only</i>, <i>than</i>,
<i>they</i>, <i>whom</i>, <i>you</i>.    </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Everyday
Vocabulary</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Newer
translations have set aside the old English second-person pronouns (<i>thou/thee/thine</i>,
<i>ye</i>) and second- and third-person singular verb suffixes (<i>-[e]<b>st</b>/[e]<b>th</b></i>),
obsolete vocabulary, and spellings that appeared in Bible versions before the
1940s. That upgrading has helped take away an antiquated, foreign feel to the Bible.
Scripture addresses every person everywhere at every time, so its language
should suit the culture at any time and place (readership verisimilitude).   </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>People’s
reading vocabulary is bigger than their speaking one; it is the latter that we
have aimed for, though we are presenting in written form. Even then, typical
conversations, as at the bus stop, show a preference for a narrower range of vocabulary,
expressions, and sentence structures than what the same speakers would use in a
public presentation. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Everyday
vocabulary includes minimizing the use of “religious” jargon to talk in a way
typical people identify with. That goal does not succeed well because adequate substitutes
do not exist for everything religious concern addresses.</span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> A
sizeable set of English words in Old Testament texts currently appear mostly in
modern religious contexts with religious connotations—sometimes negative ones.
It is difficult to find authentic-sounding, current replacements for Bible
words like Almighty, bless(ed/ness), defile, flesh, glory, godly, heaven (for
sky), holy/holiness, iniquity, joy, proclaim, prophesy/prophecy, rejoice,
repent/ance, righteous/ness, redeemer, salvation/save/savior, sanctify,
sin/sinner/sinful/sinless, testify (outside of courtroom proceedings),
transgression, voice (for sound), witness (outside of court proceedings),
worship, wrath, and so on.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>The
Conversational Version</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> has not aimed at a certain “grade
level” even though it tends toward that effect by simplifying vocabulary and shortening
sentences.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Pendens
Constructions Avoided</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Pendens
constructions mark off appositives or stray comments, especially long ones that
are not grammatically integral parts of their sentences or modifying an
integral element. We have <u>eliminated</u> them, for example, in Numbers <b>14</b>:22-23;
Judges <b>17</b>:2; Jeremiah <b>13</b>:11; Amos <b>8</b>:14; <b>9</b>:5-6. They
are <u>retained</u> in several places like Genesis <b>2</b>:19; <b>23</b>:15; <b>24</b>:14,
43-44; 2 Kings <b>17</b>:36; Proverbs <b>20</b>:10; Nehemiah<b> 13</b>:10;
Isaiah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>56</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:6-7.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>CV</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
has used a couple nominative absolutes: Exodus </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>22</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:11,
13.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Possessive Gerunds Avoided</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpFirst style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.5pt;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>The
Message the L</span></i><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> Spoke to Jeremiah
About Nebuchadnezzar Coming to Strike Egypt” </span></i><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>appears in the Jeremiah
<b>46</b>:13 heading instead of the ‘correct’ <i>“Nebuchadnezzar’s Coming to Strike
Egypt.” </i>(The subject of a gerund in standard English carries the possessive
case.)</span><b><sup><span style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> </span></sup></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Zephaniah <b>2</b>:8
says, <i>“I’ve heard Moab’s taunting my people and Ammon’s reviling them” </i>changed
to <i>“I’ve heard Moab taunting my people and Ammon reviling them” </i>(using
participial objective complements). But see Jeremiah: <i>“I remember . . . your
following me in the open country”</i> (Jeremiah <b>2</b>:2).</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpLast style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.5pt;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:0in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Quotation Marks </span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpFirst style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>Quotation marks identify direct discourse, that is, a
speaker’s purported words. Imbedded quotations are direct discourse that falls within
direct discourse: Isaiah told the king, <i>“This is what the L</i></span><i><span
style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
says, ‘Set your house in order; you’re going to die’”</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
(1 Kings <b>20</b>:1). Sometimes embedded quotations can go three, four, five,
or maybe even six levels deep: </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“The L</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
says, ‘These people say, “It’s not time to rebuild the L</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>’s
house”’”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> (Haggai <b>1</b>:2). Zechariah is four deep in <b>1</b>:2-6.
</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>English uses indirect discourse much more frequently
than the Hebrew Old Testament does, so <i>CV</i> transforms quite a number from
direct to indirect form. A list of many such transforms appears later under
“indirect <u>discourse</u> formats.”</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>Texts with indirect <u>questions</u> include Genesis <b>24</b>:47;
Exodus <b>12</b>:26; <b>16</b>:15; Joshua <b>4</b>:3; <b>15</b>:18; Judges<b> 6</b>:29;<b>
9</b>:2;<b> 12</b>:5; 2 Samuel <b>14</b>:32; 2 Kings <b>9</b>:17, 18, 19;<b> 18</b>:22;
Ezekiel <b>12</b>:9; <b>20</b>:49; <b>21</b>:7; Zechariah <b>1</b>:21; <b>2</b>:2;
<b>5</b>:10. </span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>Quotation marks appear around words that indicate the
meaning of place names: See below under “Mechanics of Written English:
Quotation Marks.”</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpLast style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Sentence
Structures</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Using
<u>shorter sentences</u> mimics the way people talk in contrast to their formal
writing. Complex-compound sentences yield to smaller units unless they are
short. Compound-complex sentences have two or more independent clauses along
with one or more subordinate ones: <i>“She opened the back door and let the dog
out into the back yard because she wanted to mop the kitchen floor.”</i> </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>If
the original text has a lengthy sentence, a period break can appear with
italicized re-start words for the rest of the thought (unitalicized if inside
italics):</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpFirst style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:49.5pt;
margin-bottom:10.0pt;margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><b><sup><span
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>14</span></sup></b><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(“</span></i><i><span
style='font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Don’t worship any other god,
because the L</span></i><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span style='font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>—whose name is Exclusive—is an exclusive God.</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>)
</span></i><b><sup><span style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>15</span></sup></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Destroy those things </span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif;
color:#C00000'>so you won’t </span></i><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>be making covenants with
them, carrying on prostitution with their gods, sacrificing to them, eating
their sacrifices with them—</span></i><b><sup><span style='font-size:14.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>16</span></sup></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif;
color:#C00000'>so you won’t </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>be </span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>taking
their daughters for your sons, and their daughters carry on prostitution with
their gods and get your sons to do it” </span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(Exodus <b>34</b>:14-16).<i>
</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpLast style='margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>“Destroy these things”</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (unitalicized
in verse 15 from verse 13) and “<i>so you won’t”</i> (unitalicized in verse 16
within italics) re-starts the statement in <b>34</b>:15 so readers can more
easily follow the train of thought.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>CV</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> aims
for the more common <u>sentence sequence</u>: subject, verb, direct object,
indirect object. If the indirect object is short, it may appear ahead of the
direct object without its sign (<i>to</i>). <i>CV</i> avoids putting much material
ahead of the subject, like Samson’s riddle does in Judges <b>14:</b>14: <i>“Out
of the eater came something to eat</i>.<i>”</i> An English verb seldom stands
ahead of its subject: <i>“the L</i></span><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
says,” </span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>rather than <i>“says the L</i></span><i><span
style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>”</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
(Ezekiel <b>11</b>:21, <i>etc.</i>). Direct object before subject does happen
in 2 Chronicles <b>23</b>:14 to avoid having too much material between the verb
and direct object if put in its standard place: “<i>Whoever follows her, put to
death with a sword.”</i> So, sentences keep to a minimum the amount of adverbial
material placed ahead of the subject and between the other main parts of a
sentence—subject and verb, verb and direct object. Sentences use introductory
adverbial clauses in place of participial phrases and “nominative” absolutes. (Only
in a few set expressions do English speakers ever use nominative absolutes
anyway: <i>“Truth be told”</i>; <i>“That said.”</i>). If an introductory clause
is too long, it and the main clause can become two independent clauses joined
by a comma plus coordinating conjunction or perhaps recast as two sentences
with a semi-colon between plus no conjunction.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Informal
speech tends to be <u>paratactic</u>. It reduces the frequency of introductory adverbial
clauses, participial phrases, and absolute constructions. For example, <i>“Opening
the door, she let the dog out.”</i> The wording is more likely, <i>“She opened
the door and let the dog out.”</i> Paratactic contrasts with hypotactic.
Paratactic format puts ideas—opening the door, putting the dog out—on the same
grammatical level by using <i>and</i> between equal verbal forms. Hypotactic structure
subordinates one grammatically with a participial phrase (<i>“Opening the door,
she put the dog out.”</i>),<i> </i>introductory adverbial clause (<i>“When she
opened the door, she let the dog out.”</i>), or infinitive phrase (<i>“She
opened the door to let the dog out,”</i>). The last two examples also nuance
the meaning differently in ways that the context might suggest.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>English
speech increasingly practices <u>asyndeton</u>, where speakers say sentences
without putting coordinate—or even subordinate—conjunctions between them,
especially if the sentences are long. Hebrew has the opposite, syndetic pattern;
it puts its coordinate conjunction between virtually every full sentence (as
well as between serial items in it). Whole books can start with <i>and</i>:
Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, Joshua, Judges, 1 &amp; 2 Samuel, Kings, and so on.
See below under “Hebrew-English Shifts.” <i>CV</i> reduces the number of subordinating
conjunctions, especially where a second clause supplies a reason for the first.
As based on experience and knowing the nature of things, if a listener will
understand that the second clause states a reason for the first, <i>because</i>
or <i>so </i>does not have to appear at all (Deuteronomy <b>15</b>:2; <b>28</b>:39).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>These
comments on conversational style describe an endeavor similar to creating a <u>paraphrase</u>.
A paraphrase should be more exacting than <i>“telling it in our own words.”</i>
That would more easily risk losing content or altering meaning. This version makes
a more deliberate, element-by-element decision on the way to express individual
points. The translation considers (1) grammatical and (2) custom-of-usage levels.
It also considers (a) whether an element in the original can be dropped without
loss (repeated material), (b) whether it can be expressed in fewer words
(economy of expression), (c) whether it can be relocated for more natural
English sequencing (smoother connections), and (d) whether it can be left to a
reader’s automatic inference. Below, “Shortening Mechanisms” indicates
procedures for more efficient, more interesting, expression. Hebrew idiom tends
to use pleonastic, replete, tautological, redundant expression—perhaps for
emphasis—in contrast to English. But that does not require reducing content; it
means eliminating repetition and statements about implicit information. Interesting
communication says only enough for the reader to flesh out the rest of the
picture accurately. Much of the effort in preparing this version has consisted
in tightening up the manner of expression.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal align=center style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:center'><b><span
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Hebrew-English
Differences</span></b></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></b></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>The
previous two sections (“Features of Contemporary English” and “Conversational
Characteristics”) included some Hebrew-English <u>differences</u>. This section
notes other differences between Hebrew and English <u>custom of usage</u>. We
have limited our alterations in many places that would obscure structural
features in the text: poetic parallelism, bookends, poetic repetition, portions
organized around body parts, and a number of set expressions like <i>afraid or
intimidated</i>, <i>strong hand and outstretched arm</i>, <i>strong and
courageous</i>, <i>eat and drink</i>, <i>food and water, day and night</i>, <i>on
every high hill and under every green tree</i>, <i>come back on (their) own
head</i>; and various combinations of the related descriptors: <i>commandments,
ordinances, statutes, laws, regulations, charges, </i>and<i> judgments through
Moses.</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></b></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Anaphoric Entries</span></p>

<p class=MsoListParagraphCxSpFirst style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(a)<span
style='font:7.0pt "Times New Roman"'>&nbsp;&nbsp; </span></span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>anaphoric
nouns </span></p>

<p class=MsoListParagraphCxSpLast style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Hebrew
creates a kind of grandiloquence by re-using a noun instead of replacing it
with a pronoun in subsequent entries. We have changed that pattern to English
preference for pronouns: Genesis <b>27</b>:22; <b>29</b>:12; Exodus <b>33</b>:7-11;
Joshua <b>6</b>:6; <b>10</b>:24; 1 Samuel <b>11</b>:6; 2 Samuel <b>21</b>:15
check; 2 Kings <b>15</b>:4; 2 Chronicles <b>28</b>:24; <b>35</b>:24-27; Psalm 1<b>15</b>:11,
13; <b>116</b>:13; <b>42</b>:1; <b>118</b>:7, 9, 14, 26; <b>44</b>:21; <b>135</b>:3,
4.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Poetry might
justify reusing a noun because, if sung/chanted as in the Psalms, the delivery might
be slower with more spacing between entries. Even there we reduce from four to
three, for example, <i>the</i> <i>L</i></span><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>’s
in Psalm <b>33</b>:1-5.</span></p>

<p class=MsoListParagraph style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(b)<span
style='font:7.0pt "Times New Roman"'>&nbsp; </span></span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>anaphoric
adjectives </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Personal possessive
suffixes in Hebrew append to each word, but English can put one possessive
ahead of a list because the possessive is a separate word: Numbers <b>14</b>:23;
<b>18</b>:22; Deuteronomy<b> 6</b>:9; 2 Kings <b>18</b>:12; 1 Chronicles <b>28</b>:6;
Nehemiah<b> 5</b>:10, 13; Psalm <b>7</b>:8; <b>8</b>:3; <b>27</b>:1; <b>35</b>:38;
<b>84</b>:3. That free-standing adjective can then apply to two or more
subsequent nouns. Sometimes that double or triple application might not be
correct, so the adjective-noun combination can go last in the clause,</span></p>

<p class=MsoListParagraph style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(c)<span
style='font:7.0pt "Times New Roman"'>&nbsp;&nbsp; </span></span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>anaphoric
articles </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>This version often
omits second articles: (“the x and the y” becomes “the x and y”): Genesis <b>1</b>:1:
<i>“God created the heavens and the earth”</i> (<i>“created the earth and sky”</i>);
note also Nehemiah <b>5</b>:6, 11<sup>2</sup>; Esther <b>1</b>:16. Using one
article in these situations does alter slightly how the author views the nouns’
referents—as two separate items of equal importance here or as one set comprised
of two items. The semantic value remains.</span></p>

<p class=MsoListParagraph style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(d)<span
style='font:7.0pt "Times New Roman"'>&nbsp; </span></span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>anaphoric
prepositions </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Hebrew has several
inseparable prepositions, which cannot have multiple objects because they prefix
to their objects. They are not free standing as they are in English, Greek, and
Latin. So, they are re-expressed for each object in a series. We have
eliminated the re-expressions in places like Exodus<b> 29</b>:27.</span></p>

<p class=MsoListParagraphCxSpFirst style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(e)<span
style='font:7.0pt "Times New Roman"'>&nbsp;&nbsp; </span></span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>anaphoric
conjunctions (See <i>and</i> under “Individual Words” below.)</span></p>

<p class=MsoListParagraphCxSpLast style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(f)<span
style='font:7.0pt "Times New Roman"'>&nbsp;&nbsp; </span></span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>recurrent
descriptors that seem unnecessary for clarity as in “the Law of Moses, the man
of God” (2 Chronicles </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>30</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:16) and similar additions
like 2 Chronicles </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>34</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:33 (“their God”); </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>35</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>36</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:13,
15</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Body-Part Imagery</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Hebrew
uses body parts to symbolically express many ideas in ways not customary in
English that has not been influenced by biblical expression: arm, bones,
bowels, cheek (Lamentations <b>3</b>:30), ears, eyes, face, feet, fingers,
flesh, forehead, hair, hand, head, heart, knee/knees, leg, lips, loins, mouth,
neck, shoulder, teeth, throat, toes, tongue, voice. <i>CV </i>drops them in
favor of English idiom whenever possible.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(a) voice/sound of
x: 1 Kings <b>1</b>:48; <b>5</b>:24; <b>18</b>:9; 2 Kings <b>21</b>:8; 2
Chronicles <b>30</b>:27; Jeremiah <b>47</b>:3; Ezekiel <b>26</b>:13</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      (b) agency: arm</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      (c) perspective:
eyes</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      (d) <i>“(stiff)
neck”</i>: stubbornness</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Such
expression may remain in poetic structures or in body-part lists for oratorical
flare: Deuteronomy <b>29</b>:4; Nehemiah</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 1</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:6;
Job <b>8</b>:20-21; </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>20</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:12-19; <b>16</b>:9-15; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>33</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:2-4;
Psalm </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>66</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:17; <b>115</b>:5-7; Isaiah </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>6</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:5-6,
9-10; </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>11</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:4; <b>32</b>:3-4; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>30</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:27-28;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>33</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:15;
<b>35</b>:3-6; </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>37</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:29; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>45</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:23;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>58</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3;
<b>59</b>:3; </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>66</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:12; Habakkuk </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>3</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:16.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Body-part
language is part of the propensity for parallelism, double entry, as well as concrete
and expansive expression, which includes specificity of expression, as
illustrated in “Specificity for Vividness.”</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Brevity</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>What
departs norm grabs attention, which can give emphasis. Hebrew seems to use <u>expanded
expression</u> to emphasize (above norm) while English prizes brevity for that
purpose (under norm). Pleonastic style comes across in English as less interesting;
it is wordy, tautological, redundant, repetitious, something a written comp
prof marks in red. After this section, “Shortening Mechanisms” lists efforts <i>CV</i>
has made to adjust the idiom without losing the content.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>We
have not shortened or removed some set expressions: </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:1.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“Jeroboam
<i>that made Israel sin</i>” (1 Kings </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>15</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:26,
30, 34; </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>16</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:2, 19, 26; 2 Kings </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>3</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>9</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:9;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>10</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:29;
<b>13</b>:2, 6, 11, 23; <b>14</b>:16, 24; <b>15</b>:9, 18, 24, 28; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>17</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:21),
a standardized label that, along with Jeroboam</span><b><sup><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>I</span></sup></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>,
helps distinguish him from Jeroboam<b><sup>II</sup></b> (2 Kings </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>3</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:1,
6ff; <b>14</b>:23-24; <b>15</b>:8-9); </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>afraid
or intimidated </span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(Deuteronomy <b>1</b>:21; <b>31</b>:8;
Joshua <b>1</b>:9;<b> 8</b>:1; <b>10</b>:25; 1 Chronicles <b>22</b>:13; <b>28</b>:20;
2 Chronicles <b>20</b>:15, 17; <b>32</b>:7; Jeremiah <b>46</b>:27; Ezekiel <b>3</b>:8);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-indent:.25in'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>day <s>and night</s></span></i>
<span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(Exodus
<b>24</b>:18;<b> 34</b>:28; Deuteronomy <b>9</b>:9; <b>10</b>:10; 1 Samuel <b>30</b>:12;
1 Kings <b>19</b>:8; Job <b>2</b>:13) </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>strong
and courageous</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (Deuteronomy <b>31</b>:6, 7, 23; Joshua <b>1</b>:6,
18; <b>10</b>:25; 2 Samuel <b>10</b>:12; <b>13</b>:28; 1 Chronicles <b>19</b>:13;
<b>22</b>:13; <b>28</b>:20; 2 Chronicles <b>32</b>:7; Psalm <b>27</b>:14), and
others;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>afraid
or intimidated </span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(Deuteronomy </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>1</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:21;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>31</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:8;
Joshua </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>1</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:9; </span><b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>8</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:1;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>10</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:26;
1 Samuel</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> 17</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:11; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>20</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:23;
1 Chronicles </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>22</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:13; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>28</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:20;
2 Chronicles </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>20</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:17; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>32</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:17;
Jeremiah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>8</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:9; </span><b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>46</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:27;
Ezekiel </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>2</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:6; 3:9)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>eat
and drink</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>: By custom, English says “eat” to cover whatever goes
on at a meal (Judges <b>19</b>:21; Ruth </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>3</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3;
1 Samuel </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>1</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:9; 2 Samuel <b>19</b>:35; 1 Kings </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>18</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:41,
42; </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>19</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:5,
6, 8<sup>2</sup>; 2 Kings </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>6</span></b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:23; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>9</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:34;
Job <b>1</b>:4). In about a dozen cases, the double statement stays in <i>CV </i>because
drinking was perhaps a more prominent concern in arid countries or because food
and wine were more special than food and other drinks: Exodus <b>32</b>:6;
Deuteronomy <b>2</b>:6, 28; 2 Samuel <b>11</b>:11; 1 Kings <b>18</b>:41, 42;
Proverbs <b>23</b>:7; Ecclesiastes <b>2</b>:24; <b>22</b>:13; Jeremiah <b>22</b>:15;
Daniel <b>1</b>:10; Zechariah <b>7</b>:6<sup>2</sup>; (note Jeremiah <b>16</b>:7
as well.);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>food
and water</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> (Deuteronomy <b>2</b>:6, 28; <b>23</b>:4; Isaiah <b>3</b>:1);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Haman
enemy of the Jews</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (Esther </span><b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>8</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:1;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>9</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:25);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Mordecai
the Jew </span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>(Esther </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>5</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:13;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>6</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:10;</span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
9</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:29;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>10</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3);
</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>strong
hand and outstretched arm</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (Deuteronomy<b> 4</b>:15;<b> 5</b>:15;<b>
7</b>:19; <b>9</b>:29; <b>11</b>:2; <b>26</b>:8; 2 Chronicles <b>6</b>:32;
Psalm <b>136</b>:12; Jeremiah <b>21</b>:5; <b>32</b>:21; Ezekiel <b>33</b>:34;
cp. 2 Kings <b>17</b>:36);</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpFirst style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>bookends
structure</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>: Exodus<b> 6</b>:1-8; Numbers</span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
3</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:42;<b>
5</b>:11-15 (intro), 16-28 (body), 29-31 (end); Judges </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>11</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:32;
Psalm <b>8</b>:1 + 9 (<i>“L</i></span><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>,
our Lord, you’re so majestic all over the world</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>!”</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>)</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpLast style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>The
L</span></i><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> <s>our/your
God</s> </span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>is shortened in several texts where the longer
expression has recently occurred: Exodus </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>8</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:27;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>10</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:26;
Numbers </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>10</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:9; Deuteronomy</span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
2</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:7,
28, 33, 37; </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>4</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:34; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>12</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:29;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>16</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:5,
21; </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>20</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:4;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>24</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:19;
1 Samuel </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>12</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:14; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>14</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:26.</span></p>

style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Poetic
repetition</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> stands in antiphonal Psalm <b>136</b></span><b><span
style='font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> </span></b><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(</span></b><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“His
love is everlasting”</span></i><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>)</span></b><span style='font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> and </span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Psalm <b>103</b>:1-2 + 20-22; Psalm</span><span
style='font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> <b>135</b>:19-21</span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (<i>“bless
the L</i></span><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>”</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>).
</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Note
also 1 Samuel </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>1</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:11.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Discontinuities</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Texts
frequently do not maintain continuity of person, tense, number, or viewpoint,
especially in poetic materials. Instead of being a lapse in thinking, authors
made such changes perhaps to break up sameness by shifting perspectives. At any
rate, to an English reader they distract and confuse rather than increase interest.
Consequently, in shorter sections especially, <i>CV</i> has taken the liberty
of establishing a continuity of person, tense, or number. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>person</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:
Genesis </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>4</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:15; <b>18</b>:14, 17-19; <b>27</b>:25,
27; <b>28</b>:22; <b>49</b>:26; </span></p>

margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span style='font-size:

line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:23; </span><b><span
