<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> “Give
him over to his enemies,”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> in place of <i>“give him over to <u>the
desires of</u> his enemies”</i> </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(Psalm <b>41</b>:2);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.5pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> “Spoken
by the prophets,”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> rather than <i>“spoken by <u>the mouth of</u>
the prophets”</i>;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <i>“Go near <s>the
door of</s> her house”</i> (Proverbs</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> <b>5</b>:8);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“the
<s>time of</s> battle”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (Proverbs <b>21</b>:31 Leviticus <b>23</b>:43;
<b>24</b>:8; 2 Kings<b> 7</b>:6);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <i>“<s>the
sound of</s> melody”</i> (Isaiah<b> 51</b>:3);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <i>“the ones
that despise you will bow at <s>the soles of </s>your feet”</i> (Isaiah </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>60</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:14);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <i>“the <s>tents
of</s> destroyers prosper”</i> (Job </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>12</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:6);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <i>“(your
words) became a <s>joy and</s> delight to me”</i> (Jeremiah </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>15</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:16;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Write
it <i>“on <s>the tablet of</s> your heart”</i> (Jeremiah<b> 17</b>:1).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“thrown
out <s>of the gates</s> of Jerusalem”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (Jeremiah</span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> <b>22</b>:19);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:45.0pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:-45.0pt'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <i>“Obey
the Lord,”</i> in place of <i>“obey the <u>voice of</u> the Lord.”</i> <i> </i> </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:45.0pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:-45.0pt'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <i>“Toss
your bread on the <s>surface of the </s>water”</i> (Ecclesiastes <b>11</b>:1);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:45.0pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:-45.0pt'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            “The
L</span></i><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
watches over <s>the way of</s> the godly” </span></i><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(Psalm <b>1</b>:6<sup>2</sup>).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:45.0pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:-45.0pt'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Note
the parallelism in Hosea <b>13</b>:14:</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpFirst style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-63.0pt;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      “Should
I ransom him from <s>the power of</s> Sheol?</span></i></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpLast style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-63.0pt;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      “Should
I redeem him from death?</span></i></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:45.0pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:-45.0pt'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            “He’ll
eat the <s>meat of the</s> healthy ones and tear off their hoofs” </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(Zechariah
<b>11</b>:16)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:45.0pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:-45.0pt'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            “Put
your turbans <s>on your heads</s> and your shoes <s>on your feet</s>” becomes
“Put on your      turbans and your shoes.” </span></i><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(Ezekiel <b>24</b>:23).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>The
structure includes phraseology with the body-part imagery (see above):</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                  <i>“No
rest for <s>the soles of</s> your feet”</i> (Deuteronomy <b>28</b>:65; cp. 1
Kings<b> 5</b>:3, 4 redone in <i>CV</i>);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      <i>“I’ll
be with your mouth and his”</i> becomes <i>“I’ll be with you and him”</i>
(Exodus <b>4</b>:16).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      <i>“It’s
pleasant <s>for the eyes</s> to see the sunrise”</i> (Ecclesiastes </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>11</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:7).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:45.0pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:-45.0pt'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            See
also Psalm<b> 27</b>:12;<b> 55</b>:18; <b>63</b>:10; Jeremiah<b> 7</b>:34;
Ezekiel<b> 2</b>:8.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Partitive Reference</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            English
expects the hearer to recognize partitive reference in obvious cases:</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                  <i> “eat
<s>from</s> the fruit of the tree” </i>(Genesis</span><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> <b>3</b>:2);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                  <i>“eat
<s>from</s> my game”</i> (Esau to Isaac, Genesis <b>27</b>:19).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Relative Clause Formats </span></p>

<p class=MsoListParagraph style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:1.0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(a)<span
style='font:7.0pt "Times New Roman"'>&nbsp;&nbsp; </span></span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>The
redundant pronoun falls out:</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“the
land that you are about to possess <s>it</s>”</span></i><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (Deuteronomy <b>31</b>:13).
The Hebrew <i><u>’asher</u></i> (<i>that</i>, </span><span lang=HE dir=RTL
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>אשר</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span>) splices two clauses without occupying a grammatical slot in either
one—and so is not really a relative pronoun.</span></p>

<p class=MsoListParagraph style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>(b)<span style='font:7.0pt "Times New Roman"'>&nbsp; </span></span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>When
possessives appear before a clause and there is a duplicating pronoun inside
the clause, <i>CV</i> has replaced the possessive with a definite article:            </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:1.0in;text-align:justify'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“<s>your</s>/the
inheritance (that) you redeemed”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (Deuteronomy</span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> <b>9</b>:26);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“<s>my</s>/the
sacrifices (that) I’ve commanded”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (1 Samuel</span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> <b>2</b>:29);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                        <i>“<s>your/</s>the
people you have chosen”</i> (1 Kings <b>3</b>:8);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                        </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“<s>his</s>/the
strength he showed”</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (1 Kings <b>21</b>:45; 2 Kings <b>17</b>:15)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                        <i>“out
of <s>my</s>/the land I’ve given them”</i> (2 Chronicles<b> 7</b>:20);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:1.0in;text-align:justify'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“<s>your</s>/the wonders (that)
you did”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> (Nehemiah <b>9</b>:17); </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:1.0in;text-align:justify'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>‘<s>their</s>/the enemies
that want to kill them”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (Jeremiah </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>49</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:37);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“All
<s>their</s>/the abominations they committed”</span></i><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (Ezekiel<b> 33</b>:29);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                        <i>“all
<s>his</s>/the benefits (that) he’s given”</i> (Psalm <b>116</b>:12).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:40.5pt'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>See also Genesis <b>31</b>:18;
<b>32</b>:13; Numbers <b>14</b>:31?; <b>22</b>:30; 1 Samuel <b>10</b>:19?; 2
Kings <b>2</b>:13; 1 Chronicles<b> 10</b>:13; <b>16</b>:12; Ecclesiastes <b>9</b>:9;
Ezekiel </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>13</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:20; <b>16</b>:20; <b>18</b>:24; <b>35</b>:11;
<b>45</b>:9; Daniel <b>3</b>:17;<b> 6</b>:16;<b> 9</b>:10; <b>11</b>:4; Amos <b>5</b>:26(?);
Zechariah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>11</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:16.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Relative
Negatives </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Relative
negatives, also called limited negatives, use both the negative and positive
forms of a statement.</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> We have translated as </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>apparently
</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>relative
negatives what in form are direct opposites. A reader can tell from context and
the kind of action involved that the two elements are not real opposites, but real
alternatives for different degrees of the same thing.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Genesis<b> 29</b>:30:
<i>“</i>(Jacob) <i>loved (Rachel) more than Leah” </i>(lit. <i>“He loved Rachel
from Leah.”</i>); </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Genesis <b>45</b>:8:
<i>“It wasn’t so much you as God”</i>;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                        The
loved wife and the less-loved wife (?): Deuteronomy<b> 21</b>:15-17;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>2 Chronicles <b>19</b>:6:
<i>“You’re not just judging for people but for the L</i></span><i><span
style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
that’s with you”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>‘It’s
not </span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>so
much<i> for your sake that I’m about to act; it’s for my holy name that you’ve
profaned in the nations where you went’</i> (Ezekiel <b>36</b>:22).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                        Hosea<b>
6</b>:6: <i>“I want mercy and not sacrifice”</i> becomes </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                                         
<i>“I want mercy more than sacrifice”</i> (since God actually commanded </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                                                sacrifice);
cp. Jeremiah <b>7</b>:22-23; Ezekiel <b>36</b>:32.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:1.0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>CV</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
applies the relative negative idiom to the famous text of Jeremiah <b>7</b>:22-23,
where God says, </span><b><sup><span style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>22</span></sup></b><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“When
I brought your ancestors out of Egypt, I didn’t speak to them about burnt
offerings and sacrifices </span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif;color:#C00000'>so much as<i> </i></span><b><sup><span
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>23</span></sup></b><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>I
commanded them, ‘Obey me, and I’ll be your God, and you’ll be my people. Follow
the way I command you so things will go well for you.’” </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>God
obviously <i>“spoke to them about burnt offerings and sacrifices” </i>in the
same records that chronicle the exodus and wilderness peregrinations. A reader
would not expect Jeremiah to be denying that, given all the instructions in
Moses regarding that very thing, starting with the Passover lamb while the
Israelites were still in Egypt. The more basic point Yahveh wanted was
obedience; ritually offering sacrifices to obtain benefit apart from expressing
commitment to God and personal righteous living was not obeying in the spirit
of why God instituted them. The red-letter addition in <i>CV’s</i> wording above
nuances Jeremiah <b>7</b>:22-23 in that direction. As such, Jeremiah’s comment
about sacrifices echoes Isaiah’s comments about ritual fasting (<b>58</b>:3-6).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                  Note Exodus
<b>16</b>:8; 1 Samuel<b> 8</b>:7; Proverbs <b>21</b>:3; Ezekiel <b>32</b>:36.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:1.0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Cp.
in </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>CV</span></i><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> </span></b><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“loved
Jacob and hated Esau”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (Malachi <b>1</b>:2-3) as handled by Paul
in Romans <b>9</b>:13.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Streamlining
Causal Distinctions</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Hebrew
idiom can use the same wording for direct cause, indirect cause, conditional
cause, combinational cause, and occasional cause (permission). Translating that
idiom into English in a direct-cause format creates misleading impressions, to
say the least. In Hebrew, God is even said to do evil: <i>“all the evil the L</i></span><i><span
style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> <u>brought</u>
on Job”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> (Job <b>42</b>:11). The same book details how God <u>allowed</u>
Satan to do those evils to Job (<b>1</b>:6-2:7). What God allowed he is said to
have done: occasional cause in the form of efficient cause. So, in the case of
Job </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>42</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:11,
<i>CV</i> reads, <i>“</i></span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>They consoled him for the misfortunes
the L</span></i><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> <u>had let come</u> on
him.”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> Some unfortunate turns of events are said to be “<i>from
the L</i></span><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>,”</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
a wording we have recast in some places as <i>“the L</i></span><i><span
style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> <u>let</u>
x happen”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> (1 Kings </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>12</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:15;
cp. 1 Kings </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>12</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:24; Joshua </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>11</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:20;
Judges </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>2</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:14; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>14</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:14;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>21</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:15;
Zechariah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>11</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:6). Others like Judges </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>21</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:15
have been let stand as worded.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Restating
that idiom could be an aspect of second-level translation, but in many cases we
have left the first-level wording stand. Changing the wording from efficient
cause to occasional cause assumes the translator knows what the case was. Such verdicts
should often be left with readers—after being made aware that causal
streamlining is a feature of Hebrew idiom. Removing the original wording could
interfere with that possibility. In obvious cases, we have changed the wording
a from “did” to “let do” (Jeremiah <b>21</b>:4). Pharaoh’s house steward tells
Jacob’s sons how he put their money in their sacks: <i>“Your God . . . put your
treasures in your sacks”</i> (Genesis <b>41</b>:16). <i>“I’ll give you long life”</i>
(Exodus </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>23</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:26) comes into English <i>“and you
will have long lives.”</i> Note also Malachi </span><b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>1</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:9,
<i>etc.</i> The important point is the fact that causal streamlining happens in
Semitic idiom and affects interpretation <u>and</u> translation. The two
enterprises are intertwined, but some cases are more difficult to translate
because they are more difficult to interpret.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            In
that light, we have let the presentation stand about God’s bringing Babylon against
Judah in judgment. Yet once that judgment had taken place, God saw to it that
the Medes and Persians brought judgment on Babylon for that evil against God’s
people. The fact that God allowed it did not remove the fact that it was evil,
and that God would punish them for it even though it served his purpose (as
evil often does):</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpFirst style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-63.0pt;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            </span><b><sup><span
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>11<i>“</i></span></sup></b><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Sharpen
the arrows, fill the quills!</span></i></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-63.0pt;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            The
L</span></i><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
has stirred up the kings of the Medes</span></i></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-63.0pt;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                  to
destroy Babylon.</span></i></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpLast style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-63.0pt;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <b><i>It’s
the L</i></b></span><b><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i></b><b><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>’s
revenge for his Temple</span></i></b><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>”</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
(Jeremiah <b>51</b>:11).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Solving
that conundrum calls for understanding that God let the Babylonians do what the
Babylonians wanted to do anyway. He did so because it served his purpose of
disciplining and cleansing Judah; God did not incite the Babylonians to do it.
But the evil that Babylon was inclined to do in Israel and elsewhere in the
region was itself something subject to divine condemnation. The reader,
however, does not get that understanding from the immediate words or context,
but from near and distant contextual considerations, including the
understanding of Semitic causal idiom.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Translation
involves hermeneutics, but translating scripture should not interfere with the theological
enterprise that readers must be free to carry on. Not keeping in mind this
Semitic mode of expression can lead to conclusions that are not correct about
divine operation or worthy of divine character. That is the rationale for highlighting
the idiom in these notations.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Some
features of Hebrew idiom get covered up by “conversationalizing” the text. <i>CV</i>
takes away some of the charm of biblical presentation, especially in the poetic
portions. That character of the text has been retained in traditional translations,
which can be consulted as desired. The presentation here has structured and
worded the text to give some poetic flare: balanced construction for
parallelism, appropriate cadence for meter, alliteration for (rare) rhyme
scheme, and the use of pointed expression,</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Vocabulary</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            We
have tried to use familiar equivalencies for measures of length, weight, and volume.
Lengths, weights, and measures are somewhat approximate because ancient Middle East
units do not closely match modern ones. Relationships between the Hebrew
measurements are fairly exact, but finding ways to word the English equivalents
is more difficult. Various scholarly sources provide fairly different values. For
internal consistency in measurement of volume, <i>CV </i>follows G. F. Hill, <i>Dictionary
of the Bible</i> (Hendrickson Publishers, 1989; James Hasting, ed.), 967-71
even though it yields considerably larger amounts than several more recent
versions. The Hebrew terms often appear in the text in superscript so readers
can consult other reference works for comparison if exactness is desirable.
Such sources should include <i>BDB</i> (<i>A Hebrew and English Lexicon of the
Old Testament </i>by Brown, Driver, and Briggs (based on William Gesenius). </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Measures
of <u>length</u> are complicated by having royal and common variants. G. F.
Hill puts the royal cubit at 20.67 inches and the common one at 17.72, a 16%
difference. Ezekiel evidently uses the royal cubit (</span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>20</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:5);
so <i>CV</i> uses there the royal cubit and other measures of size. The cubit
in Solomon’s Temple may have been the royal variant as well. Nevertheless, for
simplicity’s sake, <i>CV</i> gives the cubit as 1½ feet aside from Ezekiel. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Before
the exile, talents, shekels, darics, and minas/minehs/manehs were <u>weight</u>s
rather than coins (cp. Jeremiah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>32</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:9-10;
Ezekiel </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>4</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:10, “shekels of food”). <i>CV </i>has
standardized these weights as 75½ lbs for a talent, .4 oz for a shekel, 185 lbs
for a daric, and 20 oz for a maneh (Exodus</span><b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 38</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:25-26).
See<i>, e.g</i>. <i>The Zondervan Pictorial Bible Dictionary</i>, 891-92.  </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Measurements
of <u>value</u> present an even more difficult problem because modern inflation
constantly alters the correlations, so the transliterated terms appear in the <i>CV</i>
text. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Genealogy,
deference language, arranged marriages, wanton executions, and other matters
foreign to modern western culture and taste lie outside the purview of
translation work. We have not always interfered with the wordings to “tone them
down” or sanitize them verbally. Those behaviors existed in those degrees, so
we have “allowed” the <i>CV </i>text to report them.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>On
deferential language, <i>CV</i> has reduced the amount of it (a) by dropping
its repetition (<i>“your servant”</i>: 1 Kings <b>3</b>:7-9; cp. 1 Samuel <b>1</b>:16,
18; <b>12</b>:19). It has altered it (b) by reducing the number of jussive
expressions (third-person imperatives) in second-person interchanges, because
they have less natural English formats: <i>“<u>Let him be</u> wet with dew from
the sky”</i> (Daniel <b>4</b>:23<sup>2</sup>). In most cases, <i>CV</i> has
struck a compromise (c) by toning down the deference custom to reduce the
foreign feel and moderate the distancing from today’s readership. It replaces
the noun or third-person pronoun in second-person circumstances. It does not
leave the literal rendering in adjacent superscript as it might have<i>: “Your</i>
</span><sup><span style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Pharaoh’s</span></sup><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> <i>dreams
both mean the same thing”</i> (Genesis <b>41</b>:25). The superscripted entry could
give access to the original sentiment by using the literal rendering—especially
in the public reading of scripture. Consequently, <i>The Conversational Version
</i>sacrifices a degree of verisimilitude to bring modern readers more
naturally within the horizon of the biblical content without changing its
objective meaning. <i>The Yahveh Version</i> retains quite a number of
deference expressions. That version is a variant of <i>The Conversational
Version </i>and uses the personal name of Israel’s God along with a couple of
other main features. See the “Introduction to the Yahveh Version” elsewhere on
this website.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Terminology
About Relatives</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            The
breakdown of Hebrew social division does not come into English cleanly. Ancient
and modern cultures structure society differently, so the terms in each do not correspond
well. Something of the Jewish social structure shows up when the L</span><span
style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
has Joshua conduct a search for who sinned at the conquest of Jericho.
According to </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>CV</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>, he had Joshua narrow
down the nation down from tribe to family to household to individual (Joshua</span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
7</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:14-18).
The New American Standard Bible has tribe, family, household, individual. </span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>The
New Living Translation</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> uses tribe, clan, family,
individual. Things are less clear in free-standing references, which include
elders, leaders, heads of tribes (cp. 1 Kings </span><b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>8</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:1-2).
Translation options become difficult when texts like Ezra </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>2</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:1-70
(cp Nehemiah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>7</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:7-73); </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>8</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:1-14,
and </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>10</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:18-44
speak of </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>sons-of</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> and </span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>men-of</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>.
</span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Descendants</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
for </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>sons
</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>sounds
too distant for the 70-100 years of captivity. </span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Family</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (and
</span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>house[hold]</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>)</span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>sounds
too small for the large numbers indicated, especially since by custom only men
and nuclear family heads were probably counted.  Besides, the lists consist of
persons and places, and which is which is often unclear. Some person or place
names may not appear elsewhere; if they do, their common identity is not
evident. So, do we say </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>residents of</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>,
</span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>people
from</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>,
</span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>sons
of, </span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>or
what? The accompanying numbers give the clearest clue. Nothing major hinges on
the choice of terms, but readers need to remember that the structure is
ambiguous because it does not correspond to modern western societal structure.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Variety
of Expression</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            With
some words that occur frequently, </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>CV </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>has
substituted several more specific terms to enhance interest by variation: </span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>say</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>,
</span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>tell</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>,
</span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ask</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>;</span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
men, servant, master,</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>lord, troops, soldiers,
warriors, advisers, officers, officials, etc.</span></i><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> See under </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Individual
Words </span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>toward the end of these introductory notations. </span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>CV</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
has chosen more specific terms based on context and custom.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Syntactical
Placement</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            English
tends to put time words (<i>today</i>, <i>tomorrow</i>, <i>yesterday</i>) after
their clause. In many cases, Hebrew puts them in initial position: <i>“Today,
if you hear him” </i>becomes<i> “If you hear him today”</i> (Psalm </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>95</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:7).
If English uses initial position, it carries more emphasis as a variant from
the usual sentence sequence.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Poetic
Portions</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <i>CV</i>
has kept something of the integrity of Semitic poetry by using separate lines
for <u>parallelism</u>, its most fundamental feature. That usually means not
melting synonymous parallels into one expression to gain economy of expression as
has happened in narrative sections. It also means tolerating more repetition in
poetry than elsewhere, which includes cases of synthetic parallelism:</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpFirst style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:.5in'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>“The L</span></i><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
will have a day of vengeance,</span></i></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:.5in'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>     a year of payback for what Edom did to Zion”</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
(Isaiah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>34</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:12; also </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>13</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:12;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>51</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:18
MT). </span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>            We have paid more attention to <u>cadence</u>
(<i>vs.</i> meter) than in non-poetic portions. Cadence serves as a loose substitute
for meter, which Hebrew poetry does not have anyway. Balanced construction can
assist in creating that poetic feel. </span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>            <u>Rhyme scheme</u> does not appear in
Hebrew poetry. The closest thing <i>CV</i> uses to salve that English
expectancy is some initial, medial, and terminal </span><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>alliterations with
same and similar sounds in nearby words: <i>“He <u>thw</u>arts <u>wh</u>at the <u>w</u>icked
<u>w</u>ant”</i> (Proverbs <b>10</b>:3). Rhyme scheme usually applies to the
ends of lines whereas alliteration occurs anywhere within the same line.</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>            Some attention to <u>formatting</u> and
layout helps achieve a poetic <i>vs.</i> narrative or didactic appearance. In
some sections of Proverbs and Ecclesiastes, that includes grouping lines via
indentation patterns to help trace topical continuity and distinction.</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>            The difference between Hebrew poetry and
narrative or didactic content is not as obvious as what English speakers
associate with poetry <i>vs.</i> prose. Readers can verify that point by noting
that translations frequently differ in what they present as poetry—by parallel
lines rather than continuous text. Non-poetic presentation can approach free
verse.</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.5pt;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:0in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>Coordinating <i>vs.</i> subordinating constructions;</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>Paratactic <i>vs.</i> hypotactic formatting</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>            In comparison to Hebrew, English tends
more to subordinate one clause to another (hypotactic) instead of making them
grammatically coordinate (paratactic). <i>CV</i> sometimes replaces a parallel
format with a subordinate one. For one independent clause, the subordinating
format can use an adverbial introductory clause introduced by a subordinate
conjunction: </span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>            <i>“If you listen to what I’m telling you,
you’ll be my people, and I’ll be your God” </i>(Jeremiah <b>11</b>:4). The
original more literally reads, <i>“<u>Listen</u> to my voice and do-them
according to all that I command you, and <u>you will be</u> to me for a people,
and <u>I will be</u> to you for God.”</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>            </span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Esther <b>6</b>:14 reads,
<i>“The king’s eunuchs <u>arrived and took</u> him to Esther’s banquet.” CV</i>
rewords the sentence to say, <i>“The king’s eunuchs <u>arrived to take</u> him
to Esther’s banquet.”</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.5pt;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:0in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Personalizing
Proverbs</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>            Many Hebrew proverbs come in imperative
form, which personalizes them in second person. But in other places, <i>CV</i>
has changed Hebrew third-person proverbs from that more abstract form into
second and sometimes first-person declarative transforms: Proverbs </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>10</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:9,
17, 22, 27, 29; </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>11</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:4; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>12</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:5,
11; </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>13</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3,
11, 20, 24; </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>14</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:2, 6, 11, 27; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>15</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:4,
11, 13, 17, 22, 31, 32; </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>18</span></b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:8; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>20</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:25;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>21</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:2,
5, 16; </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>24</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:24; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>25</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:7;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>26</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:13;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>27</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:9,
18. Using second person reference also circumvents the problem of inclusive
language.</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>Rhetorical Questions</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>Speakers sometimes make claims in question form in a
way unexpected by English custom. When Saul was trying to locate David, some
men came up from Ziph and said to Saul, “<i>Isn’t David</i></span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> hiding
among us in the strongholds at Horesh on the Hill of Hachilah south of Jeshimon.
Come down and do whatever you want to. We’ll surrender him to you” </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(2
Samuel <b>23</b>:19-20).<i> </i>The wording is a form of deference language.
See also Deuteronomy<b> 11</b>:30; 1 Samuel <b>10</b>:1; <b>24</b>:12; <b>26</b>:1;
2 Samuel <b>11</b>:3, 21; <b>13</b>:28; <b>15</b>:23<sup>2</sup>, 27; <b>19</b>:13;
1 Kings <b>12</b>:16; 2 Kings <b>5</b>:26; 2 Chronicles <b>32</b>:11; Psalm <b>54</b>
heading; Isaiah <b>29</b>:17. In these case <i>CV</i> has shifted the words
into declarative sentences.</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>In 2 Samuel <b>18</b>:13, <i>CV</i> has made a
question out of a statement.</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:.5in'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>CV </span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>exhibits a preference for
statements in place of questions: Genesis <b>34</b>:23; Proverbs <b>8</b>:1;
Ezekiel <b>17</b>:9, 10; Amos <b>6</b>:13.</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.5pt;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:0in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> </span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>Gentilics/Eponyms</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>            The Hebrew Old Testament employs gentilics
more frequently than English does. <i>CV </i>has moderated their appearance and
replaced some <i>ite</i>,<i> ian</i>, -<i>i</i> (Israel<u>i</u>, Pakistan<u>i</u>)
words with “John of/from y,” “the people of/from y,” or “descendants of x.” Sometimes
<i>Israel </i>substitutes for <i>Israelites </i>(modern<i> Israelis</i>), for
example.<i> </i>The use of such forms takes fewer words than “people of/from x”
and serves as a reason for retaining a majority of them. </span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>            Most of the biblical gentilics are straightforward
and familiar, but sometimes avoiding the eponym can clarify whether the word designates:
</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>            a place of origin: Zorah (Zorathites, 1
Chronicles</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> 4</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:2), </span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>            an ancestor: Abiezer (Abiezrites, Judges </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>6</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:11-12;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>8</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:32,
32), Rechab (Rechabites, Jeremiah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>35</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:2),</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>            or even a practitioner: Nazirites, people
who took certain vows. </span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>            At other times the form of the eponym can
obscure the proper name itself: Ahohite &lt; Ahoah, Archevites &lt; Erech, Arkites
&lt; Arka, Avvites &lt; Ivvah, Benjamites &lt; Benjamin, Gittite &lt; Gath, Gazites
&lt; Gaza, Gibeathite &lt; Gibeah, Hittite &lt; Heth, Hushathite &lt; Hushah, Kenezite
&lt; Kenaz, Manassites &lt; Manasseh, Netophathite &lt; Netophah, Shilonite
&lt; Shiloh, Suchathites/Sucathites &lt; Soco/Sucah, Tekoite &lt; Tekoa,
Tyrians &lt; Tyre. Representative passages are Genesis</span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 23</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3,
18; </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>38</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:12;
Numbers </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>10</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:29; Judges </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>10</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>11</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:2;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>16</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:2;
1 Samuel </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>16</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:1, 18; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>18</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:19;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>23</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:19;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>26</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:1;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>27</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3<sup>2</sup>;
2 Samuel </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>3</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:2, 3; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>4</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3;</span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
6</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:10;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>15</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:19;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>17</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:27;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>18</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:2;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>20</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:26;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>21</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:1,
2, 8; </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>23</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:24-29; 1 Kings </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>1</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>11</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:1;
Isaiah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>21</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:13; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>37</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:12;
Jeremiah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>9</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:26; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>25</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:21;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>27</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>40</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:14;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>41</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:10,
15; </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>49</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:2;
Amos </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>1</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:13;
Zephaniah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>2</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:9. See also the listing under <i>Israelites
</i>in the </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>Individual Words</span></b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> section below. </span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.5pt;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:0in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Deference
Language Reduced</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>            From the standpoint of today’s English
idiom and culture, the deference language in the Hebrew scriptures seems
extreme. To reduce the heavy feel of such recurring expressions, </span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>            (a) <i>CV</i> has put the deference
language in superscript beside less extreme wording. </span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>            (b) “My lord” has frequently changed to
“sir” when possible (Genesis </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>18</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>23</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:6;</span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 42</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:10;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>43</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:20;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>47</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:25;
Judges</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> 6</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:13; Ruth </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>2</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:13;
1 Samuel </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>1</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:15, 26; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>25</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:24;
2 Samuel </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>14</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:9; 1 Kings </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>3</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:17,
26; 2 Kings </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>8</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:12; Daniel </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>4</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:19;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>10</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:16;
Zechariah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>1</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:9). The frequent “my lord the king”
still stands.</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpMiddle style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;
text-indent:0in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>            (c) The use of third person reference has
typically been changed to second person in deference accounts: 1 Kings </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>1</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:19,
25.  </span></p>

<p class=MsoListBulletCxSpLast style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
