for <i>“Adam”</i> in the margin. <i>CV </i>has taken the author’s intension
more broadly, “</span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Have I covered up my sins like <u>people</u>
do by hiding my iniquity in my heart?”</span></i></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> The
Assyrian (and Babylonian) names/posts in 2 Kings <b>18</b>, Isaiah<b> 20</b>,
and Jeremiah <b>39</b> we do not understand well, so capitalization is a moot
point with some. In 2 Kings <b>18</b>:17, <i>CV </i>has treated <i>tartan</i>, <i>rabsaris</i>,
and <i>rabshakeh </i>(plus <i>rabmag) </i>as Assyrian military designations
instead of proper names and so does not capitalize them. They appear in the
text respectively as <i>commander-in-chief </i></span><i><sup><span
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>tartan</span></sup></i><span
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>,<i>
</i></span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>field
commander</span></i><i><span style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> <sup>rabsaris</sup></span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>,<i>
and chief officer </i></span><i><sup><span style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>rabshakeh</span></sup></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>.</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
The last designation appears also in 2 Kings <b>18</b>:19, 26, 28, 37; <b>19</b>:4,
8 and in Isaiah<b> 36</b>:2, 4, 11, 22;<b> 37</b>:4, 8, (9, he). The word <i>tartan</i>,
a military post, also appears in Isaiah <b>20</b>:1;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                        <i>rabsaris
</i>appears as well in Jeremiah <b>39</b>:3a;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                        <i>rabsaris
</i>appears as <i>field commander</i> in Jeremiah <b>39</b>:3 and 13;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                        <i>rabmag
</i>is a military post in Jeremiah <b>39</b>:3;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                        <i>Nergal-sherezer</i>
appears as a military position in Jeremiah <b>39</b>:3b and 13;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                        <i>Shamgar-nebo</i>
is entered as a name in Jeremiah<b> 39</b>:3;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                        <i>sarsekim
</i>is taken as a descriptor, the king’s advisor, in Jeremiah <b>39</b>:3.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Punctuation and Accidence
Considerations</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            The
original Hebrew Old Testament text was consonantal (<i>abjad</i>); that is, it
had “consonants only.” In the (sixth to) eleventh century A.D., the Jewish Masoretes
added “points” to indicate vowel sounds and diacritical markings to show how to
pronounce and phrase the text at a time when Hebrew was becoming less well-known
in the Jewish masses. Many diacritical marks are conjunctive and disjunctive symbols
that show how words are to be “joined to” or “disjoined from” other words. Their
effect is something akin to punctuation, but they do not match up very well
with English mechanics. Besides, they are not part of the original inspired/canonical
text. So, we have added a few notations about how that affects our punctuation
choices, because they largely come at a translator’s discretion.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>no
comma</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>; no way to distinguish restrictive from
non-restrictive clause modifiers grammatically: Exodus</span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> <b>4</b>:26</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>no
dash</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>;
supplied for asides, for long entries before the main part of a sentence, and
for pendens constructions: Deuteronomy <b>30</b>:1-3; Judges <b>17</b>:1-2; 1
Samuel <b>30</b>:31; 2 Samuel<b> 2</b>:9; <b>11</b>:21; Isaiah </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>33</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:15-16</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBullet style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>no
exclamation mark</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>; In one place we have supplied a double
exclamation mark to indicate a prophet’s insincere directive to attack: </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“<i>Micaiah</i>
answered him, <i>‘Go right on up there! The L</i></span><i><span
style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>’s
going to deliver it to you!!’”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (1 Kings <b>22</b>:15).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      <u>no parenthesis</u>; supplied
for apparent textual asides, </span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>the original author’s explanatory
comments, or perhaps later redactors’ additions.    </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      <u> no brackets</u>; supplied in
<i>CV</i> for a few translator’s additions like the <i>[El-Shaddai]</i>
entries: Genesis <b>17</b>:1; <b>28</b>:3; <b>35</b>:11: <b>43</b>:14; <b>48</b>:3;
Exodus<b> 6</b>:3*.</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> In a few cases, we have added our own
brackets of explanations within the text: Ecclesiastes <b>12</b>:3-4. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>no
quotation marks</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>: a reader can tell from sentence flow
where direct discourse begins, but where it closes can be unclear: Genesis <b>26</b>:28-29;
Exodus<b> 3</b>:15; Job <b>10</b>:2 or 18 or 19 or 22? Isaiah </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>40</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:7-8?;
Jeremiah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>5</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:13a or b? Habakkuk</span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
2</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3
or 8?   </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.75in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>In 2 Samuel <b>7</b>,
does the direct imbedded quotation starting at <b>7</b>:8 stop at 11a and pick
back up at 12-16, or does it continue through 16? If the quotation goes 8-16,
then 11b is an example of discontinuity of person, a frequent occurrence in Old
Testament texts. But without quotation marks, the structure does not come
through clearly. Note also 2 Samuel </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>14</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:17;
Isaiah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>42</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:4 or 7, or 9?; Jeremiah </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>20</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:7-10
or 7-12? Ezekiel </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>6</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:11a or 12c?</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>no
bold-face print</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>; supplied to indicate emphasis, draw
attention to recurring elements</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>no
underlining</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>: supplied for emphasizing or drawing
attention to connections in the text. Verbal equivalency of underlining happens
in Deuteronomy <b>9</b>:4; Isaiah <b>52</b>:12 (<i>“I even I”</i>); the <i>“as
for me”</i> expression can be handled by underlining <i><u>I</u></i>.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>           Underlining draws
attention to lists or aids in proper reading for <u>clarity</u> by showing
where contrast lies. Underlining for clarity also draws attention to a list
spread across a context. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>             <u>Word
division</u> does not often create a problem in the Old Testament because,
unlike first-century Greek used in the New Testament, Hebrew spaced between
words. In Proverbs <b>30</b>:1b the Masoretic text can be divided to read <i>“I’m
weary, God; I’m weary and worn out, God”</i> or <i>“This man declared to
Ithiel, to Ithiel and to Ucal.”</i>  </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
 style='text-decoration:none'>&nbsp;</span></span></u></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Hebrew’s Verb System</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Hebrew
verbs have two “tenses,” completed and uncompleted action. They roughly
correspond to past and future, but finer distinctions within “past” and “future”
must be inferred from context and experience rather than from verb form, and must
be expressed according to English custom of usage. Two or three finer
distinctions may make sense. (Participles cover present tense or timeless
action.) These “tenses” do not <u>mean</u> all the possible nuances within each
category in each instance; they “cover for” situations where such variants could
be present without being expressible by variant verb forms. English speakers
looking at the perfect and imperfect occurrences want to specialize the usages
into what English custom wants to say in those circumstances. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Perfect
tense</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> covers for the following:</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      simple past
(went<i>): “In the beginning God created earth and sky”</i> (Genesis<b> 1</b>:1);
</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      characteristic
past (would go): <i>“He<u>’d go </u>to Gibeon to sacrifice”</i> (1 King <b>3</b>:4);
</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      progressive
past (was going): <i>“Pharaoh dreamed he <u>was standing</u> by the Nile”</i>
(Genesis <b>41</b>:1; cp. 2 Chronicles <b>18</b>:11; <b>23</b>:13; Daniel <b>6</b>:14?);
</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      ingressive/inchoative
perfect (began to go): <i>“Hagar <u>started disrespecting</u> her mistress”</i>
(Genesis <b>16:</b>4, 5; cp. </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>41</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:2;
<b>42</b>:1; Joshua <b>5</b>:11; Judges </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>10</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:8;
<b>15</b>:19; 2 Chronicles <b>32</b>:23; Ecclesiastes <b>1</b>:17; Joshua <b>5</b>:11;
Daniel <b>6</b>:10; <b>8</b>:15); </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      used to (go):
<i>“We <u>used to eat</u> free in Egypt”</i> (Numbers<b> 11</b>:5); </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      kept (­­­going):
<i>“He <u>kept urging</u> Esau”</i> (Genesis <b>33</b>:11); <i>“Absalom kept
urging David”</i> (2 Samuel <b>13</b>:27);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      emphatic
past (did go): <i>“We really <u>did come</u> down to buy food”</i> (Genesis <b>43</b>:26);
<i>“You <u>do</u> see trouble and distress”</i> (Psalm </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>10</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:14);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      present
perfect (have gone): <i>“My brothers . . . <u>have arrived</u>”</i> (Genesis <b>46</b>:31);
</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      stative
perfect (had them x): Elijah . . <i>. “had the Baal prophets killed”</i> (1
Kings </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>18</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:40);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      past perfect/pluperfect
(had gone): <i>“which Abraham <u>had bought</u> from Ephron”</i> (Genesis <b>50</b>:13);
cp. Exodus </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>11</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:9; 2 Kings <b>9</b>:14, 29; Isaiah </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>38:</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>21,
22</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      conative
past (tried to go): <i>“You’re <u>trying to destroy</u> a city”</i> (2 Samuel<b>
20</b>:19); cp. Genesis </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>37</span></b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:35; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>39</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:19;
Exodus </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>2</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:15; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>8</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:18;
Deuteronomy </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>13</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:10; 1 Samuel</span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
17</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:39;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>19</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:10;
2 Samuel</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> 21</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:5; 1 Kings </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>11</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:40;
Ezra </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>4</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:4;
Nehemiah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>4</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:5; Esther </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>2</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:21;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>3</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:6;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>6</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:2;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>9</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:2;
Job </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>31</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:5;
Psalm </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>40</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:5; 54:3; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>73</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:16;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>78</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:36;
Psalm </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>119</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:42; Proverbs </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>10</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:24;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>12</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:21;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>23</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:4;
Ecclesiastes </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>2</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3; Isaiah</span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
22</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3;
Jeremiah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>2</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3; <b>6</b>:14?; </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>26</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:21;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>51</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:9;
Daniel </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>6</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:4, 14; Micah</span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
5</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:6;
 Zechariah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>12</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3; Malachi <b>2</b>:12?);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      prophetic
perfect, where a future event is treated as so certain that it is worded as
having happened: Genesis <b>15</b>:19; <b>35</b>:12; Numbers <b>21</b>:34; Deuteronomy<b>
3</b>:2; Joshua <b>1</b>:3; <b>2</b>:9; <b>6</b>:2; <b>5</b>:16; <b>8</b>:1; <b>10</b>:19;
Judges<b> 1</b>:2; <b>3</b>:28;<b> 4</b>:14; <b>7</b>:9, 14, 15; <b>18</b>:10;
1 Samuel <b>14</b>:10, 12; <b>23</b>:4; 2 Samuel <b>5</b>:24?; 1 Kings </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>3</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:12,
13; Isaiah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>15</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:1-4; <b>34</b>:2; Ezekiel <b>32</b>:20.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      Adjustments
like those above can color the text in perhaps unintended ways. 1 Samuel </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>1</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3
has David saying to the man that reported Saul’s death, <i>“Where have you come
from?” </i>The same question in Job</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 1</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:7;
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>2</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:2,
<i>CV</i> renders, <i>“Where’d <u>you</u> come from?” </i>So worded, it implies
Satan did not particularly belong there. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Imperfect
tense</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> covers for the following:</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      simple
future (will go), <i>“Many women . . . <u>will carry out</u> judgments on you” </i>(Ezekiel
<b>16:</b>41); progressive future (will be going), <i>“Everyone is accursed
that does not continue to obey everything”</i> (Deuteronomy </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>27</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:26,
imperfect hiphil);  </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      future
perfect (will have gone); <i>“God <u>will have gone</u> out ahead of you”</i> (1
Chronicles <b>14</b>:15; see also Zephania</span><b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 3</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:11);</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      conative
future (will try to go): <i>“Any prophet will die that <u>tries to speak</u> in
my name”</i> (Deuteronomy <b>18</b>:20; Psalm </span><b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>4</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:2;
Zechariah </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>13</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:3); </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      emphatic
future (will go):<i> “Whatever I say <u>will</u> happen soon” </i>(Ezekiel <b>12</b>:28?);
</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      tendential
future: <i>“The fears of the wicked tend to be fulfilled”</i> (Proverbs</span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
10</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:24;
cp. </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>12</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:21).
Many proverbs could have the “tend to” auxiliary added by virtue of the nature
of what a proverb is: a generality or tendency to be remembered instead of a
prediction or uniform promise.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                  imperatival
future (the Ten Commandments; frequent, as in Genesis <b>17</b>:10, 12; <b>21</b>:30;
2 Chronicles <b>19</b>:9)</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>. Everyday English rarely uses it now. If
it does, it carries emphasis—as in the example. <i>“You <u>will</u> stay home
this evening, Johnnie”</i> gets restated with the future command form <i>“going
to __”</i>: <i>“You are too going to stay home this evening.”</i> <i>CV</i> typically
renders as command forms what are apparently imperatival futures.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>No Vowels </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Vowel
points and <i>matres lectionis</i> were added to the original consonantal
Hebrew text. The first set indicates vowel sounds; the second set was an early use
of some consonants to indicate a few of the vowel sounds (</span><span lang=HE
dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>א
ו י ה</span><span dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span dir=LTR></span>). That lack of
pointing famously comes up in discussing the meaning of Genesis <b>1</b>:1-2.
Does it read, <i>“In the beginning, God created earth and sky, and the earth
was . . .</i> ” (cp. LXX)? Or does it mean, <i>“In the beginning of God’s
creating earth and sky, (that) the earth was . . . ”</i> (cp. TEV, 1976).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Having
no written Hebrew vowels may explain variant spellings of some names: Joshua’s
father (<i>Nun</i> in Joshua </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>1</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:1,
<i>etc.</i>, but <i>Non</i> in 1 Chronicles<b> 7</b>:27); <i>Hiram</i> (1 Kings
<b>5</b>:18) and <i>Huram</i> (2 Chronicles <b>2</b>:2) as well as </span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Malcom/Milcom</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
(Jeremiah <b>49</b>:1, 3; 1 Kings<b> 11</b>:8), Addan/Addon (Nehemiah <b>7</b>:61;
Ezra <b>2</b>:59), and a host of other examples.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Smaller
Biblical Hebrew Vocabulary </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>A
smaller vocabulary calls for some words to cover a wider range of meaning than the
closest English word: <i>river-stream-creek-canal-wadi (intermittent flow),
prophesy/prophecy-predict-exhort-speak-for-another(-act in a vigorous manner?)</i>,
<i>fear-respect</i>, <i>heal-[general remedy physically, politically,
ceremonially, morally],</i> <i>father-ancestor/originator</i>, <i>God/god-[powerful
ones-leaders-judges]</i>, <i>son-descendant</i>-[<i>one like something</i>]-[<i>one-derived
from something</i>]. <i>Spirit/spirit-wind</i>, <i>ground-dirt-region-world,
ocean-sea-lake-pond-lagoon</i>—to name some prominent ones. Hebrew compensates
for its smaller vocabulary by circumlocutions, as do all languages when they
lack a precise term for a precise point. All languages suffice for the
communication needs within their culture, so there is no implication here that one
language is better than another—as if English were better than Hebrew. In the
case of the Old Testament, the hearer-reader must rely on context and the
nature of the case to express its intended meaning in English. The original
readers would have done the same thing in interpretation as we have to do in
translation.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Textual
Variants </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Many
textual differences exist between the standardized Masoretic Hebrew text and
the Septuagint Greek text (LXX), the Syriac, the Samaritan Pentateuch, and
individual manuscripts of various books. A few especially significant variants
are the following:</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Psalm
</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>40</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:6:
<i>“You pierced/opened my ears”</i> (MT) <i>vs.</i> <i>“You gave <u>me</u> a
body”</i> (LXX); note Hebrews </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>10</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:5
&lt; LXX.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Also
note in 2 Chronicles</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 25</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:28: <i>CV </i>has <i>David</i>
instead of <i>Judah</i> to agree with the more likely reading in 2 Kings </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>14</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:20,
<i>“The City of </i>David<i>.”</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Omission
of the Linking Verb</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“Your
throne (is) God”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> or <i>“Your throne, O God,”</i> said of
the Son according to Hebrew </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>1</span></b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:8 &lt; Psalm </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>45</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:6
(</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>45</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:7
in the Hebrew text); hence, an affirmation of the deity of the Son in the New
Testament, while the original Hebrew is technically ambiguous because its verb
is unexpressed. (Vocative and predicate nominative form would be the same.) Cp.
in contrast the force of </span><b><span lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>היה</span></b><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span> in Exodus </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>3</span></b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:14 </span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>vs.</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
its lack in Exodus</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 3</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:6 </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>and
how that point plays out in Jesus’ argument from </span><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>3</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:6
against the Sadducees (Matthew</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 22</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:31-33;
Mark </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>12</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:26-27;
Luke </span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>20</span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:37-39:
“is” of identity <i>vs.</i> time).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Elative
for Superlative</span></p>

<p class=MsoListBullet style='margin:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>In
Jeremiah</span><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> 25</span></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:32, <i>CV</i> has worded
the text as elative rather than superlative because the Babylonian empire,
though distant, was not the “most distant” area to the north, as the text is often
translated: </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>a great storm is brewing from the very
remote parts of the earth.</span></i></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal align=center style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:center'><b><span
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Mechanics
in Written English</span></b></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Abbreviations</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>measurements
(without a period and sometimes without a space before the unit: ft, in; see
Exodus <b>25</b>:10, 23; <b>26</b>:13; Ezekiel <b>40-48</b>, <i>etc.</i>)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Bold
Type</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>book
titles (all caps)     </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>listings
</span></u></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>the plagues in
Egypt: Exodus <b>6</b>:8-<b>11</b>:10</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                        David’s
mighty men: 2 Samuel <b>23</b>:8-39</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:1.0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>the judges during the judgeship
period: Judges <b>3</b>:9 (Othniel), 15 (Ehud), 31 (Shamgar); <b>4</b>:4
(Deborah), 6 (Barak); <b>6</b>:11 (Gideon), <b>10</b>:1 (Tola), 3 (Jair); <b>11</b>:1
(Jephthah); <b>12</b>:1 (Ibzan), 11 (Elon), 13 (Abdon); <b>13</b>:24 (Samson);
1 Samuel <b>3</b>:19 (Samuel).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>dated
prophesies</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> and major chronological notations within narratives</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:1.0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Genesis <b>7</b>:11, 17; <b>8</b>:3,
4, 5, 6, 10, 12, 13, 14; Exodus <b>12</b>:41; <b>16</b>:1; <b>19</b>:1; <b>40</b>:1;
Numbers <b>9</b>:1; <b>10</b>:11; <b>33</b>:38; Deuteronomy <b>1</b>:3; 1 Kings
<b>6</b>:1, 37, 38; <b>14</b>:25; <b>15</b>:1, 9, 25, 33; <b>16</b>:8, 10, 15,
29; <b>22</b>:2, 41, 51; 2 Kings <b>1</b>:17; <b>3</b>:1;<b> 8</b>:16, 25; <b>9</b>:29;
<b>11</b>:4; <b>12</b>:1, 6; <b>13</b>:1, 10; <b>14</b>:1, 23; <b>15</b>:1, 8,
13, 23, 27, 32; <b>16</b>:1; <b>17</b>:1, 6; <b>18</b>:1, 9, 10, 13; <b>24</b>:12;
<b>25</b>:1, 2, 3, 8, 25, 27; 2 Chronicles <b>3</b>:2; <b>7</b>:10; <b>12</b>:1;
<b>13</b>:1; <b>15</b>:10; <b>16</b>:1, 12; <b>17</b>:7; <b>29</b>:3, 17; <b>34</b>:3,
8; <b><u>36</u></b><u>:22 = Ezra <b>1</b>:1</u>; <b>3</b>:3, 6, 8; <b>4</b>:23;
<b>5</b>:13; <b>6</b>:3, 15, 19; <b>7</b>:1, 9; <b>10</b>:0, 16, 17; Nehemiah <b>1</b>:1,
2; <b>6</b>:15; <b>8</b>:1, 14; <b>9</b>:1; <b>13</b>:6; Esther <b>1</b>:1; <b>2</b>:16;
<b>3</b>:7, 12, 13; <b>9</b>:1, 14, 17, 21; Isaiah <b>7</b>:1; <b>14</b>:28; <b>36</b>:1;
Jeremiah <b>1</b>:2-3; <b>25</b>:1, 3; <b>27</b>:1; <b>28</b>:1; <b>32</b>:1; <b>34</b>:1,
8; <b>35</b>:1; <b>36</b>:1, 9; <b>39</b>:1, 2; <b>41</b>:1; <b>45</b>:1; <b>46</b>:2;
<b>51</b>:59; <b>52</b>:4, 6, 12, 31; Ezekiel <b>1</b>:1-2; <b>8</b>:1; <b>20</b>:1;
<b>24</b>:1; <b>26</b>:1; <b>29</b>:1, 17; <b>30</b>:20; <b>31</b>:1; <b>32</b>:1,
17; <b>33</b>:21; Daniel <b>1</b>:1, 21; <b>2</b>:1; <b>7</b>:1; <b>8</b>:1; <b>9</b>:1;
<b>10</b>:1; Hosea <b>1</b>:1; Amos <b>1</b>:1; Micah <b>1</b>:1; Zephaniah <b>1</b>:1;
Haggai <b>1</b>:1, 15;<b> 2</b>:1, 18, 20; Zechariah <b>1</b>:1, 7; <b>7</b>:1</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:1.0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>genealogical
entries</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> (to highlight or connect with a recurrent name;
indentation maps extensive genealogical listing to help the eye trace through
the listing). Genealogical interest lay in its being the basis for inheriting land
and possessions, the offices of priest and king, tracing strictures on
descendants of certain ancestors like an illegitimate son: a Moabite or
Ammonite could not enter the L</span><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>’s assembly for
ten generations (Deuteronomy <b>23</b>:2-3), and an Edomite for three
(Deuteronomy <b>23</b>:8; Amalekite as well). Citizenship was conferred
automatically by lineage unless deliberate action took place to the contrary (<i>“The
older will serve the younger” </i>in the case of Jacob and Esau).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:1.0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>headings
sometimes</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>: added topical labels supplied to aid navigating
through complex material</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      <u>chapter
numbers</u> (enlarged); </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>verse
numbers</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> (superscripted), </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:1.0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>repeated
content</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> in nearby entries: Exodus <b>29</b>:13-34 repeats;
Numbers <b>24</b>:3-4 = 15-16; Deuteronomy <b>27</b>:15-26 repeats; 1 Kings <b>19</b>:10
=14; <b>36</b>:22-23 = Ezra <b>1</b>:1-3; Job <b>1</b>:1 = 8 = <b>2</b>:3;
Psalm <b>8</b>:1 = 9 bookends; <b>18</b>:20 = 24; ; <b>24</b>:7-8a = 9-10a; <b>42</b>:3,
5 = 10b-11 and 43:5; <b>46</b>:7 = 11; <b>62</b>:2 = 6; 67:3 = 5; <b>80</b>:3 =
7 = 19; <b>103</b>:1a = 2a; 20a = 21a = 22a;<b> 104</b>:1 = 35b bookends; <b>104</b>:35
= <b>105</b>:45 = <b>106</b>:1 = 48 bookends [Hallelujahs]; <b>107</b>:6, 8 =
13, 15 = 19, 21 = 21 =28; <b>111</b>:1 = <b>112</b>:1 = <b>113</b>:1 = 9 = <b>115</b>:9
= 10 = 11; <b>111</b>:4 = <b>112</b>:3 = 9 = <b>115</b>:18 = <b>116</b>:18 = <b>117</b>:2;
<b>115</b>:9 = 10 = 11 =<b> 118</b>:1 = 2 = 3 = 4; <b>118</b>:1b, 2b, 3b, 4b =
20b; <b>119</b>:154b = 156b = 159b; <b>135</b>:1 = 21 bookends [Hallelujah]; <b>118</b>:10
= 11 = 12 = 29; <b>119</b>:154 = 156 = 159; <b>135</b>:135; <b>135</b>:19<sup>2
</sup>= 20<sup>2 </sup>= 21; <b>136 </b>(antiphonal 1 per verse); <b>146</b>:10
= <b>147</b>:1 = 20 = <b>148</b>:1 = 14 = <b>149</b>:1= 10 = <b>150</b>:1 = 6
bookends; Song of Solomon <b>4</b>:9 = 10 = 12 = <b>5</b>:1 = 2 (”my sister, my
bride”); Isaiah <b>2</b>:10 = 19 = 21; <b>5</b>:25 = <b>9</b>:12 = 17 = 21 = <b>10</b>:4;
<b>18</b>:2 = 7; <b>28</b>:10 = 13; <b>44</b>:6 = <b>45</b>:5 = 6 = 14 = 18 +
21</span><b><sup><span style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>2</span></sup></b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> = 22 = <b>46</b>:9;
Jeremiah <b>6</b>:12-15 = <b>8</b>:10-12; <b>49</b>:19-20 = <b>50</b>:44-45; <b>51</b>:11b
= 12b; Ezekiel <b>18</b>:9 = 17; <b>19</b>:3 = 6; 24:6 = 9; Daniel <b>4</b>:15-17
= 24, 25-26 = 32; Amos <b>1</b>:3-4 = 6-7 = 9-10 = 11-12 = 13-14 = <b>2</b>:1-2
= 3-5; <b>4</b>:6b = 8b = 9b = 10b =11b; <b>7</b>:2-3 = 5-6. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:1.0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                        </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:1.0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            What
appear to be bookends or markers are often left in place as stylistic literary
form: Genesis <b>17</b>:19 + 21; <b>24</b>:12 + 14 (“master”); Numbers <b>1</b>:2-16;
2 Chronicles<b> 33</b>:2 + 9 bookends;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify'><u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Southern Kingdom</span></u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
rulers after the Northern Kingdom begins  </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:1.0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Northern
Kingdom rulers have underlining. The narratives move back and forth between the
two kingdoms, and some names occur in both king lists. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:1.0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>types
of offerings</span></u><span style='font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>: </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Leviticus<b>
2</b>:1;<b> 3</b>:1;<b> 4</b>:3</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:1.0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><span style='font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Brackets</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      <u>omitted
verses</u>: [</span><b><sup><span style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>#</span></sup></b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>] (or <i>[</i></span><b><i><sup><span
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>#</span></sup></i></b><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>]
</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>in
direct discourse)<i> </i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      </span></i><u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>translator’s
explanatory additions</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> as in name meanings not supplied in the
original:</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                  Genesis
<b>35</b>:11: God Almighty [<i>El-shaddai</i>]</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                  Exodus
<b>17</b>:15: The L</span><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> Is My Banner [<i>Yahveh-nissi</i>]</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                  Jeremiah
<b>32</b>:18: The L</span><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> of Hosts [<i>Yahveh-Tsivaot</i>]</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                  Jeremiah
<b>33</b>:16: The L</span><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> Is Our Goodness [<i>Yahveh-Tsidekenu</i>]</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                  Ezekiel
<b>48</b>:35: The </span><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>LORD</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> Is There [<i>Yahveh-shammah</i>]</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Capitalization (American
rather than British spelling when they differ)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>L</span></u><u><span
style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
in all capitals covers for God’s personal name, the Tetragrammaton (</span><span
lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>יהוה</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span>, vocalized something like “Yahveh” in modern Hebrew
pronunciation)</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>. The name not to be used as a byword became the
ineffable name at least a couple hundred years before the Christian Era. The
Hebrew vowel points for <u>Lord</u> replace it in the <i>Qere</i>, the reading
actually said out loud. In cases where the Hebrew word for “Lord” (</span><span
lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>אדני</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span>) happens to appear in front of the Tetragrammaton in the <i>Kitib</i>
(what is written) the <i>Qere</i> becomes “Lord G</span><span style='font-size:
9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>OD</span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>.”
In written form, the vowel points for “God” (</span><b><span lang=HE dir=RTL
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>אֱלֹהִים</span></b><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span>) are attached to the Tetragrammaton to remind the reader to say
<i>“Lord G</i></span><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>OD</span></i><i><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>.”</span></i><span
dir=RTL></span><span dir=RTL style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span dir=RTL></span> </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>That
avoids saying in Hebrew <i>“the Lord L</i></span><i><span style='font-size:
9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>.”</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
We have followed English convention in writing L</span><span style='font-size:
9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
(caps) for the more usual circumstance and <i>“Lord G</i></span><i><span
style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>OD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>”
</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(capital
and lower capital + all caps) for the second. The vowel points of </span><b><span
lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>אֱלֹהִים</span></b><span
dir=LTR></span><span lang=HE style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span dir=LTR></span> </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>applied
to the Tetragrammaton </span><span lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>יהוה</span><span dir=LTR></span><span
lang=HE style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span> </span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>lie behind the blended “non-word” <i>Jehovah
</i>that has become popular over the years in certain circumstances. That term
does not appear in <i>CV. The Yahveh Covenant</i> is a form of the Old
Testament that reproduces the Old Testament part of <i>The Conversational
Version</i>, but allows the personal name of God stand in the text (found also on
the <i>christir.org </i>website).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Nouns
referring to deity are not capitalized</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> unless they are used as proper
nouns: not for “the creator,” “the angel of the L</span><span style='font-size:
9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>”
(despite accompanying deific descriptors in some passages).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Nouns
understood in the New Testament as referring to the Messiah</span></u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
and then taken as deific references are not necessarily capitalized in the Old
Testament text cited (where they still look forward without fulfillment to
clarify: <i>associate</i> (Zechariah <b>13</b>:7); <i>branch</i> (Isaiah <b>4</b>:2;
<b>11</b>:1; <b>60</b>:21?; Jeremiah <b>23</b>:5; <b>33</b>:15), <u>King</u>
(Psalm <b>2</b>:6); <u>Ruler</u> (Micah <b>5</b>:2); <i>servant</i> (<b>52</b>:13;<b>
53</b>:11); <u>shepherd</u> (Ezekiel <b>34</b>:23-24; Zechariah <b>13</b>:7; );
<u>son</u> (Psalm <b>2</b>:7, 12); <u>stone</u> (Psalm <b>118</b>:22); <i>cornerstone</i>
(Psalm <b>118</b>:22; Isaiah <b>28</b>:16); <i>Messiah/Anointed One</i> (Psalm <b>2</b>:2);
<i>Messiah</i> (capitalized in Daniel <b>9</b>:25-26 as an obvious proper
name); capitalized in reference to God in Psalm <b>80</b>:1; Ecclesiastes <b>12</b>:11).
Those terms have also not usually been capitalized in the New Testament part of
<i>The Conversational Version</i>.   </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Pronouns
that refer to the Judaeo-Christian deity are not capitalized</span></u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>,
as per contemporary English practice. Previous custom often capitalized even
indefinite, relative, indefinite relative, intensive, and reflexive pronouns
along with personal pronouns. But we <u>have</u> capitalized the indefinite
pronoun <i>One </i>for clarity’s sake.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      <u>place
designations</u> taken as proper nouns. See list under “Hebrew Ambiguities”
above.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      <u>spirit</u>
capitalized in reference to deity; otherwise, lower case. The choice represents
a translator’s judgment call. See <i>supra</i> under <b>Hebrew Ambiguities</b>
and <i>spirit</i> below under <b>Individual Words</b>.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Colon</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      </span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>used
before long direct quotations or in phraseology meaning “that is.”</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Comma</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      <u>for
non-restrictive clauses</u>. Restrictive clauses are not set off with commas in
modern English. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      used
ahead of shorter direct discourse</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>used
ahead of appositional <i>or </i>(Ezekiel <b>45</b>:14)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>used
the serial comma/Oxford comma</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>, that is, a comma
between the last two items in a list with or without <i>and</i>: Malachi <b>3</b>:5.
<i>CV</i></span><i> </i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>often omits <i>and</i> between the last
two entries of lists; the effect implies the representative rather than
complete nature of the listing.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:22.5pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:13.5pt'><u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>reduced
usage around appositives</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>, especially if class precedes
particular: <i>his father Kish</i> <i>vs.</i> <i>Kish, his father</i>. When
class precedes particular, it has the feel of an adjective, and so does not
seem to require a comma to separate the two. Like class, adjectives are
typically broader than the particulars they modify. Most times in the format “x
son-of y” <i>CV</i> has not added the comma of apposition because of the custom
of using <i>son-of</i> as if it were a double name. David son of Jesse in the
Yahveh adaptation, for example, becomes David Ben-Jesse. Cp Simon Bar-Jonah
(John <b>1</b>:42). A similar construction appears with “x of y”: Gibeah of
Saul (1 Samuel <b>11</b>:4).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:22.5pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:13.5pt'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>not
used <u>between clauses</u> if the subjects are the same—unless the clauses are
long or have internal <i>and’s</i> that blur the grouping of the words
(Leviticus <b>7</b>:34). Conversely, we have used commas between clauses with
different subjects unless they are especially short.         </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Contractions<b>
(</b>see supra<b> Changes in Modern English</b>)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><b><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></b></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Dashes (reduced usage to
eliminate several pendens constructions) </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Genesis
<b>2</b>:19, <i>etc.</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      used
with an aposiopesis in Genesis <b>3</b>:22 </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      left in several other places: Genesis
<b>3</b>:22; <b>24</b>:14; Leviticus <b>24</b>:4; Numbers <b>2</b>:1-31; 2
Samuel <b>21</b>:5; Proverbs <b>6</b>:10; Isaiah <b>56</b>:4-5; Amos <b>5</b>:8</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Ellipses:
used in Genesis <b>3</b>:22; <b>27</b>:33; and Jeremiah <b>44</b>:26; Amos <b>6</b>:10</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Exclamation
Mark</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      supplied
at translator’s discretion </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Hyphens</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>The
Conversational Version</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> hyphenates all <i>En-</i> [“fountain/spring”]
names (except for Enaim/Enam): </span><span style='font-size:10.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>En-harod (Judges </span><b><span
style='font-size:10.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>7</span></b><span
style='font-size:10.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:1),
En-dor, En-eglaim, En-gannim, En-gedi, En-haddah, En-hakkore, En-hazor, En-mishpat,
En-rimmon, En-rogel (<i>vs.</i> Enrogel), En-shemesh, and En-tappuah.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <i>CV</i>
hyphenates all the <i>Beth-</i> names [“house of”] (except for Bethel and
Bethlehem by convention), especially because they otherwise get rather long: </span><span
style='font-size:10.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Beth-abara,
Beth-anath, Beth-anoth, Beth-arabah, Beth-aram/Beth-haram, Beth-arbel, Beth-aven,
Beth-azmaveth, Beth-baalmeon, Beth-ashbea (1 Chronicles </span><b><span
style='font-size:10.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>4</span></b><span
style='font-size:10.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:21),
Beth-barah, Beth-birei, Beth-car, Beth-dagon, Beth-diblathaim, Beth-eden<b> </b>(Amos
<b>1</b>:5), Beth-eked, Beth-emek, Beth-ezel, Beth-gader, Beth-gamul, Beth-gilgal,
Beth-haccerem (Nehemiah <b>3</b>:14), Beth-haran, Beth-hoglah, Beth-horon, Beth-leaphrah,
Beth-lebaoth, Beth-lejeshimoth/Beth-jesimoth, Beth-maachah, Beth-marcaboth,
Beth-meon, Beth-millo, Beth-nimrah, Beth-palet/Beth-phelet, Beth-pazzez, Beth-peor,
Beth-rapha, Beth-rehob, Beth-shan/Beth-shean, Beth-shemesh, Beth-shitta, Beth-tappua/Beth-tappuah,
Beth-togarmah,</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> </span><span style='font-size:10.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Beth-zur, </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            The
other hyphenated names have also been allowed to stand even if they have
familiar lead-ins: <i>Baal-</i>, <i>Beer-,</i> <i>Ben-</i> (except for Benjamin
by convention), <i>El-</i> (<i>“god of”</i>), <i>Lo-</i> (“not), <i>Ir-</i>
(“city of”), <i>Ish-</i> (<i>“man/husband of”</i>), <i>Kir-</i>, <i>Kiriath-</i>.
The part after the hyphen has lower case. The formidable list of conventionally
hyphenated place and personal names is as follows:</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            </span><b><span
style='font-size:10.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Abel-beth-maacaah</span></b><span
style='font-size:10.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>,
Abel-keramin, Abel-maim, Abel-meholah, Abi-albon, Adami-nekeb, Adoni-bezek, Aram-maacah,
Aram-naharaim, Aram-zobah, Ataroth-addar, Atroth-beth-joab, Aznoth-tabor, <b>Baal-berith</b>,
Baal-hazor, Baal-meon, Baal-gal, Baal-hermon, Baal-peor, Baal-perazim,
Baal-shalishah, Baal-tamar, Baal-zebub, Balale-judah, Baaleth-beer,
Bamoth-baal, Bath-rabbim, Beer-elim, Be-eshterah, Bene-berak, Bor-ashan, Ben-hail,
<b>Caleb-ephratah,</b> Chephar-ammoni, Col-hozeh, Cushan-rishathaim, <b>Dan-jaan</b>,
<b>Ebed-melech</b>, Eglath-shelishiyah, Emek-keziz, Ephes-dammim, Esar-haddon,
Eth-kazin, Evil-merodach, Ezion-geber, <b>Gath-hepher</b>, Gath-rimmon,
Ge-harashim, Gibeah-elohim, Gur-baal, <b>Hadah-rimmon</b>, Hamath-zobah, Hammoth-dor,
Hamon-gog, Harosheth-hagoyim, Hash-baddanah, Havvoth-jair, Hazar-enon, Hazar-shual,
Hazar-susah, Hazar-susim, Hazazon-tamar, Helkath-hazzurim, Huram-abi, <b>Ishbe-benob,</b>
Ish-bosheth, <b>Jaare-oregim</b>, Jabesh-gilead, Jashubi-lehem, Josheb-basshbeth,
Jushad-hesed, <b>Kadesh-barnea,</b> Keren-happuch, Kir-hareseth, Kir-heres, Kiriath-arba,
Kiriath-baal, Kiriath-sannah, Kiriath-sepher, <b>Lebo-hamath</b>, Lev-kamai,
Lo-ammi, Lo-debar, Lo-ruhamah, <b>Mahaneh-dan</b>, Mahar-shalal-hash-baz,
Malchi-shua, Me-jarkon, Meribath-kadesh, Merib-baal, Merodach-baladan,
Migdal-el, Misrehoth-maim, Muth-labben, <b>Naphath-dor</b>, Nathan-melech, Nergal-sar-ezer,
<b>Obed-edom</b>, <b>Pahath-moab</b>, Perez-uzzah, Pi-beseth,
Pochereth-hazzebaim, <b>Rab-mag</b>, Rab-osaris, Ramoth-gilead, Ramathaim-zophin,
Ramath-mizpeh, Regem-melech, Romanti-ezer, <b>Sar-sekim</b>, Sha-alabbin,
Shamgar-nebu, Shear-jashub, Shethar-bozenai, Shihor-libnath, Shimron-meron,
Succoth-benoth, <b>Taanath-shiloh</b>, Tel-abib, Tel-harsha, Tel-melah,
Tiglath-peleser, Tiglath-pilneser, Timnath-heres, Timnath-serah, <b>Uzzen-sheerah</b>,
<b>Zereth-Shahar</b>, </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Italics</span></p>
