<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      </span><u><span
lang=FR style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>direct
discourse</span></u><span lang=FR style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>, including quotation marks </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>added
words</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> for clarification when not in the Hebrew text; (non-italicized
if added in italicized material). We could not be entirely consistent in this
practice because the conversational wording significantly departs the
word-for-word Hebrew original.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      Titles of resources
cited by Old Testament writers </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                  a.   <i>The
Book of the Wars of the Lord</i> (Numbers <b>21</b>:14)</span></p>

margin-left:1.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-1.0in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            b.   <i>The
Book of Jashur</i> (Joshua <b>10</b>:13; 2 Samuel<b> 1</b>:18; and possibly in
the Septuagint at 1 Kings <b>8</b>:53)</span></p>

"Times New Roman",serif'><span dir=LTR></span>) of Solomon</span></i><span

(1 Kings <b>11</b>:41)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;

style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>v.   <i>The
Book of Chronicles </i></span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(</span></i><span lang=HE dir=RTL
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ספר
דברי הימים</span><span dir=LTR></span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span dir=LTR></span>) </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(</span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Nehemiah<b>
12</b>:23; Esther <b>2</b>:23; <b>6</b>:2)</span><span dir=RTL></span><span
dir=RTL style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=RTL></span> </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                  w.  <i>The
Chronicles of the Kings of Media</i> <i>and Persia </i>(</span><span lang=HE
dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ספר
דברי הימים למלכי מדיס</span><span dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span dir=LTR></span>;</span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                                   </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Esther
<b>10</b>:2; cp. <b>9</b>:32?)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                  x.   <i>The
Law of the L</i></span><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (2 Chronicles <b>35</b>:27)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span dir=RTL></span><span dir=RTL style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span dir=RTL></span> </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Tune
names in the introductions of 21 Psalms scattered across Psalms <b>8</b>-<b>88</b>:<b>
8</b>, <b>9 </b>(also <b>9</b>:16), <b>12</b>, <b>22</b>, <b>39</b>, <b>45</b>,
<b>46</b>,<b> 53</b>,<b> 56</b>, <b>57</b>,<b> 58</b>,<b> 59</b>,<b> 62</b>,<b>
69</b>,<b> 75</b>,<b> 77</b>,<b> 80</b>,<b> 81</b>,<b> 84</b>,<b> 88</b>, and 1
Chronicles <b>15</b>:21; Habakkuk<b> 3</b>:1</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>In
some cases, <i>CV</i> takes the liberty of putting an italicized name in place
of an at-first ambiguous pronoun reference (as with proleptic pronouns—pronouns
given before the noun they replace).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>For
variant forms of some names, we have entered both side by side with a slash
between them and italicized the one not in the current text: Joram/<i>Jehoram</i>
(2 Kings <b>8</b>:23); <i>Joram</i>/Jehoram (2 Chronicles <b>22</b>:5-11), <i>etc.</i>
</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>For
clarification to avoid contradiction with materials elsewhere: <i>“visit </i>the
consequences of<i> parents’ sins on their descendants” </i>(Exodus <b>20</b>:5;
<b>34</b>:7; Numbers <b>14</b>:18; Deuteronomy <b>5</b>:9) to avoid
contradiction with the dictum that guilt and punishment are personal to the
individual that does the sin (Deuteronomy<b> 24</b>:16; 2 Kings <b>14</b>:6; 2
Chronicles <b>25</b>:4; Jeremiah <b>31</b>:30; Ezekiel <b>18</b>:4, 17-20).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>words
used as words</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Page Layout</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Direct
discourse of three lines</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> or more is indented from both
margins (Genesis<b> 7</b>:1-4). </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Indentation</span></u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
patterns at various levels assist the eye in seeing relationships in <u>genealogies</u>.
</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            The
patterns of indentations in <u>poetic portions</u> reflect the translator’s
judgment about content relationships: Moses’ Song of Triumph (Exodus <b>15</b>:1-18),
The Song of Deborah (Judges <b>5</b>); Hannah’s “Prayer of Thanksgiving” (1
Samuel <b>2</b>:1-10); David’s “Psalm of Praise” (2 Samuel <b>22</b>:2-51); <i>“David’s
Last Words”</i> (2 Samuel<b> 23</b>:107); the Psalms, Proverbs, Song of
Solomon, extensive portions of the prophets. Whether a portion is poetry is
itself something of a judgment call. Hebrew poetry has conceptual parallelism
but not rhyme scheme or meter, which makes poetry less distinct from prose. Parallelism
can occur in more prosaic portions that by custom of usage use double entry of
various sorts. It makes spontaneous poetry a conceivable possibility: Note
Hannah’s <i>“Song of Thanksgiving”</i> (1 Samuel <b>2</b>:1-10), which is
comparable to Mary’s <i>“The Magnificat”</i> (Luke <b>1</b>:46-55), Zacharius’ <i>“Benedictus”</i>
(Luke<b> 1</b>:68-79), and others like Simeon’s <i>“Nunc dimittis” </i>(Luke<b>
2</b>:29-32).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Line
changes</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> sometimes suffice for speaker changes in dialogues
without using introductory remarks for the second speaker (1 Samuel <b>14</b>:6,
7; 2 Kings <b>1-2</b>; 2 Chronicles <b>18</b>:4-27). Oral public reading needs
to use voice inflection and pausing to clarify the change. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Empty
lines</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> for major breaks in the text are translation choices.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Empty
lines appear before and after written documents</span></u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> reproduced
in the text: 2 Kings <b>10</b>:1-4, 6; 2 Chronicles<b> 2</b>:11-17; <b>21</b>:12-15;
<b>30</b>:6-9, 17; <b>36</b>:22-23 = Ezra <b>1</b>:1-4;<b> 4</b>:11-16, 17-22; <b>5</b>:6-17;<b>
6</b>:2-12; <b>7</b>:11-26; Nehemiah <b>6</b>:6-7</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>paragraphing</span></u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
instead of separate beginnings for each verse. Breaking down the text into
paragraphs does not necessarily reproduce the underlying Hebrew textual layout.
Keeping sets of verses in paragraph form better retains conceptual connections.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Versification</span></u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> follows
English conventions. <u>Psalm headings</u> are part of the Hebrew text verse
numbering. Some Hebrew sections differ from English conventional text divisions:
Exodus <b>7</b>:26-29 (Hebrew text equals <b>8</b>:1-4 English with <b>8</b>:5-32;
English numbering adjusted accordingly); Malachi<b> 4</b>:1-6 is <b>3</b>:19-24
in the Hebrew text. <b>other examples </b>LXX versification and sectioning are
quite different in Jeremiah because whole blocks of content are re-sequenced (as
if they were from another copy (of Barack’s writing from Jeremiah’s dictation?).
Whole Psalm numberings are off as well in the LXX beginning with Psalm <b>10</b>
(LXX combines Hebrew Psalm <b>9</b> and <b>10</b>) and supplies an additional
hymn at the end of Psalms to make the standard 150.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Numbers</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Cardinal
Arabic numbers</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> appear for weights, measures, and
population figures for quicker recognition. <i>CV </i>uses them more prolifically
than style manuals call for, especially in contexts with lots of other numbers.
We have even started a number of full sentences with Arabic numbers: Genesis <b>46</b>:26,
27; <b>47</b>:9; Exodus<b> 25</b>:33; Numbers <b>25</b>:9; <b>35</b>:14; Joshua
<b>20</b>:44, 46; Judges <b>20</b>:34, 44, 46; 1 Kings <b>9</b>:23; <b>10</b>:20;
2 Kings <b>2</b>:7: <b>15</b>:25; <b>18</b>; 2 Chronicles <b>9</b>:19; Isaiah <b>55</b>:1;
Ezekiel <b>40</b>:22, <i>etc.</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoQuote style='text-indent:.5in'><u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif;font-style:normal'>Ordinal
Arabic numbers</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif;font-style:normal'> occur only twice:
Genesis<b> 7</b>:11 (the flood’s coming in the <b>600<sup>th</sup></b> year of
Noah’s life); Genesis <b>8</b>:13; 1 Kings <b>6</b>:1 (the beginning of
constructing Solomon’s Temple in the <b>480<sup>th</sup></b> year after the
exodus).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Parenthesis</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(Ben)Hinnom
because different text references use the fuller or shorter form of the same
place name; explanatory comments in the text either as asides or added by later
copyists or redactors</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>For
eye aids in genealogical strings in narrative portions (with smaller print): Numbers
<b>27</b>:1; Joshua <b>7</b>:1, 18; <b>17</b>:3; Ezra<b> 7</b>:1-5; Zechariah <b>9</b>:1</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Pronouns</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>CV</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
references <u>place names</u> by <i>it </i>rather than <i>he</i> or <i>she </i>in
most cases. Many Old Testament place names are patronymics, that is, they come
from the name of a patriarch. That calls for masculine references to them in
Hebrew. Often English, too, must use a masculine pronoun to keep from “jarring”
the wording in the context. We have changed such examples to <i>it</i> whenever
feasible.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Impersonal
referents</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> have <i>it</i>. Since Hebrew does not have neuter grammatical
gender, Hebrew pronouns and gender-specific verb endings (second and third
persons) are masculine or feminine for purposes of grammatical connection.
English has virtually lost grammatical gender, which leaves sex reference only,
and that has disappeared except for third person singular personal, intensive,
and reflexive pronouns. As noted earlier, wisdom is not referred to in English
with <i>she</i> as a translation of the main Hebrew words for it, which are
feminine (Proverbs <b>1</b>:20).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Pronunciation<b>
</b>not Provided</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Most
name pronunciation comes from English custom of usage, which derives from
patterns of accenting together with vowel length as represented by English
lettering. That differs from the accenting and letter values in the original.
Besides, custom of English usage varies over time and from place to place as
illustrated by the older <i>Daríus</i> <i>vs.</i> the contemporary <i>Dárius</i>
as a frequent name among American athletes.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></b></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Punctuation
(see individual punctuation marks)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      <i>The
Conversational Version </i>uses American rather than British punctuation where
they differ.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Modern
usage appears instead of older English style, where most clauses were set off
in commas whether restrictive or not. Greater use of commas occurred with
appositives in earlier English usage. By eliminating commas, if possible,<i> CV</i>
tries to avoid complicating the appearance of sentences. That means omitting
them when class precedes particular in appositives and with short introductory
prepositional phrases.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Quotation
Marks</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Hebrew does not
have that mark. Frequently the text uses words to indicate the <u>beginning</u>
of direct discourse, especially the participle “saying” (</span><span lang=HE
dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>לאמר</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span>).</span><span dir=RTL></span><span dir=RTL style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span dir=RTL></span>
</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>The
<u>ends</u> of quotations are not obvious in a few cases: Genesis<b> 22</b>:20-24
or 20-22</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:58.5pt;text-indent:-22.5pt'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>double quote marks around
direct quotations (with italics); for embedded within embedded quotations as
well (<i>“‘</i> . . . <i>’”</i>)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      single
quote marks around imbedded direct quotations (with italics)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:58.5pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:-22.5pt'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>main
<u>explanatory words for name choices</u> (single quotes if explanatory words
appear in a section of direct discourse): Genesis <b>2</b>:7, 23; <b>3</b>:20;<b>
4</b>:25; <b>5</b>:29; <b>16</b>:11, 13;<b> 17</b>:5; <b>21</b>:6; <b>22</b>:14;
<b>25</b>:30; <b>26</b>:22; <b>27</b>:36; <b>28</b>:22; <b>29</b>:32, 33, 34,
35; <b>30</b>:6, 8, 10, 11, 13, 18, 20, 24; 31:48-51; <b>32</b>:2, 28, 30; <b>38</b>:29;<b>
41</b>:51, 52;<b> 50</b>:11; Joshua <b>22</b>:34; 1 Samuel <b>1</b>:20; <b>4</b>:21;
<b>7</b>:12; <b>25</b>:65; 2 Chronicles <b>20</b>:26, and elsewhere very often.
</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Semi-Colon</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>used <u>more often
in poetic formats</u> to separate lines of Hebrew parallelism. <i>And</i> is
also removed from the front of the second line of synonymous parallelism in
many cases.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>reduced usage in <u>non-poetic
material</u> as per modern avoidance except in formal writing: Ezekiel <b>22</b>:27a,
27b </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>retained <u>in
listings</u> for marking off one unit that has comma subdivisions when the
whole unit is parallel with other ones that are subdivided or not</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Slash/Forward
Diagonal </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>to separate
alternate forms of the same name. The one in the text at that point has regular
lettering; the other has italics: Joash/<i>Jehoash</i>, <i>Joram</i>/Jehoram.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Underlining</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      to
highlight lists spanning across sections</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:1.0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Pharaoh’s
graduated concessions to Israel’s request to leave: Exodus <b>8</b>:25, 28; <b>9</b>:28;<b> 
10</b>:7, 11, 24; <b>21</b>:31</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:1.0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>items
Bezalel made for the tabernacle: Exodus <b>36</b>:8, 14, 20, 31, 35; <b>37</b>:1,
6, 10, 17, 25; <b>38</b>:1, 8, 9, 18; Daniel<b> 7</b>:4-7</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:1.0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>factors
associated with sacrifices: Leviticus <b>1-9</b></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:1.0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>dietary
laws: Leviticus <b>11</b></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-4.5pt'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>       to
help track names in genealogical listings (along with bold-face entries and
indenting): Genesis <b>10</b>:22-30, <i>e.g</i>.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>rulers of the
Northern Kingdom in 1 &amp; 2 Kings, 1 &amp; 2 Chronicles</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            </span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>a
few recurrent expressions: <i>I’m the L</i></span><i><span style='font-size:
9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
(Leviticus <b>18-26</b>, and elsewhere)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            translator-supplied
to help clarify suggested emphasis in reading the text</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      to replace Hebrew emphasis
formula “as for x”; changed to “<u>you</u>,” <i>etc.</i> “<u>You</u> will pass
on to your ancestors” (Genesis <b>15</b>:15)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Spelling</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      <u>American</u>
instead of British spelling of common words where they differ</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Contractions</span></u><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> are
almost always used when possible. To add dignity to the Decalogue, contractions
do not appear in Exodus <b>20</b> and Deuteronomy <b>5</b>. Contractions are a
major factor in supporting the conversational feel. See <i>supra</i> under
“Conversational Style.”</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Hebrew
name spelling</span></u><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> is by American convention rather than phonetic
spelling, letter equivalencies as offered in Hebrew grammars for
transliteration purposes, or even current English spelling sometimes used by
Jewish writers (as in <i>v</i> for non-dagesh-lene Hebrew</span><span lang=HE
dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ב</span><span
lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
</span><span dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span dir=LTR></span> as well</span><span
dir=RTL></span><span dir=RTL style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span dir=RTL></span> </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>as</span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> </span><span
lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ו</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span>; </span><span lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ב</span><span dir=LTR></span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span> is a bilabial voiced fricative; <span dir=RTL></span><span
dir=RTL><span dir=RTL></span> </span></span><span lang=HE dir=RTL
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ו</span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>is
a labiodental voiced fricative)</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>. As with other contemporary English
versions, we have not appealed to Greek spellings from the Septuagint (LXX) or
New Testament Greek. That creates another set of problems because the Greek
alphabet does not match very well the Hebrew phonetic system or the English
one. Note the confusion raised in the King James Versions that tried to use the
Greek spelling of Hebrew names cited in the New Testament. They did not
correspond well with their Old Testament antecedents (Osee for Hosea and <u>–as</u>
endings for Hebrew names ending in <u>–ah</u> because Greek does not have a written
<u>h</u> sound except in initial position (and prepositional consonant-ending prefixes
to rough-breathing roots): Elias/Elijah, Esaias/Isaiah, Jonas/Jonah,
Jeremias/Jeremiah, Judas/Judah, <i>etc.</i>). In Acts <b>7</b>:45 and Hebrews <b>4</b>:8
the Authorized Version reads <i>Jesus</i> because the Greek spelling of <i>Joshua</i>
is <i>Iesous</i>: <i>“If Joshua had given them rest.”</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      Several
Hebrew letters do not have close equivalents in the American alphabet:</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> </span><span
lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>א</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span> (like the <u>h</u> in <u>honor</u></span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>,
but not usually written in English words, where the sound occurs variantly on
the front of words beginning with vowels when deliberately pronounced—<u>apple</u>;<span
dir=RTL></span><span dir=RTL><span dir=RTL></span> </span></span><span dir=LTR></span><span
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span>’</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> represents </span><span lang=HE dir=RTL
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>א</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span lang=HE style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span dir=LTR></span> </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>in
transliteration practice</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>); </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ח</span><span dir=LTR></span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span> (conventionally Anglicized with <i>ch</i> for a velar
fricative) and pronounced <u>k</u> in all anglicized English Old Testament
names except <u>Rachel</u>),</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>    
 </span><span dir=RTL></span><span dir=RTL style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span dir=RTL></span> </span><span
lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>י</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span> (lies behind Hebrew names typically written with English <i>j</i>
despite its having an English initial <i>y</i> sound),</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>    
 </span><span lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ט</span><span dir=LTR></span><span
lang=HE style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span> </span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(retroflexed <i>t</i>), </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ע</span><span dir=LTR></span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span>  (laryngeal fricative, <i>‘</i> represents </span><span
lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ע</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span lang=HE style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span dir=LTR></span> </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>in
formal transliteration),</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>   
 </span><span lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ס</span><span dir=LTR></span><span
lang=HE style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span> </span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(retroflexed <i>s</i> but covered in
English transliteration simply by <i>s</i> as with the Hebrew</span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> </span><span
lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>שׂ</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span lang=HE style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span dir=LTR></span> </span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>vs.</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> </span><span
lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>שׁ</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span>). Modern Hebrew pronunciation makes stops out</span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> of
the fricatives</span><span lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ב</span><span lang=HE dir=RTL
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> </span><span
dir=LTR></span><span lang=HE style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span dir=LTR></span> </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(b/bh),
<span dir=RTL></span><span dir=RTL><span dir=RTL></span> </span></span><span
lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ג</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span>(g/gh), <span dir=RTL></span><span dir=RTL><span dir=RTL></span> </span></span><span
lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ד</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span>(d/dh), <span dir=RTL></span><span dir=RTL><span dir=RTL></span> </span></span><span
lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>כ</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span>(k/kh), <span dir=RTL></span><span dir=RTL><span dir=RTL></span> </span></span><span
lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>פ</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span> (p/ph), <span dir=RTL></span><span dir=RTL><span dir=RTL></span> </span></span><span
lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ת</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span>(t/th) with or without <u>dagesh lene</u> or <u>dagesh forte</u>,
which were dots inside the letter to indicate the stop or double-stop value. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ו</span><span dir=LTR></span><span
lang=HE style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span> </span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>has become <i>v</i> instead of <i>w</i>.<span
dir=RTL></span><span dir=RTL><span dir=RTL></span> </span>Among the vowels <i>patah</i>
and <i>qamets</i> are pronounced alike.<span dir=RTL></span><span dir=RTL><span
dir=RTL></span> </span>Then there is the variance between Ashkenazic and Sephardic
pronunciation with <i>sheva</i> prominence, <i>etc.</i> <i>CV</i> has used <i>Yahveh</i>
as the spelling for God’s personal name while keeping <i>Yahwism.</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>So,
it is complicated, but we have followed convention and streamlined name
spelling for practical purposes. (The Hebrew consonants in sequence appear in
the section headings of the acrostic Psalm <b>119</b> in the American Standard
Version of 1901, <i>New International Version </i>(NIV), in others, and in this
<i>The </i><i>Conversational Version</i>.)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>The
spelling of quite a few proper names has been altered in more recent renderings
of the Old Testament. A majority of these variants relate to the letter kaph (</span><span
lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>כ</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span>) in initial or medial position. Earlier versions usually
transliterated it <i>ch</i>; more lately it has been covered by <i>k</i> (or by
<i>c </i>if not followed by <i>e</i>, <i>i</i>, or <i>y</i>;<i> </i>that latter
circumstance would give <i>c</i> an <i>s</i> sound in English). The change is due
in part to shifts in Hebrew pronunciation itself. The following names are among
those affected by the shift from <i>ch</i> to <i>k </i>(before<i> e</i>, <i>i</i>,
or <i>y</i>) or <i>c </i>elsewhere): Abel-<span style='color:red'>k</span>eramim,
A<span style='color:red'>c</span>sah, A<span style='color:red'>c</span>shaph, A<span
style='color:red'>c</span>zib, Ar<span style='color:red'>k</span>i, Be<span
style='color:red'>c</span>oreth, Be<span style='color:red'>k</span>er, Bara<span
style='color:red'>k</span>ah, Bera<span style='color:red'>k</span>iah/Bere<span
style='color:red'>k</span>iah, Bi<span style='color:red'>c</span>ri, <span
style='color:red'>K</span>orashan/Borashan, <span style='color:red'>C</span>ozeba.
<span style='color:red'>C</span>un, <span style='color:red'>C</span>ushan, <span
style='color:red'>C</span>alcol, Chaldean/<span style='color:red'>Babylonian</span>,
Ha<span style='color:red'>c</span>moni, Ha<span style='color:red'>k</span>aliah,
Ha<span style='color:red'>k</span>ilah, Isma<span style='color:red'>k</span>iah,
Ja<span style='color:red'>c</span>an, Ja<span style='color:red'>k</span>in, Jebere<span
style='color:red'>k</span>iah, <span style='color:red'>K</span>aiwan/<span
style='color:red'>K</span>ium, <span style='color:red'>K</span>ebar, <span
style='color:red'>K</span>edorlaomer, <span style='color:red'>K</span>elal, <span
style='color:red'>K</span>elluki, <span style='color:red'>K</span>elubai/Caleb,
<span style='color:red'>K</span>enani, <span style='color:red'>K</span>enaniah,
<span style='color:red'>K</span>ephar, <span style='color:red'>K</span>ephirah,
<span style='color:red'>K</span>eran, <span style='color:red'>K</span>erethim, <span
style='color:red'>K</span>erith, <span style='color:red'>K</span>esed, <span
style='color:red'>K</span>esil, <span style='color:red'>K</span>esulloth, <span
style='color:red'>K</span>ezib, <span style='color:red'>K</span>idon, <span
style='color:red'>K</span>ileab, <span style='color:red'>K</span>ilion, <span
style='color:red'>K</span>ilmad, <span style='color:red'>K</span>ilmas, <span
style='color:red'>K</span>imham, <span style='color:red'>K</span>innereth, <span
style='color:red'>K</span>islev, <span style='color:red'>K</span>islon, <span
style='color:red'>K</span>isloth, <span style='color:red'>K</span>itham,<span
style='color:red'> K</span>ittim, <span style='color:red'>K</span>orashan/Borashan,
Maa<span style='color:red'>c</span>ah, Ma<span style='color:red'>c</span>aiah, Ma<span
style='color:red'>c</span>bena, Ma<span style='color:red'>c</span>bannai, Ma<span
style='color:red'>c</span>nadebai, M<span style='color:red'>ak</span>i, Ma<span
style='color:red'>k</span>ir, Mal<span style='color:red'>k</span>iel, Mal<span
style='color:red'>k</span>ijah, Mal<span style='color:red'>k</span>iram, Mal<span
style='color:red'>k</span>ishma, Mas<span style='color:red'>k</span>il, Mi<span
style='color:red'>c</span>mash, Mi<span style='color:red'>c</span>ri, Mi<span
style='color:red'>k</span>methath, Mi<span style='color:red'>k</span>tam, <span
style='color:red'>Molech</span>/Malcom/Milcom, Na<span style='color:red'>c</span>on,
Ne<span style='color:red'>c</span>o, O<span style='color:red'>c</span>ran, Po<span
style='color:red'>k</span>ereth, Ra<span style='color:red'>c</span>al, Re<span
style='color:red'>c</span>ab, Sarse<span style='color:red'>k</span>im, Se<span
style='color:red'>k</span>u, Sema<span style='color:red'>k</span>iah, She<span
style='color:red'>c</span>aniah, <span style='color:red'>S</span>ithri/Zithri, <span
style='color:red'>S</span>o<span style='color:red'>c</span>o/Shocho, Su<span
style='color:red'>c</span>athites,   </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <u>Hebrew
plurals</u> in <u>–im</u> (mas) and <u>–oth</u> (fem) are usually shifted to
English <i>s </i>plural forms in translations. <i>CV</i> has anglicized these
plurals: ashers (asherim), asherahs (asheroth), baals (baalim), cherubs
(cherubim), seraphs (seraphim), teraphs (teraphim); but not Anakim, Niphilim,
Rephaim, Rephidim, and other plural gentilics.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                        </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Superscript</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      to
distinguish Jeroboam</span><b><sup><span style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>I</span></sup></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
and Jeroboam</span><b><sup><span style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>II</span></sup></b><b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> </span></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(with
bold superscript Roman numerals)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      with
bold superscript for verse indicators</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>You</span><b><sup><span
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>sg</span></sup></b><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
and you</span><b><sup><span style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>pl</span></sup></b><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> clarify the ambiguous
English pronoun that renders the specific second-person Hebrew verbs or pronouns</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>to distinguish
masculine<sup>mas</sup> and feminine<sup>fem</sup> reference when otherwise
unclear in English</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>deference
language has largely been dropped and adjusted to English expression: 1 Samuel <b>25</b>:24-31</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>to
indicate the original names of measures given in the Hebrew text alongside
their approximate English equivalents put in regular type. Cubit measures for
length are given as a standardized 1½ft (&lt; 17.72ins). In Ezekiel the “long
cubit/royal cubit” @ 20.67ins is evidently meant. See <b>Measures</b> below.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>to
draw attention to discontinuity of person, tense, and number: Exodus<b> 15</b>:26,
<i>etc.</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>to
indicate using English imperatives (verbs<sup>f ) </sup>to replace Hebrew
imperfects for commands </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:58.5pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:-22.5pt'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>to
note a Septuagint reading: LXX as in 1 Samuel <b>13</b>:5; 2 Samuel <b>4</b>:6;
Psalm <b>40</b>:6; <b>68</b>:18; Ezekiel <b>5</b>:7; Obadiah 21; Zechariah <b>9</b>:15</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.75in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Syntax</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>    
<u>Collective noun</u> subjects have singular verbs as in American <i>vs.</i>
British usage.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:
2.5in;text-indent:.5in'><b><span style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  Measures</span></b></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:2.5in;text-indent:.5in'><b><span style='font-size:14.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></b></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Statements
about <u>value</u> are left in Semitic terminology because modern inflation
soon makes equations incorrect. Many monetary systems were originally figured
from a basic unit equal to a laborer’s daily pay. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>CV</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> translates
<u>weights</u> into rough modern equivalents and uses the ancient terms in
superscript alongside. Shekels, talents, and other terms were weights instead
of coins prior to the exile and perhaps as far back as 700 B.C., around the
time of the divided kingdom. <i>CV </i>has brought the terms into rough
American equivalents as ounces, pounds, and tons. Large amounts of gold,
silver, bronze, and iron are given in <i>tons</i> for quicker recognition; in
the following list, underlining accompanies small numbers given in <i>lbs</i>
or <i>oz</i>; the rest are given as tons (except where the total figure is a composite
of talents and shekels): Genesis <b><u>20</u></b><u>:16</u>; <b>23</b>:<u>14</u>,
<u>16</u>; <b><u>24</u></b><u>:22</u>; <b>30</b>:<u>14-15</u>; <u>23-24</u>; <b><u>33</u></b><u>:19</u>
+ Joshua <b>24</b>:32 (<i>kesitahs</i>); <b><u>37</u></b><u>:28</u>; <b><u>45</u></b><u>:22</u>;
Exodus <b><u>21</u></b><u>:32</u>; <b><u>25</u></b><u>:39</u>; <b><u>37</u></b><u>:24</u>;
<b>38</b>:24-26, <u>27-28</u>, 29; <b><u>43</u></b><u>:31</u>; Leviticus <b>27</b>:<u>3-7</u>,
<u>16</u>; Numbers <b><u>3</u></b><u>:47, 50</u>;<b> <u>7</u></b><u>:13-14</u>;
<b><u>8</u></b><u>:84-86</u>;<b> <u>31</u></b><u>:52</u>; Deuteronomy <b><u>22</u></b><u>:19,
29</u>; Joshua <b><u>7</u></b><u>:21</u>; Judges<b> <u>8</u></b><u>:26</u>; <b><u>9</u></b><u>:4</u>;
<b><u>16</u></b><u>:4;</u> <b>17</b>:<u>2, 4, 10</u>; 1 Samuel <b><u>9</u></b><u>:8</u>;
<b><u>17</u></b><u>:5, 7</u>; 2 Samuel <b><u>12</u></b><u>:30</u>; <b><u>18</u></b><u>:11,
12</u>; <b><u>21</u></b><u>:16</u>; <b><u>24</u></b><u>:24</u>; 1 Kings <b>9</b>:14,
28; <b>10</b>:10, 14, 16, 17, <u>29</u>; <b><u>16</u></b><u>:24</u>; <b><u>20</u></b><u>:39</u>;
2 Kings <b><u>5</u></b><u>:5, 22, 23</u>; <b><u>7</u></b><u>:1</u>, 16-18; <b>15</b>:19-20;
<b>18</b>:14; <b>23</b>:33a, <u>b</u>; 1 Chronicles <b>19</b>:6; <b><u>21</u></b><u>:15</u>;
<b>22</b>:14; <b>29</b>:4, 7; 2 Chronicles <b><u>2</u></b><u>:17</u>; <b>3</b>:8,
<u>9</u>; <b>8</b>:18; <b>9</b>:1, 9, 13, <u>15-16</u>; ; <b>20</b>:<u>2</u>; <b>25</b>:6,
9; <b>27</b>:5; <b>36</b>:3a, <u>b</u>; Ezra <b>2</b>:69; <b>7</b>:22; <b>8</b>:26-<u>27</u>;
Nehemiah <b><u>5</u></b><u>:15</u>; <b><u>10</u></b><u>:32</u>; Esther <b>3</b>:9;
Song of Solomon <b><u>8</u></b><u>:11, 12; <b>8</b>:12</u>; Isaiah <b><u>7</u></b><u>:23</u>;
Jeremiah<b> 32</b>:<u>9</u>; Ezekiel <b><u>4</u></b><u>:10</u>; Hosea <b><u>3</u></b><u>:2</u>;
Zechariah <b><u>11</u></b><u>:12</u>.    </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>That
leaves <u>volume</u> as something to equate roughly with modern systems.
Sources vary significantly; so <i>CV</i> has standardized size according to the
layout by G. F. Hill in Hasting’s <u>Dictionary of the Bible</u> (1989), pp.
967-71. That analysis puts the “homer/cor” as the guide dry measure for volume
and yields numbers significantly larger than what appear in several more recent
translations:</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>homer        11
bushels</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ephah        1.1
bushels            seah           .36 bushel (4½ pecks, ⅕ ephah)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>omer          .11
bushels            </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>The wet measures of
volume are geared to the “bath/ephah”:</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            bath           9
gallons               cab            4 pints</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            hin             1½
gallons            log             1 pint</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Measures of <u>length</u>
are termed loosely as follows:</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            cubit          1½
feet                  reed           9 feet (6 cubits)              palm       3
inches</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            Besides
variables already noted, the ancient systems had two or more variants within
even the same culture, and differed somewhat between Egyptian, Phoenician, and
Babylonian systems. Time may also be a complicating factor. The cubit, for
example, had a royal and common variant. In Ezekiel our numbers reflect the
“long cubit” (Ezekiel <b>43</b>:13; hence, the rather cumbersome exactness that
comes into the text there: .11 bushels!). So <i>CV</i> has entered its numbers
relative to the 20.67-inch royal cubit, which is about 17% longer than the
common cubit (17.72 inches). Solomon’s Temple may have used the royal cubit as
well, but <i>CV </i>assumes the common cubit and rounds off everywhere outside
of Ezekiel. The measurements for the volume used with the sacrificial system
may have differed as well from common amounts. The calculated amounts appear on
the line of the text with the original terms superscripted after them.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal align=center style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:center'><b><span
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Individual
Words</span></b></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
.5in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>A
number of these entries also come up in other parts of these Translation Notes.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal align=center style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:center'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>*
or <sup>+ </sup>indicates a complete listing</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal align=center style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:center'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            <i>CV
</i>has<i> </i>not made a concerted effort to translate the same Hebrew word
with the same English word, but the following are items of notable replacements
from older translations and from words less recognizable to a general audience.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>a</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>    (a)
used instead of <i>the</i> with a noun typical of its class: Numbers <b>24</b>:8,
horns of <u>a</u> wild ox; Job <b>27</b>:18, a watchman’s shed; <b>19</b>:29; Psalm
<b>94</b>:21; <b>129</b>:3; <b>145</b>:9; Hosea <b>11</b>:4; Zechariah <b>11</b>:3.
</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      (b)
replaced with a plural or anarthrous English noun in Job<b> 21</b>:12; Proverbs
<b>6</b>:6 (ants); Isaiah <b>44</b>:9; <b>45</b>:2 (craftsmen); <b>51</b>:8
(moth); Ezekiel <b>17</b>:22 (cedar).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            (c)
Hebrew could use its article for something known and definite to the writer,
though not to the reader: note Genesis <b>14</b>:13. For English purposes, the
definite article implies that the referent is known to both. The inference
seems to be that no English definite article belongs in Isaiah <b>7</b>:14
because the reader of Isaiah would not know about “the” virgin aside from the fulfillment
of the author’s comment itself.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>affliction</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 
often replaced with more contemporary expressions: abuse, pain, suffering,
troubled </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>albeit</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>   not
used</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(is) <i>able</i> (to)    replaced
by <i>can</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(<i>not</i> <i>able </i>(to)
  replaced by <i>cannot</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>alien</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
foreigner</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>all</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>        (a)
omitted as often as feasible. Hebrew seems to include it to reinforce a
generality, perhaps more like size than number. At any rate, English risks implying
<i>all</i> in an absolute sense. That tendency, in fact, applies to a majority
of absolute and universal terms:<i> every</i>, <i>everybody</i>, <i>all</i>, <i>nobody</i>,
<i>whole, forever</i>. The Hebrew for <i>all</i> (</span><span lang=HE dir=RTL
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>כל</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span>) is greatly overused in comparison to English <i>all</i>.
Instead, English indicates generality by simple statement. </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>English
assumes that saying a group of people did such and such means they all did it
with perhaps some exceptions. That “low percentage” of exceptions, if any, does
not detract from the characterizing statement about the group. It strikes us
that the high frequency of </span><span lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>כל</span><span dir=LTR></span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span> may well be part of the broader tendency toward grand
expression that shows itself also in pleonastic style. That is another way of
looking at characterizing statement <i>vs.</i> absolute uniformity. Besides,
other features of the situation or the very nature of the case make clear the
sufficient “all-ness” of the reference whether <i>all</i> is said (Hebrew) or
not (English). <i>All</i> in such usages amounts to “as a group,” which is
different from “all” as in every example.     </span></p>

<p class=MsoListParagraphCxSpFirst style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(b)<span
style='font:7.0pt "Times New Roman"'>&nbsp; </span></span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>replaced
with <i>every</i>: Genesis <b>8</b>:1; Psalm <b>72</b>:11b; <b>117</b>:1. Contemporary
English prefers distributive singular (<i>every</i>) over general plural (<i>all</i>).</span></p>

<p class=MsoListParagraphCxSpLast style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(c)<span
style='font:7.0pt "Times New Roman"'>&nbsp;&nbsp; </span></span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>In
some instances, we have kept <i>all</i> as with “all Israel” when it seems to
have more than just Judah in mind—what would later be the Northern and Southern
Kingdoms together: David was king of “all Israel” in contrast to sharing the
rule with King Saul.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>also</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>     usually
replaced with <i>too</i>, <i>as well, and</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>and      </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(a)
</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>omitted
between elements in a list even though Hebrew characteristically puts </span><span
lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ו</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span lang=HE style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span dir=LTR></span> </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(<i>and</i>)
between each member in the set since </span><span dir=RTL></span><span lang=HE
dir=RTL style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=RTL></span> ן</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>is an inseparable prefix</span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
rather than a free-standing term</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      (b) reduced <i>and</i>’s: See
Deuteronomy<b> 4</b>:28; <b>6</b>:7; <b>7</b>:5; <b>12</b>:6; <b>13</b>:4; <b>26</b>:17;
1 Samuel <b>22</b>:2; 2 Kings <b>20</b>:20.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            (c)
omitted even between the last two entries of a list to suggest that the list is
representative rather than exhaustive</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            (d)
omitted between sentences despite the presence of </span><span lang=HE dir=RTL
style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ו</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span lang=HE style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span dir=LTR></span> </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>between
most Hebrew sentences. <i>CV</i> gives two sentences instead.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            (e)
omitted when </span><span lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ו</span><span dir=LTR></span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span> seems to mean “even” and therefore has an appositional force
(Zechariah <b>9</b>:9).</span><span dir=RTL></span><span dir=RTL
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=RTL></span> </span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>That practice carries over into the
parallelism that characterizes Semitic poetry. We have often left it off the
front of the second line in synonymous parallelism. The second line of the
parallelism is not another referent but a second way of referring to the same
referent. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            (f)
Sometimes the coordinate conjunction</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> </span><span lang=HE
dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ו</span><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span> covers an adversative situation rather than a copulative one;
so <i>but</i> may appear in the <i>CV</i> wording: Obadiah 18; Jonah <b>1</b>:3.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>and
it came to pass</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> dropped. The construction represents the recurrent
Hebrew</span><span style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
</span><span lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ויהי ו</span><span dir=LTR></span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span>, </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>angel   </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(a)
<i>angel </i>as in supernatural messenger from God. As such, “an/the angel of
the L</span><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>”
are both grammatical possibilities because names/nouns in the Hebrew construct
cannot carry the prefixed article. The matter has some importance because it
impacts the issue of whether there is a specific “angel” distinct from the
rest. The question subsequently arises whether “the <i>angel of the L</i></span><i><span
style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>”
</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>equals<i>
the Son </i>as per later revelation or at least has actual deific reference or
representative/functional reference.           </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>anger/angry</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>    sometimes
changed to “mad,” with people. <i>Anger</i> and <i>wrath</i> come up so often
with God that leaving it out or substituting a less potent word seems
appropriate for toning down the negativity in English. It also seems
appropriate when considered alongside the frequent strong emphasis on Yahveh’s
love for his people and others.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>answered</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 
often omitted altogether after a question that the next sentence obviously
answers. As with dialog generally, the respondent’s words begin on a new line
without an introductory formula.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>arise</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>    usually
omitted with expressions like “arise and go” (Jonah<b> 1</b>:2). <i>Arise</i>
amounts to part of an ingressive formula, one that indicates initiating an
action</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ark </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      capitalized
for the Ark of the Covenant; </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>lower
case for Noah’s ark</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Ark
of the Covenant   CV</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> usually enters as a name in a set, short
form as in Joshua <b>3</b>:6; the Hebrew lengthens to </span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Ark
of the Covenant <s>of God</s></span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> in 2 Samuel <b>15</b>:24<sup>2</sup>
(replaced by </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>it</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> in the second entry). </span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Ark
of the Covenant <s>of the LORD</s></span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> appears in Numbers <b>10</b>:33;
Deuteronomy <b>31</b>:9, 25; 1 Samuel<b> 4</b>:3; 1 Kings <b>8</b>:1; 1
Chronicles <b>28</b>:2, 18 and </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Ark of the Covenant <s>of
the L</s></span></i><i><s><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></s></i><i><s><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> Your
God</span></s></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
