"Times New Roman",serif'> in Joshua <b>3</b>:3. </span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Ark
of the Covenant of the L</span></i><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
of Hosts Enthroned Above the Cherubs</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>, however, appears fully
in </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>CV
</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>in
1 Samuel <b>4</b>:4 since by its length it approaches the feel of an
intentional description more than a name.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>as  </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>allowed
as a preposition (1 Samuel <b>6</b>:8). The usage comes up in a 2023 TV
advertisement for a seniors’ dating service, <i>“They won’t be as lucky as me.”</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ashers</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  in
place of <i>asherim</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ashamed</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>    replaced
with <i>embarrassed</i> on occasion </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>asherahs</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>    in
place of the Hebrew plural form <i>asheroth</i> </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            uncapitalized
in most references because the contexts indicate they were constructed</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            capitalized
in six places where the term evidently refers to a Canaanite deity: Judges <b>3</b>:7;
1 Kings <b>15</b>:12; 2 Kings <b>2</b>:7; <b>18</b>:19; <b>23</b>:9; 2
Chronicles <b>15</b>:16</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ashtoreth<u>s</u></span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> an
anglicized plural of the Hebrew word in place of traditional </span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Ashtar<u>oth</u></span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
when referencing the plural of the goddess Ashtoreth; </span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Ashtaroth</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
when referencing the trans-Jordan city</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>atone</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  
sometimes replaced with </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>purify</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
or </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>cleanse</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> if
ceremonial (Leviticus <b>12</b>:7, 8, 14; <b>14</b>:31, 53; <b>15</b>:15). The
words for something “incorrect” (or viewed as such—ceremonial <i>vs.</i> real)
bleed across several categories as per modern usage; the same holds true for “undoing”
these situations: physical (clean/bathe/wash), medical (heal), ceremonial
(cleanse/purify), moral (forgive/atone). Maintaining consistency in modern
classification does not always come easy. A reader has to nuance the
differences according to attending circumstances.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>aught   </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>not
used</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>authority</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
often replaced by <i>right </i>for a more positive connotation</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></i></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>baals</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>   Anglicized
plural of <i>baal </i>in place of baal<u>im</u>.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>balm</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 
replaced with <i>salve</i>; <i>ointment</i> or <i>resin</i> are alternate
possibilities</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>bare</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>     in
place of smooth as in hairless? (Isaiah <b>18</b>:2, 7)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>bath     </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>equated
roughly to one gallon: 1 Kings <b>7</b>:26, 28; 2 Chronicles <b>2</b>:10; <b>4</b>:5;
Ezra <b>7</b>:22; Isaiah <b>5</b>:10; Ezekiel <b>45</b>:10, 11 14<sup>4</sup>*</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>bear</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>     carry
(Joshua<b> 4</b>:16)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>because</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>     (a)
omitted frequently, especially if it seems to have an illative force (explanatory)
instead of being a reason for what has gone before: <i>“You won’t gather any
grapes; <s>because</s> worms will eat them”</i> (Deuteronomy <b>28</b>:39).
Even when the second clause naturally serves as a reason for the former
comment, English speakers often leave it out, expecting the hearer to supply it
(Genesis <b>20</b>:3). That habit of speech makes for greater interest because
of greater listener participation. So <i>because</i> joins <i>all</i> and <i>and</i>
as omissions that help move the story along and focus attention. See also <i>fear</i>
below.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(b)<i>
</i></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>often
dropped between clauses when it is obvious that the second clause indicates the
reason of the first (I Samuel <b>9</b>:13, <i>“</i></span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>The
people won’t eat till he comes; <s>because</s> he has to bless the sacrifice.</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      (c) replaces <i>for </i>as a
conjunction; consequently, <i>because </i>serves both today as a subordinate
and coordinate conjunction. It has probably come to have that double role
because coordinate and subordinate value can be difficult to distinguish
anyway.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>before</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 
(a) time expression more frequently than (b) position, if clear: Genesis <b>10</b>:9;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(c) “in front of” worded as “in x’s
presence”: Exodus <b>18</b>:12; <b>22</b>:11; <b>43</b>:23; Numbers <b>14</b>:37;
<b>20</b>:3; <b>25</b>:4; Deuteronomy <b>1</b>:45; <b>4</b>:10; <b>10</b>:8;<b>
16</b>:11, 16; <b>27</b>:7; <b>29</b>:10; 1 Samuel <b>2</b>:21; 2 Samuel<b> 21</b>:6;
Psalm <b>23</b>:5;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(d) “in front of” as “ahead of”
(movement): Exodus <b>14</b>:19; <b>32</b>:1, 23, 24; Psalm <b>89</b>:14; </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(e) “in front of” as “facing”: Joshua
<b>18</b>:14;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(f) “in front of” as “near”: Numbers <b>33</b>:7,
47; Judges <b>16</b>:3;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(g) “in front of” as “east of” </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(2
Chronicles <b>20</b>:16)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>began</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 
usually omitted because Hebrew has such a propensity for indicating the
beginning of action. English custom usually omits making a point of that fact;
it lets the reader infer it. Note above the similarity with the use of <i>arise</i>
or even <i>go and </i>as ingressive indicators.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>beget/begat</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
replaced with <i>was father of</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>before</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  reserved
for time reference; place reference is normally indicated by <i>in front of</i>.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:22.5pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:-22.5pt'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Behold</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> omitted:
Daniel <b>2</b>:31;<b> 4</b>:10, <i>etc.</i>; Daniel <b>2</b>:3, 4, <i>etc.</i>;
Genesis<b> 1</b>:19; see Young’s Concordance for a handy listing of Hebrew
words in these groups.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:22.5pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:-22.5pt'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>beyond
the Jordan</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> (</span><span lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>עבר הירדן</span><span
dir=LTR></span><i><span lang=HE style='font-size:14.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span dir=LTR></span> </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>and
related expressions) a recurrent expression seemingly without uniform meaning
relative to the east or west side of the Jordan River, relative to the writer’s
presumed viewpoint, relative to the preceding event, or opposite the
anticipated destination of the writing. </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>CV </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>has
replaced </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>beyond </span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>with </span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>east
of</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
(Number <b>34</b>:15; Deuteronomy <b>1</b>:1;<b> 4</b>:46; Joshua<b> 1</b>:14; <b>2</b>:10;
<b>7</b>:7; Judges <b>10</b>:8; 1 Samuel <b>31</b>:7; 2 Chronicles <b>20</b>:16),
</span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>west
of </span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>the
Jordan (Deuteronomy <b>3</b>:25, opposite from a character’s position in the
narrative), or </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>by </span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>the Jordan (Judges <b>7</b>:25).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>beware</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 
expressed as <i>watch out for</i> </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:63.0pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:-63.0pt'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>blaspheme 
</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(a)
reserved for slanderous speech toward deity  </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(b)<i> insult, rail out at, slander</i>,
or equivalent expressions about people</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>blessed
 </span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Since
not often used today, CV sometimes replaces it with <i>commend </i>(Nehemiah <b>11</b>:2;
Proverbs <b>12</b>:8; <b>28</b>:4)), frequently with <i>fortunate </i>(Judges <b>51</b>:24,
<i>etc</i>.), omits it altogether as in Daniel <b>4</b>:34.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>boast</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>   brag</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>boat</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  
the term used for smaller craft; <i>ship </i>used for sea-going vessels on the
Mediterranean or the Gulf of Aqaba</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>bones</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>   In
some places where it is used metaphorically, it has been replaced with <i>deep
inside</i> (Job <b>20</b>:30; Psalm <b>6</b>:2; Proverbs <b>17</b>:22).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>book  
</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>replaced
with <i>scroll </i>because <i>book</i> conjures up a codex in modern times,
that is, a stack of leaves bound together: Exodus <b>17</b>:14; Deuteronomy<b>
31</b>:24; Joshua <b>18</b>:9; 1 Samuel <b>10</b>:25; 2 Kings <b>22</b>:10; 2
Chronicles <b>34</b>:18, 21, 24, 31, <i>etc.</i> <i>Book</i> appears in the
names of literary works.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>bread</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>   (a)
for “bread” in the usual English sense</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(b) translated “food” or some
equivalent expression in more general applications (Genesis <b>3</b>:19, <i>etc.</i>);
<i>pastries </i>(Genesis<b> 40</b>:17)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>breastplate</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  
(a) replaced with <i>chest</i> <i>pouch</i> as part of the priestly ephod </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(Exodus
<b>25</b>:7; <b>28</b>:4, 15, 22-30; <b>29</b>:5; <b>35</b>:9, 27; <b>39</b>:8,
9, 15-17, 19, 21<sup>2</sup>) since it was something that “contained” the Urim
and Thummim (Exodus <b>28</b>:30) and lay over the high priest’s heart when he
entered the Holy of Holies (Exodus <b>25</b>:7; <b>28</b>:4, 15, 22-30; <b>29</b>:5;
<b>35</b>:9, 27; <b>39</b>:8, 9, 15-17, 19, 21<sup>2</sup></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      (b)
kept as breastplate as an article of armor, a pectoral </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>brimstone</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  sulfur</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>bring/brought</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> Along
with </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>take/took</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>,
</span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>go/went</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>,
and </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>come/came</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>,
we have been careful to maintain the correct point of orientation as
established by previous context. </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Bring</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
and </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>come
</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>for
toward the viewpoint, and </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>take</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
and </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>go</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
for away from it (Nehemiah<b> 8</b>:6).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>brother 
</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>usually
limited to full siblings. In reference to other relatives, we have substituted <i>half-brother</i>
(Judges <b>9</b>:21; 2 Samuel <b>13</b>:4, 7, 20, 26; 1 Kings <b>1</b>:10;<b> 9</b>:2;
<b>27</b>:7, 15, 21, 22), <i>relative</i> (1 Kings <b>20</b>:32) or <i>kin</i>;
note also <i>cousin</i> in 2 Samuel <b>20</b>:9, where it replaces an
endearment use of <i>brother.</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>breastplate</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 
replaced with <i>pouch</i> as part of the priestly ephod </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>       </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>brothers-in-law     </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>We
were tempted to grate on purist niceties by spelling in-law words with the <i>s</i>
on the end rather than after the first element: daughter-in-laws instead of
daughters-in-law, the “correct” form. Like the word <i>in-laws</i>, the phrases
have been set so long that people in unguarded speech slip into using the
phrase as a word. But, alas, we succumbed to traditional expectancy in this
case despite the fact that most people do not say that: Ruth <b>1</b>:6, 7, 8.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>cab/kab</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  entered
as approximately 3 pints:<b> 2</b> Kings<b> 6</b>:25*</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>calamity  
</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>usually
replaced with <i>disaster</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>can
</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>     allowed
to cover permission and ability (Numbers<b> 35</b>:28).<i> </i></span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>In
English, <i>can</i> has pretty well taken over the duties of <i>may</i> when
meaning <i>allow</i>: <i>“Yes, you can go.”</i> <i>May</i> applies to contingent
action: <i>“That may happen if you don’t take your medicine.”</i> Everyday English
speakers no longer limit <i>can</i> to <i>be able</i>. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>cavalry</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  
used in most places instead of </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>horsemen</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
(Genesis <b>50</b>:9; Exodus <b>15</b>:19, </span><i><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>etc.</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>),
but not in 1 Kings <b>1</b>:5; <b>10</b>:26, </span><i><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>etc.</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Chaldea(ns)  
</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>replaced
with Babylon</span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>[ian(s)]</span></i><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> in 2 Kings <b>24</b>:2;
2 Chronicles <b>36</b>:17; Ezra <b>5</b>:12; Jeremiah <b>22</b>:25; <b>24</b>:4;
<b>25</b>:12; <b>32</b>:5, 24, 25, 28, 43:<b> 33</b>:5; <b>37</b>:5, 8, 10, 11,
13; <b>39</b>:5, 8; <b>41</b>:18; <b>43</b>:3; <b>50</b>:1, 8, 10, <s>15</s>, 25,
45; <b>51</b>:4, 24, 54; <b>52</b>:17; Ezekiel <b>12</b>:13; <b>23</b>:14, <s>15<sup>1</sup></s>,
15, 17; Daniel <b>1</b>:9; <b>3</b>:8</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>               
  left as </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>Chaldean</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> in the early times (Genesis<b>
11</b>:28, <s>31</s>; Job <b>1</b>:17) and when Chaldea and Babylonia are
mentioned together (Isaiah <b>13</b>:19; Ezekiel <b>23</b>:23)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                  replaced
with </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>astrologers</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
in Daniel when mentioned alongside magicians, conjurers, and fortunetellers
(Daniel <b>2</b>:2, 4, 10; <b>3</b>:8; <b>4</b>:7; <b>5</b>:7, 11).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>cherubs</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>     Anglicized
plural instead of the Hebrew plural cherub</span><i><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>im</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>child/children</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  used
for immediate offspring, not for descendants </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>inhabitants</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
of a city: Jerusalem</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(with) child</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>     pregnant</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Christ</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 
replaced by <i>Anointed One </i>in the Old Testament case (Psalm <b>2</b>:2
&lt; <i>Messiah</i>)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>city</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>reserved
for the largest urban places like Jerusalem, Babylon, Tyre, Sidon. Many
unwalled locations should probably be called <i>towns</i> or <i>villages</i>,
but our information is not sufficient now to make a proper determination. So,
we have probably overused <i>city</i> to label places. <i>Settlements</i>
commends itself for what by context sound like little satellites <i>CV</i> has
dubbed <i>settlements </i>in Nehemiah <b>11</b>:25-26, for instance, because
they were evidently small since the Israelites have only recently returned from
captivity. The term appears elsewhere like Joshua <b>17</b>:11, 16 and <i>seriatim</i>.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>city      </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(a)<i>
</i>used for Bethlehem, Damascus, Hebron, Jericho, Jerusalem, Samaria, Sidon,
Tyre. Because of uncertainty about the urban areas, the judgment call here is
uncertain because knowing the population for ancient places is difficult. A
number of locations are not labeled, so the issue is moot for them.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(b) Most other places called <i>cities
</i>we have called <i>towns</i>. We have kept <i>city</i> for places
conventionally so called: the cities of Egypt, cities of refuge, store cities,
chariot cities, the five cities of the Philistines.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>clean</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 
preceded by <i>ceremonially (un)clean</i> when not dealing with physical,
medical, or moral concerns. Cp. a similar issue with <i>religious </i>prostitution.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>cloud</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 
The expression “thick cloud” has been worded “dark cloud” in Exodus <b>19</b>:9,
16; <b>20</b>:21; 2 Samuel <b>22</b>:12; 1 Kings <b>8</b>:12; 2 Chronicles <b>6</b>:1;
Job <b>22</b>:14; <b>37</b>:11, 16; Psalm <b>18</b>:11; Isaiah<b> 44</b>:22.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>come/came</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            reserved
for “toward the viewpoint of speaker”; see </span><i><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>bring/brought</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
above likewise.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-indent:-.5in'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>coming and going</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 
 worded as “everyday life” (Joshua<b> 14</b>:11); “conduct” (1 Samuel<b> 29</b>:6);
“in all that you do” (Psalm <b>121</b>:8) </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>command/commandment</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>        especially
when authority is not prominent in the </span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>context, sometimes
weakened to <i>tell</i> (Numbers <b>34</b>:29), <i>bring </i>(2 Chronicles <b>7</b>:13),
<i>declare </i>(Esther <b>9</b>:32), <i>demand </i>(Daniel <b>2</b>:5), <i>forbid
</i>(Lamentations <b>1</b>:10),<i> gave </i>(Joshua <b>22</b>:9), <i>give the
go-ahead </i>(Amos <b>9</b>:9),<i> have </i>(Amos <b>9</b>:4), <i>permit</i>
(Lamentations <b>3</b>:37), <i>say</i> (2 Chronicles <b>25</b>:4), <i>will</i>
(Leviticus <b>24</b>:12). The alterations reduce the “heavy” feel of the hand of
God and human leaders in relation to people of lower rank.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>conceived</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  replaced
sometimes with </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>became pregnant</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>;
</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>sometimes
omitted in the series </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“had relations with his wife, <s>and she
conceived</s>, and had a son</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>” </span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(Isaiah
<b>8</b>:3)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>concerning</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>        
about</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>congregation</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  
replaced with </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>community</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
rather than an individual </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>assembly</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>cor/kor  
</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>entered
roughly as 11 bushels: Ezekiel <b>45</b>:14*</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>court  
</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>used
</span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>courtyard
</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>to
an open area outside, especially in the construction of the Tabernacle, the Temple,
and Ezekiel’s Temple (Ezekiel <b>40</b>ff.) </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      Court
</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>labels
(a) an inside area as in Xerxes’ palace (Esther <b>4</b>:11; <b>5</b>:1), </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                  </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(b)
the process of judgment (Job <b>24</b>:1), or</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>                  (c)
the king plus his closest circle of officers (Jeremiah <b>29</b>:2; Daniel <b>1</b>:4;
<b>2</b>:49).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>covenant</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 
used for the God-to-Israel system; </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>replaced
by </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>treaty
</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>for
international agreements (Ezekiel <b>17</b>:13, 15, 17); </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      </span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>pact</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
between individuals (2 Kings <b>11</b>:4), </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      marriage
</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>between
husband and wife (Malachi <b>2</b>:14), </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>commitment</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
for reaffirming the covenant under Josiah (2 Kings <b>22</b>:3) and Ezra (Ezra <b>10</b>:3;
Proverbs <b>18</b>:17).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Covenant
of salt</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>  In Numbers<b> 18</b>:19 and 2 Chronicles <b>13</b>:5,
CV has added the word </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>permanent</span></i></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>creature</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>    replaced
with some other expression like </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>created thing</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>.
</span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Creature</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
has come to mean, not something “created,” but something bizarre.  </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>cry
out</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>       (a)
reserved for intensive appeals or outcries from fear; </span></p>

<p class=MsoListParagraph style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(b)
often replaced with </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>shout</span></i></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(b) <i>Cry</i> is reserved for
mourning, except for cases where people express obvious fear.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>cubit</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  
 standardized as 1</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Agency FB",sans-serif'>½ </span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ft; the royal and common
cubit have not been differentiated.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Cush</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>    rendered
<i>Nubia</i> in place of <i>Ethiopia</i>, the term used in many standard
English versions: 2 Kings <b>19</b>:9; Esther <b>1</b>:1; <b>8</b>:9; Job <b>28</b>:19;
Psalm <b>7</b> (heading); <b>68</b>:31: <b>87</b>:4; Isaiah <b>11</b>:11; <b>18</b>:1;
<b>20</b>:3, 5; <b>37</b>:9; <b>43</b>:3; <b>45</b>:14; Jeremiah <b>46</b>:9;
Ezekiel <b>29</b>:10;<b> 30</b>:4, 5, 9; <b>38</b>:5; Amos <b>9</b>:7; Nahum <b>3</b>:9;
Zephaniah <b>3</b>:10. </span><b><span lang=HE dir=RTL style='font-size:14.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>כוש</span></b><span
dir=LTR></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'><span
dir=LTR></span> refers to the southern section of ancient Egypt (above the
second Nile cataract) and some of northern Sudan. Ethiopia today indicates a
land at the “horn of Africa.”</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Left
as </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Cush</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
in Genesis <b>2</b>:13.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      rendered
</span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Cush</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
for men’s names in Genesis <b>10</b>:6, 7, 8; 1 Chronicles<b> 1</b>:8, 9, 10.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            </span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Cushite</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
appears as </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>Nubian</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (rather than</span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
Ethiopian</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>) in Numbers <b>12</b>:1; 2 Samuel <b>18</b>:21, 23,
31, 32; 2 Chronicles <b>12</b>:3; <b>14</b>:9, 12, 13; <b>16</b>:8; <b>21</b>:16;
Jeremiah <b>13</b>:23; <b>38</b>:7; <b>39</b>:16; Daniel <b>11</b>:43;
Zephaniah <b>2</b>:12. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></i></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>daughters</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  </span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Inhabitants</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
is used whenever possible if </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>daughters</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
means the residents of a city/place.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>day</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      (a)
a 24-hour period</span></p>

<p class=MsoListParagraph style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;
margin-bottom:0in;margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(b)<span
style='font:7.0pt "Times New Roman"'>&nbsp; </span></span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>replaced
</span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“in
the day/s of”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> with </span><i><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“at the time of”</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
(1 Kings<b> 10</b>:21)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(c) <i>daylight</i> for when the sun
shines</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            (d)
<i>time</i> for an indefinite time  </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>deep</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
sometimes <i>open water</i></span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>; used as an adjective</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>desert</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  not
particularly an area of cactus and sand as an area where few people lived. Not
living somewhere, of course, could be because it was too arid.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>detestable</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  disgusting</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>divination</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  
fortunetelling</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>dove</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  
</span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>pigeon</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
often, except in Song of Solomon, where </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>dove </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>is
kept (because </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>pigeon</span></i><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> does not have a
very romantic connotation!)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>draw
near</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> replaced
with </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>come
close</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> or </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>come near </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>as
contemporary expressions</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>drink
offering  </span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>replaced with </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>liquid offering </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>because
no one “drank” it; </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Drink offering</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> could
be thought of as God or the gods “drinking” it. The expression occurs in fifty
places: Genesis <b>35</b>:14; Exodus <b>25</b>:29; <b>29</b>:40, 41; <b>30</b>:9;
<b>37</b>:16; Leviticus <b>23</b>:13, 18, 37; Numbers <b>4</b>:7; <b>6</b>:15; <b>15</b>:5,
7, 10, 24; <b>28</b>:7, 8, 9, 10, 14, 15, 25, 31; <b>29</b>:6, 11, 16, 18, 19,
39; Deuteronomy <b>32</b>:38; 2 Kings<b> 16</b>:13, 16; 1 Chronicles <b>29</b>:21,
35; Ezra <b>7</b>:17; Psalm <b>16</b>:4; Isaiah <b>57</b>:6; Jeremiah <b>7</b>:18;
<b>19</b>:13; <b>32</b>:29; <b>44</b>:17, 19, 25; <b>52</b>:19; Ezekiel <b>20</b>:28;
<b>45</b>:17; Hosea<b> 9</b>:4; Joel <b>1</b>:9, 13; <b>2</b>:14. Cp David’s
libation to the L</span><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> in 2 Samuel <b>23</b>:13-17
and Jacob’s anointing of the stone marker in Genesis<b> 35</b>:14-15.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>dwell 
</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>generally
avoided as a word not as commonly used as</span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> live, stay, reside,
inhabit</span></i></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>dwelling
place</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> not used</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></i></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>earth</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
when in contrast to sky or heaven; replaced with <i>ground</i> as when Moses
bowed down to the <i>ground </i>(Exodus <b>34</b>:8), cycled with <i>world </i>for
variety</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>earthenware</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 
<i>clay </i>(pot, amphora, jug, <i>etc.</i>)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>east      </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>translation
for “in front of” (2 Chronicles <b>20</b>:16)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>entreat  
</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>often
replaced with <i>ask</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ephah</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> entered
as approximately 1.1 bushels: Exodus <b>16</b>:36; <b>29</b>:40; Leviticus<b> 5</b>:11;
<b>6</b>:20; <b>19</b>:36; <b>23</b>:13; <b>24</b>:5; Numbers <b>5</b>:15; <b>15</b>:4,
6, 9; <b>28</b>:5; Judges<b> 6</b>:19; Ruth<b> 2</b>:17; 1 Samuel <b>1</b>:24; <b>17</b>:17;
Ezekiel <b>45</b>:10, 11, 13<sup>2</sup>, 24<sup>2</sup>; <b>46</b>:5<sup>2</sup>,
7<sup>2</sup>, 11<sup>2</sup>, 13</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Zechariah
<b>5</b>:6, 8, 9* carries the term </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ephah</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
because it apparently refers to a typical measuring unit <u>and</u> names a
container (of a size a woman could be put in it) rather than something equal to
⅒ of a homer. See “<b>Measures</b>” above.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>even</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>     Occasionally
a “misplaced <i>even</i>” appears in <i>CV</i>. In everyday conversation,
English speakers put <i>even</i> ahead of the verb instead of the adjective or
noun it modifies: Esau says, <i>“Don’t you even have one blessing for me,
father?”</i> instead of <i>“Don’t you have even one blessing for me, father?” </i>(Genesis
<b>27</b>:38); also. Proverbs <b>16</b>:7. (cp. “misplaced <i>only</i>” and
“misplaced <i>just</i>”<i> </i>below)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>every</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>   used
in place of <i>all</i> whenever possible as per current English preference</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.25in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Sometimes <i>CV</i>
replaces <i>every</i> with <i>people</i> because <i>everybody</i> sounds too
strong for what probably happened in cases like Exodus <b>35</b>:23, 24. See
similar comments under <i>all</i> above.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>everybody/everyone/everything</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>          a
distributive singular in place of plural <i>all</i> (Proverbs<b> 2</b>:19)</span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>.</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> <i>Every</i>
sees the full number (“all”) one at a time instead of as a set. Current idiom often
mixes the singularity (“everybody hear<u>s</u>”) and total plurality (<i>“Everybody
. . . will clap <u>their</u> hands”</i>; Nahum <b>3</b>:19b; <i>“against
everybody that’s exalted . . . so <u>they</u> can be abased”</i>; Isaiah <b>2</b>:12).
Contrast Jeremiah <b>31</b>:30. <i>CV</i> treats antecedent <i>every</i> as an
idiomatic plural: Psalm <b>5</b>:11, <i>“Let <u>everybody</u> be happy . . .
let <u>them</u> always sing for joy.” </i>Cp. Exodus <b>25</b>:2; Numbers <b>21</b>:9;
Proverbs <b>5</b>:21; Jeremiah <b>8</b>:6; <b>18</b>:16; Lamentations <b>2</b>:15;
Haggai <b>2</b>:13. <i>CV </i>has adopted this usage as part of its use of inclusive
language.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:22.5pt;text-align:justify;text-indent:-22.5pt'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>evil 
 </span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>reserved
for moral considerations and intense wrong quite often; replaced with <i>bad</i>
(Isaiah <b>3</b>:20), <i>difficult</i> (Ecclesiastes <b>12</b>:1 for the
deteriorating condition of elderly people), <i>disaster </i>(Jeremiah <b>2</b>:3)<i>,
misfortune</i> (Proverbs<b> 1</b>:33;<b> 3</b>:29), <i>perverted</i>
(Ecclesiastes <b>9</b>:3), <i>problem</i> (Ecclesiastes <b>5</b>:13), <i>sin</i>
(Jeremiah <b>2</b>:13), <i>selfish</i> (Isaiah <b>55</b>:7), <i>sinfulness</i>
(Ecclesiastes <b>7</b>:25), <i>thing</i> (Ecclesiastes <b>5</b>:16), <i>what
God forbids</i> (2 Chronicles <b>29</b>:6), <i>wrong</i> (Exodus <b>9</b>:27),</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>exalt</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 
often replaced by </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>elevate</span></i></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>exceedingly</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
replaced with <i>really</i> or omitted as a superlative</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>eyes     </span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>reserved
for organs of sight</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>            omitted
or replaced with circumlocution whenever possible in Semitic body-part
expressions like </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify'><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“in the eyes of the Lord,”</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
meaning </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>“in the L</span></i><i><span style='font-size:9.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span></i><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>’s
estimation”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>; </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“lifted up his eyes”</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
for </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“looked”</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>;
</span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“eyes
fell on”</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'> for </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;
line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“noticed</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>”;
“opened the eyes of” for </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“caused to see”</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
(Genesis <b>21</b>:19)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:-22.3pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>&nbsp;</span></i></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.25in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>face</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>     omitted
or changed to some other appropriate term as an example of body-part language
for </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“presence”</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>:
</span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>“before
<s>the face of</s> God”</span></i></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>fall
<u>on</u> the ground</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>   in place of <i>fall <u>to</u> the
ground</i></span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>family</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> 
replaced with <i>clan </i>(Zechariah <b>12</b>:12)</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>far
be it from _____</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> <i>to </i>(<i>do such and such</i>); <i>__
would never </i>(<i>do that</i>); <i>surely ___ would (not do such and such</i>):
Genesis <b>18</b>:25<sup>2</sup>; 1 Samuel <b>2</b>:30; <b>20</b>:9; <b>22</b>:15;
2 Samuel <b>20</b>:20; <b>23</b>:17; Job <b>34</b>:10.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>father</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  (a)
immediate parent</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.25in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(b)
replaced with </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ancestor</span></i><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>—or perhaps </span><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>forefather</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (Jeremiah
<b>11</b>:10)—if not referring to an immediate parent: Exodus <b>15</b>:2;
Judges <b>2</b>:19; 2 Chronicles <b>29</b>:6; <b>32</b>:13, 14, 15; Isaiah <b>39</b>:6,
and elsewhere.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-22.5pt'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (c)
kept for head of extended households, which were intergenerational sets of
extended families; clans</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-left:.75in;text-align:justify;text-indent:
-.25in'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(d)
replaced with </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>predecessor</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>
in Daniel <b>5</b>:3, 11, 13, 18 since Belshazzar was not the immediate son or
successor of Nebuchadnezzar. There were four kings with short reigns between
the two kings of Babylon known from scripture.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>See the entry for <i>sons</i>, a
correlative term with <i>father</i>; that is, the range of meanings for one
term corresponds to the range of meanings for the other. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(if I have found) <i><u>f</u>avor</i>
(with)   The idiom is somewhat like English usage as in “<i>do me a favor.”</i>
<i>CV</i> replaces the term with several expressions: <i>“blessing”</i> (Job <b>11</b>:19,
<i>etc.</i>), <i>“graciousness”</i> (Psalm <b>6</b>:4, <i>etc.</i>), <i>“grace”</i>
(Proverbs <b>12</b>:2, <i>etc.</i>), <i>“was gracious”</i> (Jeremiah <b>52</b>:31,
<i>etc.</i>), <i>“approval”</i> (Ecclesiastes <b>9</b>:11, <i>etc.</i>), <i>“is
favorable to”</i> (Proverbs <b>14</b>:35, <i>etc.</i>), <i>“is/am pleasing to”</i>
(often).</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:13.5pt;margin-bottom:
0in;margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:.5in'><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>The
wording affects Genesis <b>4</b>:6; <b>18</b>:3; <b>19</b>:14; <b>32</b>:5; <b>33</b>:5,
8, 10, 15; <b>34</b>:11; <b>39</b>:19; <b>47</b>:25, 29;<b> 50</b>:4; Exodus <b>3</b>:21;
<b>11</b>:3; <b>33</b>:12, 13, 16, 17; <b>34</b>:9; Leviticus <b>26</b>:9;
Numbers <b>11</b>:12, 15; <b>32</b>:5; Deuteronomy <b>24</b>:1; Judges<b> 6</b>:17;
Ruth <b>2</b>:10, 13; 1 Samuel <b>1</b>:18; [<b>2</b>:26]; <b>13</b>:12; 1
Samuel <b>20</b>:29; <b>25</b>:8; <b>27</b>:5; 2 Samuel <b>14</b>:22; <b>15</b>:25;
<b>16</b>:4; <b>20</b>:11; 1 Kings <b>11</b>:19; 2 Kings <b>13</b>:4;, Nehemiah<b>
2</b>:5, 17; Esther<b> 3</b>:0, 15, 17; <b>4</b>:2; <b>7</b>:3, 8; <b>8</b>:5; <b>10</b>:3;
Job <b>11</b>:19; <b>20</b>:10; Psalm <b>5</b>:12; <b>45</b>:12; <b>106</b>:4; <b>119</b>:58;
<b>147</b>:11; l Proverbs <b>3</b>:3; <b>8</b>:35;<b> 11</b>:27; <b>12</b>:2; <b>13</b>:13;
<b>14</b>:9, 35;<b> 16</b>:15; <b>18</b>:22; <b>19</b>:6, 12;<b> 22</b>:1; <b>28</b>:23;
<b>29</b>:26; Jeremiah <b>52</b>:31; Hosea <b>12</b>:4; Zechariah <b>8</b>:21,
22, Malachi <b>1</b>:9.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>fatherless
</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  not
replaced with </span><i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>orphans</span></i><span style='font-size:
12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> because the
children might still have their mothers, their fathers having been killed in
war</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>fear</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>      frequently
changed to <i>respect </i>in reference to God: Psalm <b>31</b>:19; 2 Chronicles<b>
6</b>:31</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>Like <i>hate</i> and <i>rule</i>, the
Hebrew word for <i>fear</i> can cover a wider range of degrees than the English
term <i>fear</i>. We have endeavored to use <i>respect</i> for people intending
to follow the L</span><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:
"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>, and <i>fear</i> for opponents of
the L</span><span style='font-size:9.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>ORD</span><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>.</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify;text-indent:-.5in'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>fear
and trembling</span></i><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;
font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'> (a) an expression that seems too extreme
to take strictly in many examples</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
margin-left:.5in;text-align:justify'><span style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:
115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>(b) Perhaps the phrase is a hendiadys
for “trembling fear,” “felt fear” as distinct from mental apprehension. See
also the comments on “Double Entry” above. </span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-bottom:0in;text-align:justify'><i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>feast</span></i><span
style='font-size:12.0pt;line-height:115%;font-family:"Times New Roman",serif'>  
 (a) left as <i>feast</i> that involves one meal</span></p>

<p class=MsoNormal style='margin-top:0in;margin-right:0in;margin-bottom:0in;
